Initial mapping covers the common languages a developer onboarding to a typical repo would encounter: typescript, javascript, python, ruby, go, rust, java, kotlin, swift, objective-c/cpp, c, cpp, csharp, fsharp, php, scala, clojure, elixir, erlang, elm, haskell, ocaml, dart, lua, r, perl, shell, powershell, sql, graphql, protobuf, vue, svelte, astro, html, css/scss/sass/less/stylus, markdown/mdx/restructuredtext/text, json/yaml/toml/ini/xml/csv.
+
+Unmapped extensions → `language: 'unknown'`. This is a deliberate fall-through, not an error condition; downstream agents can still read `chunkText` and reason about the file.
+~~~
+
+### `chunkAndEmbedFile` Placeholder
+
+The call site for the chunking + embedding pipeline is wired in Step 2 with its final signature so Step 3 only needs to fill in the body.
+
+~~~
+Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.
+
+Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number }>`.
+
+Step 2 behavior: log that it was called (including the fileId and the length of `rawContent`) and return `{ chunkCount: 0 }`. No DB writes.
+
+Step 3 (deferred) will:
+  1. Parse `rawContent` into an AST appropriate for the file's language.
+  2. Walk the AST to produce semantic chunks (function, class, method, interface, type, import, boilerplate, comment, other).
+  3. Generate an embedding for each chunk via the MindStudio SDK.
+  4. Persist chunks into `code_chunks` with `fileId` set.
+
+Note on the call site: `ingestRepository` currently passes an empty string for `rawContent` because Step 2 has no need to fetch raw file bytes. Step 3 will either (a) fetch raw content inside `ingestRepository` before calling `chunkAndEmbedFile`, or (b) push the fetch responsibility into `chunkAndEmbedFile` itself. That decision is part of Step 3.
+~~~
+
+### Re-ingestion Semantics
+
+Ingesting the same `githubUrl` twice is supported and idempotent at the row level:
+
+- `repositories` upserts on `githubUrl`, so the row's `id` is stable across runs. `lastScannedAt` is overwritten with the new timestamp.
+- `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)`, so file IDs are stable for paths that still exist in the repo. The `language` label is recomputed in case the extension mapping has been updated.
+
+What re-ingestion does **not** do in Step 2:
+
+- It does not delete `files` rows whose paths have been removed from the repo since the last scan. Stale rows linger. A "prune deleted files (and cascade-delete their chunks/docs)" pass is deferred to a later step — the cascading-delete chain documented earlier in this spec is what that pass will use.
+- It does not detect renames. A renamed file appears as a new `files` row with the new path; the old path remains as a stale row.
+
+~~~
+Step 3 prerequisite (flagged for early visibility, not implemented in Step 2):
+
+Once `chunkAndEmbedFile` does real work, the per-file cost dominates ingestion time. Hundreds of files × (raw-content fetch + AST parse + per-chunk embedding) will exceed any reasonable method timeout. Step 3 will need to:
+  - Add a `status` column to `repositories` (e.g. `idle | scanning | ready | failed`).
+  - Convert `ingestRepository` to a fire-and-forget shape: persist the repo + file rows synchronously, return immediately with `repositoryId` and `status: 'scanning'`, and run the chunking + embedding loop in a background promise that updates `status` on completion or failure.
+
+The shape is documented in the platform's "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" pattern.
+~~~
+
+## Out of Scope (Tracked for Later Steps)
+
+- Step 3: real chunking + embedding inside `chunkAndEmbedFile`, fire-and-forget ingestion shape, `repositories.status` column.
+- Mapper and Deep-Dive agent prompts, models, and orchestration.
+- Pruning of stale `files` rows on re-ingestion (and the cascading-delete pass that comes with it).
+- Web interface, navigation, flowchart rendering.
+- Auth, multi-user concerns, per-repository ownership.
+- Scenarios and seed data.

 Cascading delete: when a file is removed, all generated_docs with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.

-## Out of Scope for Step 1
-
-Deliberately not specified yet, to be addressed in subsequent steps under user direction:
-
-- Ingestion pipeline (cloning, AST parsing, chunking strategy, embedding generation)
-- Mapper and Deep-Dive agent prompts, models, and orchestration
-- Methods (no `dist/methods/src/*.ts` work in this step)
-- Web interface, navigation, flowchart rendering
-- Auth, multi-user concerns, rate limiting
-- Scenarios and seed data
+## Ingestion
+
+Ingestion is the process of pointing the app at a GitHub repository and turning it into rows in our database that downstream agents can read. Step 2 covers the structural ingestion: fetch the file list, filter the noise, and persist `repositories` + `files`. Chunking and embedding (the `code_chunks` table) is wired in via a placeholder and is implemented in Step 3.
+
+### `ingestRepository` Method
+
+The single public method for Step 2. The user provides a GitHub URL; the method returns a summary of what was ingested.
+
+~~~
+Manifest entry:
+  id: `ingest-repository`
+  path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
+  export: `ingestRepository`
+
+Input:
+  `{ githubUrl: string }`
+
+Output:
+  `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, fileCount, filteredCount, truncated }`
+
+The method runs unauthenticated for the MVP. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
+~~~
+
+The pipeline runs the following steps in order. Any step that fails surfaces a friendly error to the caller; the underlying details are written to `console.error` for debugging.
+
+1. **Parse the URL.** Accept `https://github.com/owner/name`, the same with `.git` suffix or trailing path (e.g. `/tree/main`), and `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalize to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so re-ingestion of variants of the same URL collapses onto one row.
+2. **Fetch repository metadata** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}` to learn the [default branch]{The branch name (e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`) the repo serves as its tip. We always ingest the default branch in Step 2; arbitrary-branch ingestion is a later step.}.
+3. **Fetch the recursive file tree** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}/git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1`. Surface the response's `truncated` flag back to the caller; we do not paginate the tree in this step.
+4. **Filter** the tree to text-only blobs worth indexing (see "Filtering Rules" below).
+5. **Upsert the `repositories` row** keyed on `githubUrl`. Sets `repoName` to `owner/name` and `lastScannedAt` to the current unix-ms timestamp.
+6. **Upsert each surviving file row** into `files`, keyed on `(repoId, filePath)`. Re-ingestion updates rows in place rather than creating duplicates, which keeps file IDs stable across runs (important once Step 3 starts attaching chunks to those IDs).
+7. **Invoke `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`** once per persisted file. In Step 2 this is a placeholder that just logs; Step 3 replaces its body with real chunking + embedding logic.
+
+~~~
+GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Uses native global `fetch` (Node 18+ runtime).
+
+Authentication is optional. If the secret `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set in `process.env`, it is sent as a Bearer token. Without it, requests are unauthenticated and subject to GitHub's 60-requests-per-hour-per-IP limit. For testing against small public repos this is fine; for any real use the token should be configured.
+
+GitHub-specific status codes get user-friendly error mapping:
+  - 404 → "Repository not found. Make sure the URL is correct and the repository is public (or that GITHUB_TOKEN is configured for private access)."
+  - 403 / 429 → "GitHub API rate limit reached. Configure a GITHUB_TOKEN secret to raise the limit, then try again."
+  - other non-2xx → "GitHub API request failed (status). Check the logs for details." with the raw response body in `console.error`.
+
+Required headers on every request: `Accept: application/vnd.github+json`, `X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28`, `User-Agent: codebase-bible-ingestor`.
+~~~
+
+### Filtering Rules
+
+Not every file in a repo is worth indexing. The filter has four layers, applied in order; the first that matches discards the file.
+
+~~~
+Filter implementation lives in `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts`. All sets are exported as `const` at the top of the file so the rule set is easy to audit and amend without rummaging through logic.
+
+1. Boilerplate directory segments. If any segment of the path matches one of these, the file is dropped. Initial set:
+   `node_modules`, `.git`, `.github`, `.svn`, `.hg`, `dist`, `build`, `out`,
+   `.next`, `.nuxt`, `.svelte-kit`, `.astro`, `.expo`, `.output`, `target`,
+   `vendor`, `__pycache__`, `.venv`, `venv`, `.idea`, `.vscode`, `.vs`,
+   `coverage`, `.nyc_output`, `.cache`, `.parcel-cache`, `.turbo`,
+   `.vercel`, `.pnpm-store`, `bower_components`, `jspm_packages`, `Pods`,
+   `DerivedData`.
+2. Skip-by-filename (exact match on the basename). Lockfiles and OS metadata:
+   `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`,
+   `Cargo.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `composer.lock`,
+   `Pipfile.lock`, `Podfile.lock`, `mix.lock`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`.
+3. Skip-by-pattern: minified bundles (`*.min.js|css|html|mjs|cjs`) and sourcemaps (`*.map`).
+4. Skip-by-extension. Binary or non-textual extensions: image (png, jpg, jpeg, gif, webp, ico, bmp, tif, tiff, svg), document (pdf, doc(x), ppt(x), xls(x)), archive (zip, tar, gz, bz2, xz, 7z, rar), audio/video (mp4, mp3, wav, ogg, mov, avi, webm, flac, aac, m4a), font (woff, woff2, ttf, otf, eot), compiled (exe, dll, so, dylib, a, o, obj, class, jar, war, ear, wasm, pyc, pyo, pyd), data (sqlite, sqlite3, db, dat, bin).
+5. Size cap: files larger than 1 MB are skipped (the GitHub tree response carries `size` in bytes per blob).
+
+Only tree nodes with `type === 'blob'` are considered — directories and submodule pointers are ignored before this filter even runs.
+~~~
+
+### Language Detection
+
+Each surviving file is tagged with a `language` label, derived purely from its filename. No content sniffing; this is a hackathon MVP and extension-based detection is sufficient.
+
+~~~
+Language detection lives alongside the filter in `fileFilters.ts` as `detectLanguage(path)`.
+
+Special-cased filenames (no extension): `Dockerfile` and `Dockerfile.*` → `dockerfile`; `Makefile`, `GNUmakefile` → `makefile`; `Rakefile`, `Gemfile`, `Podfile` → `ruby`.
+
+Otherwise mapped by extension. Initial mapping covers the common languages a developer onboarding to a typical repo would encounter: typescript, javascript, python, ruby, go, rust, java, kotlin, swift, objective-c/cpp, c, cpp, csharp, fsharp, php, scala, clojure, elixir, erlang, elm, haskell, ocaml, dart, lua, r, perl, shell, powershell, sql, graphql, protobuf, vue, svelte, astro, html, css/scss/sass/less/stylus, markdown/mdx/restructuredtext/text, json/yaml/toml/ini/xml/csv.
+
+Unmapped extensions → `language: 'unknown'`. This is a deliberate fall-through, not an error condition; downstream agents can still read `chunkText` and reason about the file.
+~~~
+
+### `chunkAndEmbedFile` Placeholder
+
+The call site for the chunking + embedding pipeline is wired in Step 2 with its final signature so Step 3 only needs to fill in the body.
+
+~~~
+Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.
+
+Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number }>`.
+
+Step 2 behavior: log that it was called (including the fileId and the length of `rawContent`) and return `{ chunkCount: 0 }`. No DB writes.
+
+Step 3 (deferred) will:
+  1. Parse `rawContent` into an AST appropriate for the file's language.
+  2. Walk the AST to produce semantic chunks (function, class, method, interface, type, import, boilerplate, comment, other).
+  3. Generate an embedding for each chunk via the MindStudio SDK.
+  4. Persist chunks into `code_chunks` with `fileId` set.
+
+Note on the call site: `ingestRepository` currently passes an empty string for `rawContent` because Step 2 has no need to fetch raw file bytes. Step 3 will either (a) fetch raw content inside `ingestRepository` before calling `chunkAndEmbedFile`, or (b) push the fetch responsibility into `chunkAndEmbedFile` itself. That decision is part of Step 3.
+~~~
+
+### Re-ingestion Semantics
+
+Ingesting the same `githubUrl` twice is supported and idempotent at the row level:
+
+- `repositories` upserts on `githubUrl`, so the row's `id` is stable across runs. `lastScannedAt` is overwritten with the new timestamp.
+- `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)`, so file IDs are stable for paths that still exist in the repo. The `language` label is recomputed in case the extension mapping has been updated.
+
+What re-ingestion does **not** do in Step 2:
+
+- It does not delete `files` rows whose paths have been removed from the repo since the last scan. Stale rows linger. A "prune deleted files (and cascade-delete their chunks/docs)" pass is deferred to a later step — the cascading-delete chain documented earlier in this spec is what that pass will use.
+- It does not detect renames. A renamed file appears as a new `files` row with the new path; the old path remains as a stale row.
+
+~~~
+Step 3 prerequisite (flagged for early visibility, not implemented in Step 2):
+
+Once `chunkAndEmbedFile` does real work, the per-file cost dominates ingestion time. Hundreds of files × (raw-content fetch + AST parse + per-chunk embedding) will exceed any reasonable method timeout. Step 3 will need to:
+  - Add a `status` column to `repositories` (e.g. `idle | scanning | ready | failed`).
+  - Convert `ingestRepository` to a fire-and-forget shape: persist the repo + file rows synchronously, return immediately with `repositoryId` and `status: 'scanning'`, and run the chunking + embedding loop in a background promise that updates `status` on completion or failure.
+
+The shape is documented in the platform's "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" pattern.
+~~~
+
+## Out of Scope (Tracked for Later Steps)
+
+- Step 3: real chunking + embedding inside `chunkAndEmbedFile`, fire-and-forget ingestion shape, `repositories.status` column.
+- Mapper and Deep-Dive agent prompts, models, and orchestration.
+- Pruning of stale `files` rows on re-ingestion (and the cascading-delete pass that comes with it).
+- Web interface, navigation, flowchart rendering.
+- Auth, multi-user concerns, per-repository ownership.
+- Scenarios and seed data.

content.text:
Step 2 is built, verified end-to-end, and documented in the spec.

**Files written:**

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts` | Public method. Parse → fetch repo info → fetch tree → filter → upsert repo → batched upsert files → call placeholder. |
| `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts` | URL parsing, GitHub REST calls, `GITHUB_TOKEN` support, friendly error mapping for 404 / 403 / 429. |
| `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts` | Four-layer filter (boilerplate dirs, lockfiles, minified/sourcemap patterns, binary extensions, 1 MB size cap) plus extension-based language detection. |
| `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts` | Placeholder. Final signature `(fileId, rawContent)`, just logs. Step 3 fills in the body. |

**Manifest:** `ingest-repository` registered.
**TS support:** added `@types/node` so `process.env` types resolve cleanly.

**Smoke test results** against `https://github.com/sindresorhus/is-plain-obj`:
- 12 files persisted, 3 filtered (the `.github/` directory).
- Languages detected correctly: typescript, javascript, json, markdown, with config files (`.editorconfig`, `.gitignore`, etc.) appropriately marked `unknown`.
- Re-ingestion with the `.git` suffix returned the **same** `repositoryId` and same file counts — upserts on `githubUrl` and `(repoId, filePath)` work as designed.
- 404 path returns the friendly "Repository not found" message; raw GitHub response goes to `console.error`.

**Three things baked into the spec for your future visibility, not Step 2 work:**
1. **Step 3 needs a `status` column on `repositories`** and a fire-and-forget shape. The current synchronous method only works because the placeholder is free; real per-file embedding will time out.
2. **Stale-file pruning is deferred.** Re-ingesting a repo where files have been deleted leaves orphan `files` rows behind. The cascade-delete chain from Step 1 is what'll clean these up when we add the pruning pass.
3. **`GITHUB_TOKEN` is optional.** Unauthenticated works for public repos at 60 req/hr. Set `GITHUB_TOKEN` as a secret when you want higher limits or private-repo support.

Standing by for Step 3.

messages.content:
STEP 3: ADVANCED CHUNKING & EMBEDDING LOGIC
We are now expanding the chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent) placeholder. This method is the core of our RAG engine and must be highly robust.

Please implement the following strict logic for this method:

1. Structural Chunking Strategy
Do not use a naive character-count splitter. Implement a smart chunking function that:

Attempts to split the rawContent by logical boundaries (e.g., class definitions, function blocks, or markdown headers depending on the file type).

Enforces a maximum chunk size of roughly 1,000 tokens (approx 4,000 characters) to ensure the embedding model retains high semantic density.

Implements a 10% overlap (approx 400 characters) between chunks so we do not lose context if a function spans across a split.

2. Vector Embedding Generation

For each generated chunk, call the platform's default Embedding API (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-3-small or equivalent) to generate the vector array.

Implement a try/catch block here. If the embedding API rate-limits or fails, log the error gracefully and continue to the next chunk rather than crashing the entire ingestion pipeline.

3. Database Persistence

Iterate through the successful chunks and generate records for the code_chunks table.

Map the data correctly: fileId = the passed fileId, chunkText = the raw string of the chunk, chunkType = dynamically assign based on content (e.g., 'code', 'documentation', 'mixed'), and embedding = the generated number[] array.

Use batch inserts if the SQLite ORM supports it to optimize write performance (O(1) database transaction vs O(N) individual writes).
STEP 4: AGENT ORCHESTRATION LOGIC
The database and ingestion pipeline are locked. We now need the AI agent logic to generate the documentation. Create a backend method called generateBible(repoId).

Implement this exact sequence:
1. The Mapper Phase:

Fetch all files associated with the repoId.

Pass the file paths to the platform's LLM API (e.g., Claude 3.5 Sonnet or GPT-4o) with a system prompt to generate a "High-Level Architecture Summary" and a Markdown flowchart (using Mermaid.js syntax) of how the repository is structured.

Save this overview into generated_docs under a simulated fileId called 'architecture-overview'.

2. The Deep-Dive Phase:

Iterate through the files for this repo.

For each file, fetch its associated code_chunks.

Pass the combined chunks to the LLM with the instruction: "Write a highly technical, interactive explanation of this file for a senior engineer. Explain the 'why', not just the syntax. Include the actual code snippets."

Save the LLM's output into the generated_docs table linked to the actual fileId.

Constraint: Do not build the UI yet. Ensure all database writes use the established SQLite batched helper methods. Confirm when the agent logic is compiled.

dimiss above prompt, here follow this: STEP 3: ASYNC ARCHITECTURE & EMBEDDING PIPELINE
Excellent catch on the timeout risk. We will pivot to an asynchronous, fire-and-forget architecture immediately.

1. Schema Update
Update the Repositories table schema to include a status column (string: e.g., 'pending', 'processing', 'completed', 'failed').

2. Async Refactor of ingestRepository
Modify ingestRepository so that it immediately sets the repo status to 'processing', returns a 202 Accepted response to the client, and then triggers the actual fetching, filtering, and chunking process in the background (or handles it gracefully without holding the HTTP request open).

3. Implement chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)
Replace the placeholder with robust logic:

Chunking: Split the rawContent by logical boundaries (double newlines, function blocks). Enforce a maximum chunk size of ~4,000 characters with a 10% overlap (approx 400 characters) so semantic context is not lost across splits.

Embedding: Call the platform's default Embedding API for each chunk.

Resilience: Wrap the embedding call in a try/catch block. If the API rate-limits, log the error, implement a brief delay if possible, and continue. Do not crash the entire run.

Persistence: Batch insert the successful chunks into the code_chunks table, storing the vector array in the embedding JSON column.

4. Completion State
Once all files are chunked and embedded, update the Repositories table status to 'completed'.

Write the TypeScript methods and update the spec. Do not build UI yet. Acknowledge when Step 3 is locked.

content.thinking:
The user sent conflicting instructions in one message — first a combined Step 3+4 prompt, then a dismissal with a revised Step 3-only prompt, so I'm following the second one. I need to focus on async architecture and the embedding pipeline without touching Step 4. Looking at the actual Step 3 requirements, the first piece is adding a `status` column to the repositories table that tracks the embedding state through pending, processing, completed, and failed states.

For the async refactor of ingestRepository, I'm thinking through the right split between synchronous and asynchronous work. The URL parsing, validation, and initial repo metadata fetch should happen synchronously so the caller gets immediate feedback on invalid URLs, then return early with the repository ID and processing status. The heavy lifting — fetching the tree, filtering files, chunking, and embedding — happens in the background, with the status eventually updated to completed or failed once that work finishes.

For the chunking strategy, I'm planning a pragmatic approach that respects semantic boundaries without needing a full AST parser. I'll split the raw content into logical blocks using heuristics like double newlines and function boundaries, then pack those blocks into chunks around 4000 characters with roughly 10% overlap for context continuity. Each chunk gets embedded via the platform's API with retry logic and rate-limit handling, and successful chunks batch-insert into the database. Once all files are processed, the repository status flips to completed.

For detecting chunk type, I'll keep it simple and map based on file language rather than analyzing content dynamically—markdown and documentation formats get labeled as 'documentation', everything else as 'code'.  The original spec vocabulary was more granular with AST-level types, but since I'm using heuristic chunking without a full parser, I'll note that the vocabulary is relaxed. For fetching raw content, I'll use the raw.githubusercontent.com endpoint with the GitHub token as a Bearer header if available, and wrap each file fetch in try/catch to handle failures gracefully.

For embeddings, I need to check the mindstudio SDK for available embedding actions and understand the API shape, batching strategy, and retry behavior. Now I'm sketching out the overall pipeline architecture: the main ingestRepository function parses the URL and returns immediately with a processing status, then fires off a background task that fetches the repo tree, filters files, upserts them in batches, and for each file fetches the raw content and chunks it with embeddings. If anything fails, the repo status updates to failed with an error message.

Defining the chunkAndEmbedFile function... I'm refining the chunking algorithm to handle both double newlines and function/class boundaries as split points. The greedy packing approach groups paragraphs until hitting the max size, then starts a fresh chunk with overlap from the previous one. For any chunk that still exceeds the limit, I'll fall back to character-window splitting. I'm debating whether to use a complex regex pattern that catches function and class declarations, or stick with double-newline splitting since those declarations are typically preceded by blank lines anyway—the simpler approach should work well for most code files.

Now I need to check what embedding capabilities are available in the SDK.

content.text:
Got it — dismissing the combined Step 3+4 prompt and proceeding with the revised Step 3 only (async architecture + embeddings). Step 4 stays untouched until you give the word.

Before writing the code, I need to confirm the SDK embedding action and a few orchestration questions.

content.name: askMindStudioSdk

input.query:
I'm building a RAG ingestion pipeline. For each text chunk (a piece of source code or markdown, ~4000 chars max), I need to generate an embedding vector and store it as `number[]` in a database column. Then later I'll do in-process cosine similarity over those vectors to do retrieval.

Specific questions:
1. What's the embedding action in @mindstudio-ai/agent? I don't see "generateEmbedding" or similar in the summary table I have. Does one exist? What's the import name, function signature, input shape, and output shape (especially: how is the vector returned — flat number[] at the top level, or nested under some field)?
2. What's the recommended/default embedding model for general-purpose text + code chunks? Is there a way to override the model, and what model IDs are valid? I want something in the OpenAI text-embedding-3-small class (cheap, fast, ~1536 dims).
3. Can I pass an array of texts in one call to embed many chunks at once, or is it strictly one-text-per-call? If batching is supported, what's the input/output shape for the batch form?
4. Does the action auto-retry on rate limits? If not, what error shape does it throw on 429 / quota errors so I can detect that case and back off vs other errors?

Also please give a concrete code snippet showing how to call it inside a method and access the resulting vector.

content.thinking:
I'm hitting a key decision point—the SDK doesn't support embedding generation natively, so I need to choose between two approaches: either add OpenAI as a dependency to handle embeddings ourselves and store vectors directly in the database, which aligns with our current schema but requires managing an API key, or pivot toward a managed RAG solution using the SDK's built-in data source capabilities.

The user explicitly chose the first path in Step 1 with full awareness of the tradeoffs, so switching now would undermine that commitment. But this also means introducing OpenAI as a dependency, which technically contradicts the "zero-dependency" framing—though it's the only way to keep the architecture we've already specced. I should surface both constraints clearly to the user and let them decide whether to proceed with OpenAI or reconsider the approach. I'm settling on presenting this as a genuine fork rather than making the call myself—the user clearly prefers to drive architectural decisions. I'll lay out the two paths cleanly: direct OpenAI API via a secret, or the platform's managed RAG system, then recommend the first option since it aligns with their existing Step 1 design and keeps the vectors visible for debugging. I'll ask them to pick and mention they'll need to set the API key before anything runs.

content.text:
Hold up — I hit a real fork before writing any code. The SDK consultant confirmed something I should have surfaced earlier: **`@mindstudio-ai/agent` does not have an embedding action.** No `generateEmbedding`, no `embed`, nothing in the catalog. There's no platform "default embedding API" to call.

This contradicts an implicit assumption in the spec since Step 1, and it's a decision big enough that I shouldn't make it unilaterally. Two real paths forward:

### Path A — Stay the course. Direct embedding provider via secret.

- Add `openai` to `dist/methods/package.json`, store `OPENAI_API_KEY` as a secret.
- Call `text-embedding-3-small` directly (1536 dims, cheap, ~$0.02/1M tokens, batch up to 100 at a time).
- Everything you specified in Steps 1-3 works as written: `code_chunks.embedding: number[]`, in-process cosine similarity at retrieval time, full control over the chunking strategy (the AST-style chunkType vocabulary survives).
- **Cost:** introduces a third-party dependency and a secret you need to provide, slightly contradicting the "zero-dependency" framing from your Step 1 message.

### Path B — Pivot to managed RAG.

- The platform has `createDataSource` (Milvus-backed vector store), `uploadDataSourceDocument` (handles chunking + embedding + indexing internally), `queryDataSource` (ANN search).
- No external API key, no `code_chunks` table writes, no in-process cosine similarity, scales past 10k chunks without degradation.
- **Cost:** the managed pipeline does its own generic chunking — we lose control over the AST-/structural-chunking strategy you specified for Step 3, and the `code_chunks` table from Step 1 becomes dead weight (we'd either drop it or repurpose it as a metadata-only "document index"). The "embedding as `number[]`" column from Step 1 also goes unused.

### My recommendation

**Path A.** Three reasons:

1. You explicitly chose this architecture in Step 1 with full awareness of the scaling tradeoff ("zero-dependency speed over massive scale"). Switching now invalidates that commitment and the schema you locked.
2. Step 3's whole point is "smart chunking + visible vectors." Path B's managed chunker is a black box — you lose the control you asked for in this step.
3. For Step 4 (the agents), having raw `chunkText` + `embedding` rows lets the Mapper and Deep-Dive agents do their own retrieval logic. Path B forces you through `queryDataSource` for everything, which is less flexible.

The downside (one extra secret) is small. You'd want it anyway for any non-trivial RAG project.

### What I need from you to proceed

- **Pick a path:** [Path A — OpenAI embeddings](suggest:Go with Path A. Use OpenAI text-embedding-3-small. I'll provide the OPENAI_API_KEY secret.) | [Path B — managed RAG](suggest:Pivot to Path B. Use the managed RAG stack (createDataSource / uploadDataSourceDocument / queryDataSource) and revise the spec to drop the code_chunks table.)
- **If Path A:** an `OPENAI_API_KEY`. Paste it in chat and I'll set it via the CLI (chat is scrubbed), or set it yourself in the dashboard before I run any test calls.

Standing by. No code written, no spec changes, no schema changes until you decide.

messages.content:
DECISION: PATH A (OpenRouter Integration)
I have successfully added OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL to the Secrets tab.

EXECUTE STEP 3: ASYNC ARCHITECTURE & OPENROUTER EMBEDDING
Please proceed with the implementation. I need you to be very detailed with the TypeScript logic:

Dependencies: Add openai to package.json.

Async Refactor: Update ingestRepository to be asynchronous (Fire-and-Forget). It should update the repository status to 'processing', return a 202 Accepted to the UI immediately, and then continue the work in the background.

Smart Chunking: In chunkAndEmbedFile, implement logic to split code by logical blocks (functions, classes, or double-newlines). Enforce a ~4,000 character limit with a 400-character overlap between chunks to maintain context.

OpenRouter Embedding:

Initialize the OpenAI client using the OPENAI_BASE_URL from the environment secrets.

Call the text-embedding-3-small model via OpenRouter for each chunk.

Use try/catch to handle potential API rate limits or errors gracefully.

Database Persistence: Batch-insert the generated chunks and their vector number[] arrays into the code_chunks table.

Status Update: Once all files are processed, set the repository status to 'completed'.

Please update the app.md spec and write the full TypeScript source code for these methods. Wait for my instruction for Step 4.

content.thinking:
OpenRouter recently added embedding support with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so I can use `openai/text-embedding-3-small` as the model name when posting to their embeddings API.

For the schema, I need to add a `status` column to the repositories table with values like 'pending', 'processing', 'completed', and 'failed'. I'll stick to exactly what the user requested and avoid adding extra columns like error messages unless they ask for it.

For the async pattern, I'm structuring the ingestion as a two-phase operation: first validate and fetch repo metadata synchronously so the caller gets immediate feedback on bad URLs, then upsert the repo with `status: 'processing'` and fire off the slow embedding work in the background without waiting for it to complete.

Implementing the async handler... Writing the chunking algorithm...

Now I'm thinking through the chunk type detection—since we're not doing full AST analysis, I'll use a simple heuristic that marks markdown and text files as documentation and everything else as code. For the embedding calls, I'm setting up the OpenAI client to use OpenRouter as the base URL, then making requests to the text-embedding-3-small model with retry logic for rate limits. I need to handle the user's core requirement: wrap embedding calls in try/catch, log errors on rate limits, add a brief delay, and keep the run going without crashing. I'm thinking through a hybrid approach where I batch up to 100 chunks per embedding call, retry once on 429 errors with a 2-second delay, and skip any chunks that fail rather than halting everything. For database persistence, I'll batch inserts in groups of 100 to keep mutations efficient. The trickier part is re-ingestion—if a file gets processed again, I need to avoid duplicating chunks that were already stored from a previous run.

I'll delete existing chunks for that fileId before inserting the new ones, which is the cleanest approach. I'm also noting that stale chunk cleanup (when files disappear from the repo) isn't being handled yet, but that's consistent with deferring it like Step 2 did. For fetching raw file content, I'll use raw.githubusercontent.com with the GitHub token for private repos—much simpler than the API endpoint. On concurrency, I could parallelize the per-file fetch and embed loop with a limit like 5 at a time, but for the MVP I'll keep it serial since the embedding batching already reduces API calls significantly. edding calls — for a 50-file repo that's about 100 seconds total, and even 500 files would take around 17 minutes, which is acceptable for a background task. I'll stick with serial processing to keep things simple, with each file batching its chunks into a single embedding call.

Let me map out the files I need to create or modify: adding a `status` column to the repositories table, setting up the OpenAI/OpenRouter client, implementing the full chunk-and-embed logic, writing the text chunking function, adding a raw content fetch to the GitHub client, and refactoring the main ingest method to be async.

I should also add the `openai` package to dependencies, update the spec and manifest description, then validate everything with diagnostics and a smoke test. One thing I want to be careful about — if embedding fails, I should at least log any dimension mismatches rather than silently accepting bad vectors. The cost is negligible too: a 100-file repo with 3 chunks each is roughly 300k tokens, which costs about $0.006 with text-embedding-3-small.

Let me start writing the implementation in order: update package.json first, then the table schema, GitHub client, text chunker, OpenAI client, the main chunk-and-embed function, and finally refactor the ingest method.

Now I'm thinking through the `chunkAndEmbedFile` signature — the user kept it as `(fileId, rawContent)` but I need to know the chunk type for proper categorization. I could add a language parameter, look it up from the database, or infer it from the content itself. Since the signature is locked, I'll detect the chunk type by analyzing the content: if it contains lots of code keywords like `function`, `class`, `def`, `import`, it's code; if it has markdown patterns, it's documentation; otherwise default to code.

Detecting chunk type from content...

Now I'm realizing that per-chunk detection makes more sense than per-file, since files often mix code and prose. I should also handle edge cases like very small content that doesn't warrant multiple chunks. One remaining question: should `ingestRepository` fetch the raw content and pass it to `chunkAndEmbedFile`, or should `chunkAndEmbedFile` fetch it itself?

Option A keeps the chunking function pure and lets the background task handle orchestration and GitHub interactions. That's cleaner. So I'll add a `fetchRawContent` helper to the GitHub client, then structure the ingestion pipeline to fetch content per file before chunking and embedding. The background task will upsert all file rows first, then iterate through them to fetch raw content and process each one, with error handling to continue on failures.

Writing the implementation now...

+/** Lifecycle of a repository's ingestion run. */
+export type RepositoryStatus =
+  | 'pending' // row exists, ingestion not yet started (reserved for future queueing)
+  | 'processing' // ingestion is currently running in the background
+  | 'completed' // last ingestion finished successfully

+
+interface Repository {
+  // Full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. "https://github.com/owner/name".

+
+  // Display name, typically derived from the URL ("owner/name").
+  // Stored separately so the UI does not re-parse on every render.
+  repoName: string;
+
+  // Lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. The column is typed as
+  // `string` at the DB level (SQLite has no enums); the values are
+  // restricted via the `RepositoryStatus` type alias above and enforced
+  // by the call sites that write this column.
+  status: RepositoryStatus;
+
+  // Unix-ms timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run.
+  // `undefined` until the first scan reaches `status === 'completed'`.
+  // Deliberately not bumped on transitions to 'processing' or 'failed' —
+  // the column should answer "when did we last have a complete picture
+  // of this repo?" rather than "when did we last touch the row?". Use the
+  // auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.
   lastScannedAt?: number;
 }

The solution is upgrading to OpenAI SDK v5+, which switched to native fetch and avoids this compatibility problem entirely.

SELECT
  f.filePath,
  f.language,
  c.chunkType,
  LENGTH(c.chunkText) AS textLen,
  json_array_length(c.embedding) AS embDim,
  SUBSTR(c.chunkText, 1, 60) AS preview
FROM code_chunks c
JOIN files f ON f.id = c.fileId
ORDER BY f.filePath
LIMIT 12;

content.text:
Pipeline is working end-to-end:

- **12 files → 12 chunks**, 1:1 because every file in this repo is small enough to fit a single chunk.
- **All embeddings = 1536 dimensions** (matches `text-embedding-3-small` exactly — OpenRouter routed correctly).
- **chunkType detection**: 10 'code' / 2 'documentation'. Spot-checking: `readme.md` → 'documentation' ✓, `index.js` → 'code' ✓, `package.json` → 'code' ✓. The only oddity is `.gitattributes` classified as 'documentation' because the `>` in `* text=auto` is read as a blockquote marker. Acceptable noise on a 19-char file.
- **Re-ingestion idempotency**: 12 files, 12 distinct fileIds, no chunk duplication despite this being a re-ingest of the repo from Step 2.

Now let me also confirm the chunker handles a larger file with multiple chunks correctly. I'll seed in a file with content that exceeds 4000 chars to verify overlap and packing logic.

input.sql:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_chunks,
  COUNT(DISTINCT c.fileId) AS files_with_chunks,
  AVG(LENGTH(c.chunkText)) AS avg_chunk_len,
  MAX(LENGTH(c.chunkText)) AS max_chunk_len,
  MIN(json_array_length(c.embedding)) AS min_dim,
  MAX(json_array_length(c.embedding)) AS max_dim
FROM code_chunks c
JOIN files f ON f.id = c.fileId
WHERE f.repoId = 'aa7f1c9f-9756-47e2-9064-f6722f8576c6';

content.result:
1 ---
   2 name: Codebase Bible
   3 description: A RAG-based engine that ingests GitHub repositories, analyzes them with AI agents, and produces an interactive onboarding guide for new developers.
   4 ---
   5 
   6 # Codebase Bible
   7 
   8 An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app clones the repo, parses each source file into an Abstract Syntax Tree, breaks the code into semantically meaningful chunks (functions, classes, boilerplate), embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context from the rest of the repo to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible: navigable markdown documentation with embedded flowcharts, designed to onboard new developers quickly.
   9 
  10 ~~~
  11 Build proceeds strictly step-by-step under user direction. This file currently covers Step 1 (data model) only. Methods, agent orchestration, ingestion pipeline, and interface specs are deliberately out of scope until subsequent steps are unlocked by the user.
  12 
  13 Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
  14 - The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
  15 - Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
  16 - All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
  17 ~~~
  18 
  19 ## Data Model
  20 
  21 The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.
  22 
  23 ~~~
  24 Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):
  25 
  26   delete repository
  27     → delete all files where file.repo_id = repository.id
  28       → delete all code_chunks where chunk.file_id IN (those file ids)
  29       → delete all generated_docs where doc.file_id IN (those file ids)
  30 
  31 A shared helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) should own this so any method that removes a repo, or re-ingests one, calls a single function. Same shape applies for cascading delete of a single file.
  32 
  33 All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
  34 ~~~
  35 
  36 ### Repositories
  37 Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system.
  38 
  39 ~~~
  40 Table name: `repositories`. Columns:

(showing lines 1–40 of 233 — use offset and maxLines to read more)

content.name: editSpec

edits.content:
An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app fetches the repo's file tree, breaks each source file into semantically meaningful chunks (function/class/declaration boundaries plus paragraph boundaries), embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context from the rest of the repo to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible: navigable markdown documentation with embedded flowcharts, designed to onboard new developers quickly.

~~~
Build proceeds strictly step-by-step under user direction. This file currently covers Steps 1–3:
  - Step 1: data model.
  - Step 2: GitHub ingestion (URL parsing, tree fetch, filtering, file persistence).
  - Step 3: async architecture, smart chunking, OpenAI-compatible embeddings, persistence.
Methods for the agent layer (Mapper, Deep-Dive) and the web UI are deliberately out of scope until subsequent steps are unlocked by the user.

Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
- The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
- Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
- The MindStudio SDK does NOT have an embedding action. Embeddings are generated by calling an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package, configured to point at OpenRouter (or bare OpenAI, or any other compatible provider) via the `OPENAI_BASE_URL` secret.
- All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
~~~

edits.heading: Data Model > Repositories
edits.content:
Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Includes a lifecycle status because ingestion is asynchronous.

~~~
Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
- `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
- `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`). Stored separately so the UI doesn't re-parse on every render.
- `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type alias) — lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. Values:
    - `pending` — row exists, ingestion not yet started. Reserved for a future queueing layer; not actively written today.
    - `processing` — ingestion is currently running in the background.
    - `completed` — last run finished successfully.
    - `failed` — last run crashed; details in method logs (`console.error`).
- `lastScannedAt` (number, unix ms, optional) — timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run. `undefined` until the first run reaches `status === 'completed'`. Deliberately NOT bumped on transitions to `processing` or `failed` — the column answers "when did we last have a complete picture of this repo?", not "when did we last touch the row?". Use the auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.

Unique constraint: `[['githubUrl']]`. Required so re-ingestion can `upsert` on URL instead of duplicating rows.
~~~

edits.heading: Ingestion > `ingestRepository` Method
edits.content:
The single public method for ingestion. The caller provides a GitHub URL; the method validates synchronously and kicks off the heavy work in a background task. Returns immediately with `status: 'processing'`.

~~~
Manifest entry:
  id: `ingest-repository`
  path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
  export: `ingestRepository`

Input:
  `{ githubUrl: string }`

Output (returned synchronously):
  `{ repositoryId: string, repoName: string, defaultBranch: string, status: 'processing' }`

The method runs unauthenticated for the MVP. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
~~~

The method runs in two phases:

**Phase A — synchronous validation and row creation.** Returned to the caller as soon as it completes. Bad URLs and missing repositories produce errors here, before any background work is started.

1. **Parse the URL.** Accept `https://github.com/owner/name`, the same with `.git` suffix or trailing path (e.g. `/tree/main`), and `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalize to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so re-ingestion of variants of the same URL collapses onto one row.
2. **Fetch repository metadata** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}` to confirm the repo exists and learn the [default branch]{The branch name (e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`) the repo serves as its tip. We always ingest the default branch in Step 3; arbitrary-branch ingestion is a later step.}.
3. **Upsert the `repositories` row** keyed on `githubUrl`. Sets `repoName` to `owner/name` and `status` to `'processing'`. `lastScannedAt` is intentionally not touched yet — it will be set only when (and if) the background phase reaches `'completed'`.
4. **Return** `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, status: 'processing' }` to the caller.

**Phase B — fire-and-forget background ingestion.** Begins after Phase A returns. Uses the platform's [un-awaited promise pattern]{The execution environment persists between requests, so a promise that is not awaited continues running after the method returns. See "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" in the platform docs.}.

5. **Fetch the recursive file tree** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}/git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1`.
6. **Filter** the tree to text-only blobs worth indexing (see "Filtering Rules" below).
7. **Upsert each surviving file row** into `files`, keyed on `(repoId, filePath)`. Re-ingestion updates rows in place rather than creating duplicates, which keeps file IDs stable across runs (important so chunks from previous runs can be cleanly replaced in step 8).
8. **Per file**, fetch raw content from `raw.githubusercontent.com/{owner}/{repo}/{branch}/{path}` (with `GITHUB_TOKEN` Bearer if configured) and pass it to `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`. Per-file failures (network blips, transient 5xx, unicode trouble) are logged and skipped — a single bad file does not abort the whole run.
9. **Mark the repository `'completed'`** and write a fresh `lastScannedAt`. On any unhandled error in steps 5–9, the row is marked `'failed'` instead and the error detail is in `console.error`.

~~~
GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Uses native global `fetch` (Node 18+ runtime).

Authentication is optional. If the secret `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set in `process.env`, it is sent as a Bearer token on both the REST API requests and the `raw.githubusercontent.com` requests. Without it, requests are unauthenticated and subject to GitHub's 60-requests-per-hour-per-IP limit.

GitHub-specific status codes get user-friendly error mapping (only relevant for Phase A; Phase B logs and continues):
  - 404 → "Repository not found. Make sure the URL is correct and the repository is public (or that GITHUB_TOKEN is configured for private access)."
  - 403 / 429 → "GitHub API rate limit reached. Configure a GITHUB_TOKEN secret to raise the limit, then try again."
  - other non-2xx → "GitHub API request failed (status). Check the logs for details." with the raw response body in `console.error`.

Required headers on every request: `Accept: application/vnd.github+json`, `X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28`, `User-Agent: codebase-bible-ingestor`.
~~~

### Background Task Concurrency

Files are processed serially inside the background task. Per file: fetch raw content, chunk, embed (one batched API call), persist. Files do not run in parallel today.

~~~
Serial processing is a conscious choice for the MVP:
  - Embedding cost is dominated by the per-call round trip, not the per-chunk cost. Batching all of one file's chunks into a single embedding call (which we already do) captures most of the available speedup.
  - Adding parallelism across files introduces concurrency-limit bookkeeping and complicates rate-limit handling.

If throughput becomes a problem on large repos, the natural upgrade is a small concurrency pool (e.g. 4-wide) around the per-file loop in `runIngestion`.
~~~

edits.heading: Ingestion > `chunkAndEmbedFile` Placeholder
edits.content:
Per-file chunk + embed + persist routine. Public signature unchanged from Step 2 (`chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`).

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.

Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number, failedChunkCount: number }>`.

Behavior:
  1. Drop existing `code_chunks` rows for `fileId` so re-ingestion replaces in place. (This is intra-file idempotency. The cascade-delete chain documented earlier handles the case where the entire `files` row is removed.) Implemented as a single `removeAll` with a bindings predicate, which compiles to one DELETE WHERE.
  2. Run the smart chunker (`chunkText`) over `rawContent`. Empty / whitespace-only files produce zero chunks and exit with `{ chunkCount: 0, failedChunkCount: 0 }`.
  3. Embed all chunks in a single batched call to `embedTexts`. Slots that fail to embed (after one rate-limit retry) come back as `null` and are skipped, not retried per-chunk.
  4. Build rows for `code_chunks`: `fileId`, `chunkText`, `chunkType` (classified per-chunk by `classifyChunkType`), `embedding`. Embeddings whose dimensionality differs from `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (1536 for text-embedding-3-small) are still persisted, but a `console.warn` is emitted so model swaps are visible.
  5. Persist via `db.batch` in groups of 100 mutations.

Returns `{ chunkCount, failedChunkCount }` for diagnostic purposes; the ingestion driver aggregates these across files for its summary log line.
~~~

### Smart Chunker (`textChunker.ts`)

A pragmatic chunker that splits source files and prose along logical boundaries, packs them into chunks of ≌4,000 characters, and preserves a 400-character overlap between adjacent chunks so context is not lost when a function or paragraph straddles a split.

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/textChunker.ts`.

Exports:
  - `chunkText(text: string, opts?: { maxChars?: number, overlap?: number }): string[]`
  - `classifyChunkType(content: string): 'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`

Defaults: `maxChars = 4_000`, `overlap = 400` (10% of maxChars per the spec).

Algorithm (in `chunkText`):
  1. Empty / whitespace-only input → `[]`.
  2. Input shorter than `maxChars` → `[text]` (single chunk, no work).
  3. Otherwise split into "blocks" along logical boundaries. The split regex matches:
     - Runs of blank lines (`\n\s*\n+`).
     - Lines starting (after optional indentation) with a declaration keyword common in code: `export`, `default`, `async`, `function`, `class`, `interface`, `type`, `enum`, `namespace`, `module`, `struct`, `impl`, `trait`, `fn`, `def`, `public`, `private`, `protected`, `static`.
     - Markdown headings (`#` through `######`).
     This is heuristic, NOT an AST. It is good enough that well-formatted code keeps function/class units together; it is not perfect on minified or oddly-formatted input.
  4. Greedy-pack blocks into chunks: append blocks to the current chunk until adding the next block would exceed `maxChars`. At that point, flush the current chunk, then seed a new chunk with the LAST `overlap` characters of the just-flushed chunk, followed by the new block. The seeded overlap is what preserves cross-chunk context.
  5. If a single block is itself larger than `maxChars` (e.g. a giant minified line, or a function with no internal blank lines), it goes through a sliding-window splitter with stride `maxChars - overlap`. This guarantees no chunk exceeds the cap, at the cost of arbitrary boundaries inside that block.
  6. Belt-and-suspenders: any chunk that somehow ends up over the cap gets a final pass through the sliding-window splitter.

Note on the relaxed `chunkType` vocabulary: the Step 1 spec mentioned a more granular vocabulary (`function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, etc.) that would only be reachable with a true AST parser. Step 3 uses heuristic chunking, so the implemented vocabulary is the simpler `'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`. The schema column is still a free-form `string`, so widening the vocabulary later is a code change, not a schema change.

`classifyChunkType` is line-anchored regex sniffing:
  - Counts code signals: declaration keywords at line starts.
  - Counts documentation signals: markdown headings, list bullets at line starts, blockquotes, inline `[text](url)` links.
  - Decision rule: if either side is at least 2x the other and non-zero, that wins. Otherwise `'mixed'`. Files with neither signal default to `'code'` (config files, plain text).
~~~

### Embedding Client (`openaiClient.ts`)

MindStudio's SDK has no embedding action, so we call an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly. Default deployment points at OpenRouter; bare OpenAI also works.

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/openaiClient.ts`.

Exports:
  - `embedTexts(texts: string[]): Promise<Array<number[] | null>>`
  - `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (constant, currently `1536`)

Secrets required (set in the Secrets tab):
  - `OPENAI_API_KEY` — credential for the embeddings endpoint.
  - `OPENAI_BASE_URL` — e.g. `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`. If unset, the SDK falls back to OpenAI's default base URL, which lets a developer point straight at OpenAI without code changes.

Model: `openai/text-embedding-3-small`. The `openai/` prefix is required for OpenRouter routing. When `OPENAI_BASE_URL` is empty or contains `api.openai.com` we strip the prefix automatically so bare OpenAI (which expects unprefixed model IDs) also works.

NPM dependency: `openai@^6.x` in `dist/methods/package.json`. The pre-v5 SDK pulls in `node-fetch`, which uses dynamic `require('stream')` and does not survive the methods bundler. v6 uses native `fetch` and bundles cleanly.

Resilience:
  - One batched request per call. The OpenAI / OpenRouter embeddings endpoint accepts arrays of inputs and returns one vector per input in order.
  - On HTTP 429 or `code === 'rate_limit_exceeded'`, sleep ~2 seconds and retry the entire batch ONCE. If it still fails, every slot in the batch is returned as `null`.
  - On any other error, log via `console.error` and return all-`null`. The caller (`chunkAndEmbedFile`) skips null slots, persists the rest, and reports `failedChunkCount` so the operator can see partial failures in the logs.
  - Empty / whitespace-only input strings are filtered out client-side (the API rejects them) and their slots return `null`.
  - The OpenAI client is instantiated once per process and cached in `cachedClient` to avoid per-call connection overhead.
~~~

### Re-ingestion Semantics

Ingesting the same `githubUrl` twice is supported and idempotent at the row level:

- `repositories` upserts on `githubUrl`, so the row's `id` is stable across runs. `status` flips back to `'processing'` immediately, and `lastScannedAt` is overwritten with the new completion timestamp only when the new run finishes successfully. A failed re-ingestion preserves the previous successful timestamp.
- `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)`, so file IDs are stable for paths that still exist in the repo. The `language` label is recomputed in case the extension mapping has been updated.
- `code_chunks` are deleted and recreated for every file processed in this run (in `chunkAndEmbedFile` step 1). This means a file's chunks always reflect the current run; partial-run states (e.g. a crash mid-loop) can leave files with no chunks until the next run.

What re-ingestion does **not** do:

- It does not delete `files` rows whose paths have been removed from the repo since the last scan. Stale rows linger, and so do their chunks. A "prune deleted files (and cascade-delete their chunks/docs)" pass is deferred to a later step — the cascading-delete chain documented in the data model is what that pass will use.
- It does not detect renames. A renamed file appears as a new `files` row with the new path; the old path remains as a stale row with its old chunks.

edits.heading: Out of Scope (Tracked for Later Steps)
edits.content:
- Step 4: Mapper and Deep-Dive agent prompts, models, and orchestration (`generateBible(repoId)` method).
- Pruning of stale `files` rows on re-ingestion (and the cascading-delete pass that comes with it).
- Concurrency in the per-file processing loop (currently serial).
- Web interface, navigation, flowchart rendering.
- Auth, multi-user concerns, per-repository ownership.
- Scenarios and seed data.

content.result:
--- src/app.md
+++ src/app.md
@@ -1,232 +1,321 @@
----
-name: Codebase Bible
-description: A RAG-based engine that ingests GitHub repositories, analyzes them with AI agents, and produces an interactive onboarding guide for new developers.
----
-
-# Codebase Bible
-
-An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app clones the repo, parses each source file into an Abstract Syntax Tree, breaks the code into semantically meaningful chunks (functions, classes, boilerplate), embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context from the rest of the repo to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible: navigable markdown documentation with embedded flowcharts, designed to onboard new developers quickly.
-
-~~~
-Build proceeds strictly step-by-step under user direction. This file currently covers Step 1 (data model) only. Methods, agent orchestration, ingestion pipeline, and interface specs are deliberately out of scope until subsequent steps are unlocked by the user.
-
-Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
-- The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
-- Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
-- All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
-~~~
-
-## Data Model
-
-The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.
-
-~~~
-Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):
-
-  delete repository
-    → delete all files where file.repo_id = repository.id
-      → delete all code_chunks where chunk.file_id IN (those file ids)
-      → delete all generated_docs where doc.file_id IN (those file ids)
-
-A shared helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) should own this so any method that removes a repo, or re-ingests one, calls a single function. Same shape applies for cascading delete of a single file.
-
-All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
-~~~
-
-### Repositories
-Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system.
-
-~~~
-Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
-- `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
-- `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`). Stored separately so the UI doesn't re-parse on every render.
-- `lastScannedAt` (number, unix ms, optional) — timestamp of the most recent successful ingestion run. `undefined` until the first scan completes; updated on every successful re-scan. Distinct from the auto-managed `updated_at` column, which moves on any write.
-
-Unique constraint: `[['githubUrl']]`. Required so re-ingestion can `upsert` on URL instead of duplicating rows.
-
-No status column in this step. Ingestion lifecycle (queued/scanning/ready/failed) is a Step 2+ concern and will be added when the ingestion pipeline is specified.
-~~~
-
-### Files
-One row per source file discovered inside a repository during ingestion. Owned by exactly one repository.
-
-~~~
-Table name: `files`. Columns:
-- `repoId` (string, FK → `repositories.id`) — owning repository. Required.
-- `filePath` (string) — path relative to the repo root, e.g. `src/lib/auth.ts`. Stored with forward slashes regardless of source OS.
-- `language` (string) — detected language label (e.g. `typescript`, `python`, `go`, `markdown`, `unknown`). Free-form string in this step; a controlled vocabulary can be introduced later if needed.
-
-The TypeScript interface for this table is named `RepoFile` (not `File`) to avoid colliding with the global DOM `File` type. The exported table object is still `Files`.
-
-Unique constraint: `[['repoId', 'filePath']]` — a given path appears at most once per repo. Allows re-ingestion to upsert rather than duplicate.
-
-Cascading delete: when a repository is removed, all files with matching `repoId` are removed in the same batch.
-~~~
-
-### Code Chunks
-Semantic units extracted from a file by the AST parser. A single file produces many chunks. Each chunk carries its text, a coarse type label, and an embedding vector for retrieval.
-
-~~~
-Table name: `code_chunks`. Columns:
-- `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — owning file. Required.
-- `chunkText` (string) — the exact source text of the chunk, preserved verbatim (whitespace and all) so retrieval results can be shown to the user as-is.
-- `chunkType` (string) — coarse category produced by the AST parser. Initial vocabulary: `function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, `type`, `import`, `boilerplate`, `comment`, `other`. Free-form string at the column level; the parser is responsible for staying within the agreed set. Widening or narrowing the set later is a code change, not a schema change.
-- `embedding` (number[], JSON-serialized) — embedding vector for semantic search. Stored as a JSON array of floats. Length is whatever the chosen embedding model produces (e.g. 1536 for OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`); the column is not length-constrained at the schema level so the embedding model can be swapped without a migration.
-
-No unique constraint. A file can produce many chunks, and identical chunk text can legitimately recur (boilerplate, repeated imports). De-duplication, if desired, is a pipeline concern, not a schema constraint.
-
-Cascading delete: when a file is removed (directly, or transitively via its repository), all chunks with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
-
-Embedding-search note: the initial query path loads candidate chunks via SQL filters (e.g. by `repoId` joined through `files`) and computes cosine similarity in the method process. Acceptable up to roughly ten-thousand chunks per query scope; above that, plan to migrate to an external vector index. The column shape does not change in either world.
-~~~
-
-### Generated Docs
-Markdown documentation produced by the AI agents for a given file. Multiple rows per file are allowed and intentional, so the history of generated docs is preserved across re-runs.
-
-~~~
-Table name: `generated_docs`. Columns:
-- `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — file the doc describes. Required.
-- `markdownContent` (string) — the full markdown body produced by the Deep-Dive agent (or, in some flows, the Mapper). May embed Mermaid flowchart blocks; rendering is a frontend concern. Stored as-is.
-
-Deliberately NOT declared on this table: a `createdAt` column. The platform provides a system `created_at` (unix ms) on every row, which is what the UI reads for "generated on …" values and what queries sort by. Adding a separate `createdAt` would create a confusing duplicate. The user-facing data contract for creation timestamp is satisfied by `doc.created_at`.
-
-No unique constraint. Multiple generations per file are valid — re-running the agents must not destroy previous output. "Latest doc for a file" is a query (`sortBy(d => d.created_at).reverse().take(1)`), not a uniqueness rule.
-
-Cascading delete: when a file is removed, all generated_docs with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
-~~~
-
-## Ingestion
-
-Ingestion is the process of pointing the app at a GitHub repository and turning it into rows in our database that downstream agents can read. Step 2 covers the structural ingestion: fetch the file list, filter the noise, and persist `repositories` + `files`. Chunking and embedding (the `code_chunks` table) is wired in via a placeholder and is implemented in Step 3.
-
-### `ingestRepository` Method
-
-The single public method for Step 2. The user provides a GitHub URL; the method returns a summary of what was ingested.
-
-~~~
-Manifest entry:
-  id: `ingest-repository`
-  path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
-  export: `ingestRepository`
-
-Input:
-  `{ githubUrl: string }`
-
-Output:
-  `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, fileCount, filteredCount, truncated }`
-
-The method runs unauthenticated for the MVP. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
-~~~
-
-The pipeline runs the following steps in order. Any step that fails surfaces a friendly error to the caller; the underlying details are written to `console.error` for debugging.
-
-1. **Parse the URL.** Accept `https://github.com/owner/name`, the same with `.git` suffix or trailing path (e.g. `/tree/main`), and `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalize to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so re-ingestion of variants of the same URL collapses onto one row.
-2. **Fetch repository metadata** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}` to learn the [default branch]{The branch name (e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`) the repo serves as its tip. We always ingest the default branch in Step 2; arbitrary-branch ingestion is a later step.}.
-3. **Fetch the recursive file tree** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}/git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1`. Surface the response's `truncated` flag back to the caller; we do not paginate the tree in this step.
-4. **Filter** the tree to text-only blobs worth indexing (see "Filtering Rules" below).
-5. **Upsert the `repositories` row** keyed on `githubUrl`. Sets `repoName` to `owner/name` and `lastScannedAt` to the current unix-ms timestamp.
-6. **Upsert each surviving file row** into `files`, keyed on `(repoId, filePath)`. Re-ingestion updates rows in place rather than creating duplicates, which keeps file IDs stable across runs (important once Step 3 starts attaching chunks to those IDs).
-7. **Invoke `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`** once per persisted file. In Step 2 this is a placeholder that just logs; Step 3 replaces its body with real chunking + embedding logic.
-
-~~~
-GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Uses native global `fetch` (Node 18+ runtime).
-
-Authentication is optional. If the secret `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set in `process.env`, it is sent as a Bearer token. Without it, requests are unauthenticated and subject to GitHub's 60-requests-per-hour-per-IP limit. For testing against small public repos this is fine; for any real use the token should be configured.
-
-GitHub-specific status codes get user-friendly error mapping:
-  - 404 → "Repository not found. Make sure the URL is correct and the repository is public (or that GITHUB_TOKEN is configured for private access)."
-  - 403 / 429 → "GitHub API rate limit reached. Configure a GITHUB_TOKEN secret to raise the limit, then try again."
-  - other non-2xx → "GitHub API request failed (status). Check the logs for details." with the raw response body in `console.error`.
-
-Required headers on every request: `Accept: application/vnd.github+json`, `X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28`, `User-Agent: codebase-bible-ingestor`.
-~~~
-
-### Filtering Rules
-
-Not every file in a repo is worth indexing. The filter has four layers, applied in order; the first that matches discards the file.
-
-~~~
-Filter implementation lives in `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts`. All sets are exported as `const` at the top of the file so the rule set is easy to audit and amend without rummaging through logic.
-
-1. Boilerplate directory segments. If any segment of the path matches one of these, the file is dropped. Initial set:
-   `node_modules`, `.git`, `.github`, `.svn`, `.hg`, `dist`, `build`, `out`,
-   `.next`, `.nuxt`, `.svelte-kit`, `.astro`, `.expo`, `.output`, `target`,
-   `vendor`, `__pycache__`, `.venv`, `venv`, `.idea`, `.vscode`, `.vs`,
-   `coverage`, `.nyc_output`, `.cache`, `.parcel-cache`, `.turbo`,
-   `.vercel`, `.pnpm-store`, `bower_components`, `jspm_packages`, `Pods`,
-   `DerivedData`.
-2. Skip-by-filename (exact match on the basename). Lockfiles and OS metadata:
-   `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`,
-   `Cargo.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `composer.lock`,
-   `Pipfile.lock`, `Podfile.lock`, `mix.lock`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`.
-3. Skip-by-pattern: minified bundles (`*.min.js|css|html|mjs|cjs`) and sourcemaps (`*.map`).
-4. Skip-by-extension. Binary or non-textual extensions: image (png, jpg, jpeg, gif, webp, ico, bmp, tif, tiff, svg), document (pdf, doc(x), ppt(x), xls(x)), archive (zip, tar, gz, bz2, xz, 7z, rar), audio/video (mp4, mp3, wav, ogg, mov, avi, webm, flac, aac, m4a), font (woff, woff2, ttf, otf, eot), compiled (exe, dll, so, dylib, a, o, obj, class, jar, war, ear, wasm, pyc, pyo, pyd), data (sqlite, sqlite3, db, dat, bin).
-5. Size cap: files larger than 1 MB are skipped (the GitHub tree response carries `size` in bytes per blob).
-
-Only tree nodes with `type === 'blob'` are considered — directories and submodule pointers are ignored before this filter even runs.
-~~~
-
-### Language Detection
-
-Each surviving file is tagged with a `language` label, derived purely from its filename. No content sniffing; this is a hackathon MVP and extension-based detection is sufficient.
-
-~~~
-Language detection lives alongside the filter in `fileFilters.ts` as `detectLanguage(path)`.
-
-Special-cased filenames (no extension): `Dockerfile` and `Dockerfile.*` → `dockerfile`; `Makefile`, `GNUmakefile` → `makefile`; `Rakefile`, `Gemfile`, `Podfile` → `ruby`.
-
-Otherwise mapped by extension. Initial mapping covers the common languages a developer onboarding to a typical repo would encounter: typescript, javascript, python, ruby, go, rust, java, kotlin, swift, objective-c/cpp, c, cpp, csharp, fsharp, php, scala, clojure, elixir, erlang, elm, haskell, ocaml, dart, lua, r, perl, shell, powershell, sql, graphql, protobuf, vue, svelte, astro, html, css/scss/sass/less/stylus, markdown/mdx/restructuredtext/text, json/yaml/toml/ini/xml/csv.
-
-Unmapped extensions → `language: 'unknown'`. This is a deliberate fall-through, not an error condition; downstream agents can still read `chunkText` and reason about the file.
-~~~
-
-### `chunkAndEmbedFile` Placeholder
-
-The call site for the chunking + embedding pipeline is wired in Step 2 with its final signature so Step 3 only needs to fill in the body.
-
-~~~
-Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.
-
-Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number }>`.
-
-Step 2 behavior: log that it was called (including the fileId and the length of `rawContent`) and return `{ chunkCount: 0 }`. No DB writes.
-
-Step 3 (deferred) will:

-### Re-ingestion Semantics
-
-Ingesting the same `githubUrl` twice is supported and idempotent at the row level:
-
-- `repositories` upserts on `githubUrl`, so the row's `id` is stable across runs. `lastScannedAt` is overwritten with the new timestamp.
-- `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)`, so file IDs are stable for paths that still exist in the repo. The `language` label is recomputed in case the extension mapping has been updated.
-
-What re-ingestion does **not** do in Step 2:
-
-- It does not delete `files` rows whose paths have been removed from the repo since the last scan. Stale rows linger. A "prune deleted files (and cascade-delete their chunks/docs)" pass is deferred to a later step — the cascading-delete chain documented earlier in this spec is what that pass will use.
-- It does not detect renames. A renamed file appears as a new `files` row with the new path; the old path remains as a stale row.
-

-Once `chunkAndEmbedFile` does real work, the per-file cost dominates ingestion time. Hundreds of files × (raw-content fetch + AST parse + per-chunk embedding) will exceed any reasonable method timeout. Step 3 will need to:
-  - Add a `status` column to `repositories` (e.g. `idle | scanning | ready | failed`).
-  - Convert `ingestRepository` to a fire-and-forget shape: persist the repo + file rows synchronously, return immediately with `repositoryId` and `status: 'scanning'`, and run the chunking + embedding loop in a background promise that updates `status` on completion or failure.
-
-The shape is documented in the platform's "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" pattern.
-~~~
