The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
  26 - Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
  27 - All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
  28 ~~~
  29 
  30 ## Data Model
  31 
  32 The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.
  33 
  34 ~~~
  35 Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):
  36 
  37   delete repository
  38     → delete all files where file.repo_id = repository.id
  39       → delete all code_chunks where chunk.file_id IN (those file ids)
  40       → delete all generated_docs where doc.file_id IN (those file ids)
  41 
  42 A shared helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) should own this so any method that removes a repo, or re-ingests one, calls a single function. Same shape applies for cascading delete of a single file.
  43 
  44 All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
  45 ~~~
  46 
  47 ### Repositories
  48 Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Includes a lifecycle status because ingestion is asynchronous.
  49 
  50 ~~~
  51 Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
  52 - `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
  53 - `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`). Stored separately so the UI doesn't re-parse on every render.
  54 - `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type alias) — lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. Values:
  55     - `pending` — row exists, ingestion not yet started. Reserved for a future queueing layer; not actively written today.
  56     - `processing` — ingestion is currently running in the background.
  57     - `completed` — last run finished successfully.
  58     - `failed` — last run crashed; details in method logs (`console.error`).
  59 - `lastScannedAt` (number, unix ms, optional) — timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run. `undefined` until the first run reaches `status === 'completed'`. Deliberately NOT bumped on transitions to `processing` or `failed` — the column answers "when did we last have a complete picture of this repo?", not "when did we last touch the row?". Use the auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.
  60 
  61 Unique constraint: `[['githubUrl']]`. Required so re-ingestion can `upsert` on URL instead of duplicating rows.
  62 ~~~
  63 
  64 ### Files
  65 One row per source file discovered inside a repository during ingestion. Owned by exactly one repository.
  66 
  67 ~~~
  68 Table name: `files`. Columns:
  69 - `repoId` (string, FK → `repositories.id`) — owning repository. Required.
  70 - `filePath` (string) — path relative to the repo root, e.g. `src/lib/auth.ts`. Stored with forward slashes regardless of source OS.
  71 - `language` (string) — detected language label (e.g. `typescript`, `python`, `go`, `markdown`, `unknown`). Free-form string in this step; a controlled vocabulary can be introduced later if needed.
  72 
  73 The TypeScript interface for this table is named `RepoFile` (not `File`) to avoid colliding with the global DOM `File` type. The exported table object is still `Files`.
  74 
  75 Unique constraint: `[['repoId', 'filePath']]` — a given path appears at most once per repo. Allows re-ingestion to upsert rather than duplicate.
  76 
  77 Cascading delete: when a repository is removed, all files with matching `repoId` are removed in the same batch.
  78 ~~~
  79 
  80 ### Code Chunks
  81 Semantic units extracted from a file by the AST parser. A single file produces many chunks. Each chunk carries its text, a coarse type label, and an embedding vector for retrieval.
  82 
  83 ~~~
  84 Table name: `code_chunks`. Columns:
  85 - `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — owning file. Required.
  86 - `chunkText` (string) — the exact source text of the chunk, preserved verbatim (whitespace and all) so retrieval results can be shown to the user as-is.
  87 - `chunkType` (string) — coarse category produced by the AST parser. Initial vocabulary: `function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, `type`, `import`, `boilerplate`, `comment`, `other`. Free-form string at the column level; the parser is responsible for staying within the agreed set. Widening or narrowing the set later is a code change, not a schema change.
  88 - `embedding` (number[], JSON-serialized) — embedding vector for semantic search. Stored as a JSON array of floats. Length is whatever the chosen embedding model produces (e.g. 1536 for OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`); the column is not length-constrained at the schema level so the embedding model can be swapped without a migration.
  89 
  90 No unique constraint. A file can produce many chunks, and identical chunk text can legitimately recur (boilerplate, repeated imports). De-duplication, if desired, is a pipeline concern, not a schema constraint.
  91 
  92 Cascading delete: when a file is removed (directly, or transitively via its repository), all chunks with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
  93 
  94 Embedding-search note: the initial query path loads candidate chunks via SQL filters (e.g. by `repoId` joined through `files`) and computes cosine similarity in the method process. Acceptable up to roughly ten-thousand chunks per query scope; above that, plan to migrate to an external vector index. The column shape does not change in either world.
  95 ~~~
  96 
  97 ### Generated Docs
  98 Markdown documentation produced by the AI agents for a given file. Multiple rows per file are allowed and intentional, so the history of generated docs is preserved across re-runs.
  99 
 100 ~~~
 101 Table name: `generated_docs`. Columns:
 102 - `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — file the doc describes. Required.
 103 - `markdownContent` (string) — the full markdown body produced by the Deep-Dive agent (or, in some flows, the Mapper). May embed Mermaid flowchart blocks; rendering is a frontend concern. Stored as-is.
 104 
 105 Deliberately NOT declared on this table: a `createdAt` column. The platform provides a system `created_at` (unix ms) on every row, which is what the UI reads for "generated on …" values and what queries sort by. Adding a separate `createdAt` would create a confusing duplicate. The user-facing data contract for creation timestamp is satisfied by `doc.created_at`.
 106 
 107 No unique constraint. Multiple generations per file are valid — re-running the agents must not destroy previous output. "Latest doc for a file" is a query (`sortBy(d => d.created_at).reverse().take(1)`), not a uniqueness rule.
 108 
 109 Cascading delete: when a file is removed, all generated_docs with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
 110 ~~~
 111 
 112 ## Ingestion
 113 
 114 Ingestion is the process of pointing the app at a GitHub repository and turning it into rows in our database that downstream agents can read. Step 2 covers the structural ingestion: fetch the file list, filter the noise, and persist `repositories` + `files`. Chunking and embedding (the `code_chunks` table) is wired in via a placeholder and is implemented in Step 3.
 115 
 116 ### `ingestRepository` Method
 117 The single public method for ingestion. The caller provides a GitHub URL; the method validates synchronously and kicks off the heavy work in a background task. Returns immediately with `status: 'processing'`.
 118 
 119 ~~~
 120 Manifest entry:
 121   id: `ingest-repository`
 122   path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
 123   export: `ingestRepository`
 124 
 125 Input:
 126   `{ githubUrl: string }`
 127 
 128 Output (returned synchronously):
 129   `{ repositoryId: string, repoName: string, defaultBranch: string, status: 'processing' }`
 130 
 131 The method runs unauthenticated for the MVP. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
 132 ~~~
 133 
 134 The method runs in two phases:
 135 
 136 **Phase A — synchronous validation and row creation.** Returned to the caller as soon as it completes. Bad URLs and missing repositories produce errors here, before any background work is started.
 137 
 138 1. **Parse the URL.** Accept `https://github.com/owner/name`, the same with `.git` suffix or trailing path (e.g. `/tree/main`), and `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalize to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so re-ingestion of variants of the same URL collapses onto one row.
 139 2. **Fetch repository metadata** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}` to confirm the repo exists and learn the [default branch]{The branch name (e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`) the repo serves as its tip. We always ingest the default branch in Step 3; arbitrary-branch ingestion is a later step.}.
 140 3. **Upsert the `repositories` row** keyed on `githubUrl`. Sets `repoName` to `owner/name` and `status` to `'processing'`. `lastScannedAt` is intentionally not touched yet — it will be set only when (and if) the background phase reaches `'completed'`.
 141 4. **Return** `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, status: 'processing' }` to the caller.
 142 
 143 **Phase B — fire-and-forget background ingestion.** Begins after Phase A returns. Uses the platform's [un-awaited promise pattern]{The execution environment persists between requests, so a promise that is not awaited continues running after the method returns. See "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" in the platform docs.}.
 144 
 145 5. **Fetch the recursive file tree** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}/git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1`.
 146 6. **Filter** the tree to text-only blobs worth indexing (see "Filtering Rules" below).
 147 7. **Upsert each surviving file row** into `files`, keyed on `(repoId, filePath)`. Re-ingestion updates rows in place rather than creating duplicates, which keeps file IDs stable across runs (important so chunks from previous runs can be cleanly replaced in step 8).
 148 8. **Per file**, fetch raw content from `raw.githubusercontent.com/{owner}/{repo}/{branch}/{path}` (with `GITHUB_TOKEN` Bearer if configured) and pass it to `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`. Per-file failures (network blips, transient 5xx, unicode trouble) are logged and skipped — a single bad file does not abort the whole run.
 149 9. **Mark the repository `'completed'`** and write a fresh `lastScannedAt`. On any unhandled error in steps 5–9, the row is marked `'failed'` instead and the error detail is in `console.error`.
 150 
 151 ~~~
 152 GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Uses native global `fetch` (Node 18+ runtime).
 153 
 154 Authentication is optional. If the secret `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set in `process.env`, it is sent as a Bearer token on both the REST API requests and the `raw.githubusercontent.com` requests. Without it, requests are unauthenticated and subject to GitHub's 60-requests-per-hour-per-IP limit.
 155 
 156 GitHub-specific status codes get user-friendly error mapping (only relevant for Phase A; Phase B logs and continues):
 157   - 404 → "Repository not found. Make sure the URL is correct and the repository is public (or that GITHUB_TOKEN is configured for private access)."
 158   - 403 / 429 → "GitHub API rate limit reached. Configure a GITHUB_TOKEN secret to raise the limit, then try again."
 159   - other non-2xx → "GitHub API request failed (status). Check the logs for details." with the raw response body in `console.error`.
 160 
 161 Required headers on every request: `Accept: application/vnd.github+json`, `X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28`, `User-Agent: codebase-bible-ingestor`.
 162 ~~~
 163 
 164 ### Background Task Concurrency
 165 
 166 Files are processed serially inside the background task. Per file: fetch raw content, chunk, embed (one batched API call), persist. Files do not run in parallel today.
 167 
 168 ~~~
 169 Serial processing is a conscious choice for the MVP:
 170   - Embedding cost is dominated by the per-call round trip, not the per-chunk cost. Batching all of one file's chunks into a single embedding call (which we already do) captures most of the available speedup.
 171   - Adding parallelism across files introduces concurrency-limit bookkeeping and complicates rate-limit handling.
 172 
 173 If throughput becomes a problem on large repos, the natural upgrade is a small concurrency pool (e.g. 4-wide) around the per-file loop in `runIngestion`.
 174 ~~~
 175 
 176 ### Filtering Rules
 177 
 178 Not every file in a repo is worth indexing. The filter has four layers, applied in order; the first that matches discards the file.
 179 
 180 ~~~
 181 Filter implementation lives in `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts`. All sets are exported as `const` at the top of the file so the rule set is easy to audit and amend without rummaging through logic.
 182 
 183 1. Boilerplate directory segments. If any segment of the path matches one of these, the file is dropped. Initial set:
 184    `node_modules`, `.git`, `.github`, `.svn`, `.hg`, `dist`, `build`, `out`,
 185    `.next`, `.nuxt`, `.svelte-kit`, `.astro`, `.expo`, `.output`, `target`,
 186    `vendor`, `__pycache__`, `.venv`, `venv`, `.idea`, `.vscode`, `.vs`,
 187    `coverage`, `.nyc_output`, `.cache`, `.parcel-cache`, `.turbo`,
 188    `.vercel`, `.pnpm-store`, `bower_components`, `jspm_packages`, `Pods`,
 189    `DerivedData`.
 190 2. Skip-by-filename (exact match on the basename). Lockfiles and OS metadata:
 191    `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`,
 192    `Cargo.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `composer.lock`,
 193    `Pipfile.lock`, `Podfile.lock`, `mix.lock`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`.
 194 3. Skip-by-pattern: minified bundles (`*.min.js|css|html|mjs|cjs`) and sourcemaps (`*.map`).
 195 4. Skip-by-extension. Binary or non-textual extensions: image (png, jpg, jpeg, gif, webp, ico, bmp, tif, tiff, svg), document (pdf, doc(x), ppt(x), xls(x)), archive (zip, tar, gz, bz2, xz, 7z, rar), audio/video (mp4, mp3, wav, ogg, mov, avi, webm, flac, aac, m4a), font (woff, woff2, ttf, otf, eot), compiled (exe, dll, so, dylib, a, o, obj, class, jar, war, ear, wasm, pyc, pyo, pyd), data (sqlite, sqlite3, db, dat, bin).
 196 5. Size cap: files larger than 1 MB are skipped (the GitHub tree response carries `size` in bytes per blob).
 197 
 198 Only tree nodes with `type === 'blob'` are considered — directories and submodule pointers are ignored before this filter even runs.
 199 ~~~
 200 
 201 ### Language Detection
 202 
 203 Each surviving file is tagged with a `language` label, derived purely from its filename. No content sniffing; this is a hackathon MVP and extension-based detection is sufficient.
 204 
 205 ~~~
 206 Language detection lives alongside the filter in `fileFilters.ts` as `detectLanguage(path)`.
 207 
 208 Special-cased filenames (no extension): `Dockerfile` and `Dockerfile.*` → `dockerfile`; `Makefile`, `GNUmakefile` → `makefile`; `Rakefile`, `Gemfile`, `Podfile` → `ruby`.
 209 
 210 Otherwise mapped by extension. Initial mapping covers the common languages a developer onboarding to a typical repo would encounter: typescript, javascript, python, ruby, go, rust, java, kotlin, swift, objective-c/cpp, c, cpp, csharp, fsharp, php, scala, clojure, elixir, erlang, elm, haskell, ocaml, dart, lua, r, perl, shell, powershell, sql, graphql, protobuf, vue, svelte, astro, html, css/scss/sass/less/stylus, markdown/mdx/restructuredtext/text, json/yaml/toml/ini/xml/csv.
 211 
 212 Unmapped extensions → `language: 'unknown'`. This is a deliberate fall-through, not an error condition; downstream agents can still read `chunkText` and reason about the file.
 213 ~~~
 214 
 215 ### `chunkAndEmbedFile` Placeholder
 216 Per-file chunk + embed + persist routine. Public signature unchanged from Step 2 (`chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`).
 217 
 218 ~~~
 219 Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.
 220 
 221 Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number, failedChunkCount: number }>`.
 222 
 223 Behavior:
 224   1. Drop existing `code_chunks` rows for `fileId` so re-ingestion replaces in place. (This is intra-file idempotency. The cascade-delete chain documented earlier handles the case where the entire `files` row is removed.) Implemented as a single `removeAll` with a bindings predicate, which compiles to one DELETE WHERE.
 225   2. Run the smart chunker (`chunkText`) over `rawContent`. Empty / whitespace-only files produce zero chunks and exit with `{ chunkCount: 0, failedChunkCount: 0 }`.
 226   3. Embed all chunks in a single batched call to `embedTexts`. Slots that fail to embed (after one rate-limit retry) come back as `null` and are skipped, not retried per-chunk.
 227   4. Build rows for `code_chunks`: `fileId`, `chunkText`, `chunkType` (classified per-chunk by `classifyChunkType`), `embedding`. Embeddings whose dimensionality differs from `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (1536 for text-embedding-3-small) are still persisted, but a `console.warn` is emitted so model swaps are visible.
 228   5. Persist via `db.batch` in groups of 100 mutations.
 229 
 230 Returns `{ chunkCount, failedChunkCount }` for diagnostic purposes; the ingestion driver aggregates these across files for its summary log line.
 231 ~~~
 232 
 233 ### Smart Chunker (`textChunker.ts`)
 234 
 235 A pragmatic chunker that splits source files and prose along logical boundaries, packs them into chunks of ≌4,000 characters, and preserves a 400-character overlap between adjacent chunks so context is not lost when a function or paragraph straddles a split.
 236 
 237 ~~~
 238 Location: `dist/methods/src/common/textChunker.ts`.
 239 
 240 Exports:
 241   - `chunkText(text: string, opts?: { maxChars?: number, overlap?: number }): string[]`
 242   - `classifyChunkType(content: string): 'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`
 243 
 244 Defaults: `maxChars = 4_000`, `overlap = 400` (10% of maxChars per the spec).
 245 
 246 Algorithm (in `chunkText`):
 247   1. Empty / whitespace-only input → `[]`.
 248   2. Input shorter than `maxChars` → `[text]` (single chunk, no work).
 249   3. Otherwise split into "blocks" along logical boundaries. The split regex matches:
 250      - Runs of blank lines (`\n\s*\n+`).
 251      - Lines starting (after optional indentation) with a declaration keyword common in code: `export`, `default`, `async`, `function`, `class`, `interface`, `type`, `enum`, `namespace`, `module`, `struct`, `impl`, `trait`, `fn`, `def`, `public`, `private`, `protected`, `static`.
 252      - Markdown headings (`#` through `######`).
 253      This is heuristic, NOT an AST. It is good enough that well-formatted code keeps function/class units together; it is not perfect on minified or oddly-formatted input.
 254   4. Greedy-pack blocks into chunks: append blocks to the current chunk until adding the next block would exceed `maxChars`. At that point, flush the current chunk, then seed a new chunk with the LAST `overlap` characters of the just-flushed chunk, followed by the new block. The seeded overlap is what preserves cross-chunk context.
 255   5. If a single block is itself larger than `maxChars` (e.g. a giant minified line, or a function with no internal blank lines), it goes through a sliding-window splitter with stride `maxChars - overlap`. This guarantees no chunk exceeds the cap, at the cost of arbitrary boundaries inside that block.
 256   6. Belt-and-suspenders: any chunk that somehow ends up over the cap gets a final pass through the sliding-window splitter.
 257 
 258 Note on the relaxed `chunkType` vocabulary: the Step 1 spec mentioned a more granular vocabulary (`function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, etc.) that would only be reachable with a true AST parser. Step 3 uses heuristic chunking, so the implemented vocabulary is the simpler `'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`. The schema column is still a free-form `string`, so widening the vocabulary later is a code change, not a schema change.
 259 
 260 `classifyChunkType` is line-anchored regex sniffing:
 261   - Counts code signals: declaration keywords at line starts.
 262   - Counts documentation signals: markdown headings, list bullets at line starts, blockquotes, inline `[text](url)` links.
 263   - Decision rule: if either side is at least 2x the other and non-zero, that wins. Otherwise `'mixed'`. Files with neither signal default to `'code'` (config files, plain text).
 264 ~~~
 265 
 266 ### Embedding Client (`openaiClient.ts`)
 267 
 268 MindStudio's SDK has no embedding action, so we call an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly. Default deployment points at OpenRouter; bare OpenAI also works.
 269 
 270 ~~~
 271 Location: `dist/methods/src/common/openaiClient.ts`.
 272 
 273 Exports:
 274   - `embedTexts(texts: string[]): Promise<Array<number[] | null>>`
 275   - `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (constant, currently `1536`)
 276 
 277 Secrets required (set in the Secrets tab):
 278   - `OPENAI_API_KEY` — credential for the embeddings endpoint.
 279   - `OPENAI_BASE_URL` — e.g. `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`. If unset, the SDK falls back to OpenAI's default base URL, which lets a developer point straight at OpenAI without code changes.
 280 
 281 Model: `openai/text-embedding-3-small`. The `openai/` prefix is required for OpenRouter routing. When `OPENAI_BASE_URL` is empty or contains `api.openai.com` we strip the prefix automatically so bare OpenAI (which expects unprefixed model IDs) also works.
 282 
 283 NPM dependency: `openai@^6.x` in `dist/methods/package.json`. The pre-v5 SDK pulls in `node-fetch`, which uses dynamic `require('stream')` and does not survive the methods bundler. v6 uses native `fetch` and bundles cleanly.
 284 
 285 Resilience:
 286   - One batched request per call. The OpenAI / OpenRouter embeddings endpoint accepts arrays of inputs and returns one vector per input in order.
 287   - On HTTP 429 or `code === 'rate_limit_exceeded'`, sleep ~2 seconds and retry the entire batch ONCE. If it still fails, every slot in the batch is returned as `null`.
 288   - On any other error, log via `console.error` and return all-`null`. The caller (`chunkAndEmbedFile`) skips null slots, persists the rest, and reports `failedChunkCount` so the operator can see partial failures in the logs.
 289   - Empty / whitespace-only input strings are filtered out client-side (the API rejects them) and their slots return `null`.
 290   - The OpenAI client is instantiated once per process and cached in `cachedClient` to avoid per-call connection overhead.
 291 ~~~
 292 
 293 ### Re-ingestion Semantics
 294 
 295 Ingesting the same `githubUrl` twice is supported and idempotent at the row level:
 296 
 297 - `repositories` upserts on `githubUrl`, so the row's `id` is stable across runs. `lastScannedAt` is overwritten with the new timestamp.
 298 - `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)`, so file IDs are stable for paths that still exist in the repo. The `language` label is recomputed in case the extension mapping has been updated.
 299 
 300 What re-ingestion does **not** do in Step 2:
 301 
 302 - It does not delete `files` rows whose paths have been removed from the repo since the last scan. Stale rows linger. A "prune deleted files (and cascade-delete their chunks/docs)" pass is deferred to a later step — the cascading-delete chain documented earlier in this spec is what that pass will use.
 303 - It does not detect renames. A renamed file appears as a new `files` row with the new path; the old path remains as a stale row.
 304 
 305 ~~~
 306 Step 3 prerequisite (flagged for early visibility, not implemented in Step 2):
 307 
 308 Once `chunkAndEmbedFile` does real work, the per-file cost dominates ingestion time. Hundreds of files × (raw-content fetch + AST parse + per-chunk embedding) will exceed any reasonable method timeout. Step 3 will need to:
 309   - Add a `status` column to `repositories` (e.g. `idle | scanning | ready | failed`).
 310   - Convert `ingestRepository` to a fire-and-forget shape: persist the repo + file rows synchronously, return immediately with `repositoryId` and `status: 'scanning'`, and run the chunking + embedding loop in a background promise that updates `status` on completion or failure.
 311 
 312 The shape is documented in the platform's "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" pattern.
 313 ~~~
 314 
 315 ## Out of Scope (Tracked for Later Steps)
 316 - Step 4: Mapper and Deep-Dive agent prompts, models, and orchestration (`generateBible(repoId)` method).
 317 - Pruning of stale `files` rows on re-ingestion (and the cascading-delete pass that comes with it).
 318 - Concurrency in the per-file processing loop (currently serial).
 319 - Web interface, navigation, flowchart rendering.
 320 - Auth, multi-user concerns, per-repository ownership.
 321 - Scenarios and seed data.
 322

messages.content:
1 An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app fetches the repo's file tree, breaks each source file into semantically meaningful chunks (function/class/declaration boundaries plus paragraph boundaries), embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context from the rest of the repo to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible: navigable markdown documentation with embedded flowcharts, designed to onboard new developers quickly.
   2 
   3 ~~~
   4 Build proceeds strictly step-by-step under user direction. This file currently covers Steps 1–3:
   5   - Step 1: data model.
   6   - Step 2: GitHub ingestion (URL parsing, tree fetch, filtering, file persistence).
   7   - Step 3: async architecture, smart chunking, OpenAI-compatible embeddings, persistence.
   8 Methods for the agent layer (Mapper, Deep-Dive) and the web UI are deliberately out of scope until subsequent steps are unlocked by the user.
   9 
  10 Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
  11 - The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
  12 - Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
  13 - The MindStudio SDK does NOT have an embedding action. Embeddings are generated by calling an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package, configured to point at OpenRouter (or bare OpenAI, or any other compatible provider) via the `OPENAI_BASE_URL` secret.
  14 - All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
  15 ~~~
  16 
  17 # Codebase Bible
  18 
  19 An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app clones the repo, parses each source file into an Abstract Syntax Tree, breaks the code into semantically meaningful chunks (functions, classes, boilerplate), embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context from the rest of the repo to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible: navigable markdown documentation with embedded flowcharts, designed to onboard new developers quickly.
  20 
  21 ~~~
  22 Build proceeds strictly step-by-step under user direction. This file currently covers Step 1 (data model) only. Methods, agent orchestration, ingestion pipeline, and interface specs are deliberately out of scope until subsequent steps are unlocked by the user.
  23 
  24 Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
  25 - The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
  26 - Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
  27 - All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
  28 ~~~
  29 
  30 ## Data Model
  31 
  32 The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.
  33 
  34 ~~~
  35 Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):
  36 
  37   delete repository
  38     → delete all files where file.repo_id = repository.id
  39       → delete all code_chunks where chunk.file_id IN (those file ids)
  40       → delete all generated_docs where doc.file_id IN (those file ids)
  41 
  42 A shared helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) should own this so any method that removes a repo, or re-ingests one, calls a single function. Same shape applies for cascading delete of a single file.
  43 
  44 All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
  45 ~~~
  46 
  47 ### Repositories
  48 Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Includes a lifecycle status because ingestion is asynchronous.
  49 
  50 ~~~
  51 Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
  52 - `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
  53 - `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`). Stored separately so the UI doesn't re-parse on every render.
  54 - `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type alias) — lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. Values:
  55     - `pending` — row exists, ingestion not yet started. Reserved for a future queueing layer; not actively written today.
  56     - `processing` — ingestion is currently running in the background.
  57     - `completed` — last run finished successfully.
  58     - `failed` — last run crashed; details in method logs (`console.error`).
  59 - `lastScannedAt` (number, unix ms, optional) — timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run. `undefined` until the first run reaches `status === 'completed'`. Deliberately NOT bumped on transitions to `processing` or `failed` — the column answers "when did we last have a complete picture of this repo?", not "when did we last touch the row?". Use the auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.
  60 
  61 Unique constraint: `[['githubUrl']]`. Required so re-ingestion can `upsert` on URL instead of duplicating rows.
  62 ~~~
  63 
  64 ### Files
  65 One row per source file discovered inside a repository during ingestion. Owned by exactly one repository.
  66 
  67 ~~~
  68 Table name: `files`. Columns:
  69 - `repoId` (string, FK → `repositories.id`) — owning repository. Required.
  70 - `filePath` (string) — path relative to the repo root, e.g. `src/lib/auth.ts`. Stored with forward slashes regardless of source OS.
  71 - `language` (string) — detected language label (e.g. `typescript`, `python`, `go`, `markdown`, `unknown`). Free-form string in this step; a controlled vocabulary can be introduced later if needed.
  72 
  73 The TypeScript interface for this table is named `RepoFile` (not `File`) to avoid colliding with the global DOM `File` type. The exported table object is still `Files`.
  74 
  75 Unique constraint: `[['repoId', 'filePath']]` — a given path appears at most once per repo. Allows re-ingestion to upsert rather than duplicate.
  76 
  77 Cascading delete: when a repository is removed, all files with matching `repoId` are removed in the same batch.
  78 ~~~
  79 
  80 ### Code Chunks
  81 Semantic units extracted from a file by the AST parser. A single file produces many chunks. Each chunk carries its text, a coarse type label, and an embedding vector for retrieval.
  82 
  83 ~~~
  84 Table name: `code_chunks`. Columns:
  85 - `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — owning file. Required.
  86 - `chunkText` (string) — the exact source text of the chunk, preserved verbatim (whitespace and all) so retrieval results can be shown to the user as-is.
  87 - `chunkType` (string) — coarse category produced by the AST parser. Initial vocabulary: `function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, `type`, `import`, `boilerplate`, `comment`, `other`. Free-form string at the column level; the parser is responsible for staying within the agreed set. Widening or narrowing the set later is a code change, not a schema change.
  88 - `embedding` (number[], JSON-serialized) — embedding vector for semantic search. Stored as a JSON array of floats. Length is whatever the chosen embedding model produces (e.g. 1536 for OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`); the column is not length-constrained at the schema level so the embedding model can be swapped without a migration.
  89 
  90 No unique constraint. A file can produce many chunks, and identical chunk text can legitimately recur (boilerplate, repeated imports). De-duplication, if desired, is a pipeline concern, not a schema constraint.
  91 
  92 Cascading delete: when a file is removed (directly, or transitively via its repository), all chunks with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
  93 
  94 Embedding-search note: the initial query path loads candidate chunks via SQL filters (e.g. by `repoId` joined through `files`) and computes cosine similarity in the method process. Acceptable up to roughly ten-thousand chunks per query scope; above that, plan to migrate to an external vector index. The column shape does not change in either world.
  95 ~~~
  96 
  97 ### Generated Docs
  98 Markdown documentation produced by the AI agents for a given file. Multiple rows per file are allowed and intentional, so the history of generated docs is preserved across re-runs.
  99 
 100 ~~~
 101 Table name: `generated_docs`. Columns:
 102 - `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — file the doc describes. Required.
 103 - `markdownContent` (string) — the full markdown body produced by the Deep-Dive agent (or, in some flows, the Mapper). May embed Mermaid flowchart blocks; rendering is a frontend concern. Stored as-is.
 104 
 105 Deliberately NOT declared on this table: a `createdAt` column. The platform provides a system `created_at` (unix ms) on every row, which is what the UI reads for "generated on …" values and what queries sort by. Adding a separate `createdAt` would create a confusing duplicate. The user-facing data contract for creation timestamp is satisfied by `doc.created_at`.
 106 
 107 No unique constraint. Multiple generations per file are valid — re-running the agents must not destroy previous output. "Latest doc for a file" is a query (`sortBy(d => d.created_at).reverse().take(1)`), not a uniqueness rule.
 108 
 109 Cascading delete: when a file is removed, all generated_docs with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
 110 ~~~
 111 
 112 ## Ingestion
 113 
 114 Ingestion is the process of pointing the app at a GitHub repository and turning it into rows in our database that downstream agents can read. Step 2 covers the structural ingestion: fetch the file list, filter the noise, and persist `repositories` + `files`. Chunking and embedding (the `code_chunks` table) is wired in via a placeholder and is implemented in Step 3.
 115 
 116 ### `ingestRepository` Method
 117 The single public method for ingestion. The caller provides a GitHub URL; the method validates synchronously and kicks off the heavy work in a background task. Returns immediately with `status: 'processing'`.
 118 
 119 ~~~
 120 Manifest entry:
 121   id: `ingest-repository`
 122   path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
 123   export: `ingestRepository`
 124 
 125 Input:
 126   `{ githubUrl: string }`
 127 
 128 Output (returned synchronously):
 129   `{ repositoryId: string, repoName: string, defaultBranch: string, status: 'processing' }`
 130 
 131 The method runs unauthenticated for the MVP. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
 132 ~~~
 133 
 134 The method runs in two phases:
 135 
 136 **Phase A — synchronous validation and row creation.** Returned to the caller as soon as it completes. Bad URLs and missing repositories produce errors here, before any background work is started.
 137 
 138 1. **Parse the URL.** Accept `https://github.com/owner/name`, the same with `.git` suffix or trailing path (e.g. `/tree/main`), and `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalize to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so re-ingestion of variants of the same URL collapses onto one row.
 139 2. **Fetch repository metadata** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}` to confirm the repo exists and learn the [default branch]{The branch name (e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`) the repo serves as its tip. We always ingest the default branch in Step 3; arbitrary-branch ingestion is a later step.}.
 140 3. **Upsert the `repositories` row** keyed on `githubUrl`. Sets `repoName` to `owner/name` and `status` to `'processing'`. `lastScannedAt` is intentionally not touched yet — it will be set only when (and if) the background phase reaches `'completed'`.
 141 4. **Return** `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, status: 'processing' }` to the caller.
 142 
 143 **Phase B — fire-and-forget background ingestion.** Begins after Phase A returns. Uses the platform's [un-awaited promise pattern]{The execution environment persists between requests, so a promise that is not awaited continues running after the method returns. See "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" in the platform docs.}.
 144 
 145 5. **Fetch the recursive file tree** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}/git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1`.
 146 6. **Filter** the tree to text-only blobs worth indexing (see "Filtering Rules" below).
 147 7. **Upsert each surviving file row** into `files`, keyed on `(repoId, filePath)`. Re-ingestion updates rows in place rather than creating duplicates, which keeps file IDs stable across runs (important so chunks from previous runs can be cleanly replaced in step 8).
 148 8. **Per file**, fetch raw content from `raw.githubusercontent.com/{owner}/{repo}/{branch}/{path}` (with `GITHUB_TOKEN` Bearer if configured) and pass it to `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`. Per-file failures (network blips, transient 5xx, unicode trouble) are logged and skipped — a single bad file does not abort the whole run.
 149 9. **Mark the repository `'completed'`** and write a fresh `lastScannedAt`. On any unhandled error in steps 5–9, the row is marked `'failed'` instead and the error detail is in `console.error`.
 150 
 151 ~~~
 152 GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Uses native global `fetch` (Node 18+ runtime).
 153 
 154 Authentication is optional. If the secret `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set in `process.env`, it is sent as a Bearer token on both the REST API requests and the `raw.githubusercontent.com` requests. Without it, requests are unauthenticated and subject to GitHub's 60-requests-per-hour-per-IP limit.
 155 
 156 GitHub-specific status codes get user-friendly error mapping (only relevant for Phase A; Phase B logs and continues):
 157   - 404 → "Repository not found. Make sure the URL is correct and the repository is public (or that GITHUB_TOKEN is configured for private access)."
 158   - 403 / 429 → "GitHub API rate limit reached. Configure a GITHUB_TOKEN secret to raise the limit, then try again."
 159   - other non-2xx → "GitHub API request failed (status). Check the logs for details." with the raw response body in `console.error`.
 160 
 161 Required headers on every request: `Accept: application/vnd.github+json`, `X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28`, `User-Agent: codebase-bible-ingestor`.
 162 ~~~
 163 
 164 ### Background Task Concurrency
 165 
 166 Files are processed serially inside the background task. Per file: fetch raw content, chunk, embed (one batched API call), persist. Files do not run in parallel today.
 167 
 168 ~~~
 169 Serial processing is a conscious choice for the MVP:
 170   - Embedding cost is dominated by the per-call round trip, not the per-chunk cost. Batching all of one file's chunks into a single embedding call (which we already do) captures most of the available speedup.
 171   - Adding parallelism across files introduces concurrency-limit bookkeeping and complicates rate-limit handling.
 172 
 173 If throughput becomes a problem on large repos, the natural upgrade is a small concurrency pool (e.g. 4-wide) around the per-file loop in `runIngestion`.
 174 ~~~
 175 
 176 ### Filtering Rules
 177 
 178 Not every file in a repo is worth indexing. The filter has four layers, applied in order; the first that matches discards the file.
 179 
 180 ~~~
 181 Filter implementation lives in `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts`. All sets are exported as `const` at the top of the file so the rule set is easy to audit and amend without rummaging through logic.
 182 
 183 1. Boilerplate directory segments. If any segment of the path matches one of these, the file is dropped. Initial set:
 184    `node_modules`, `.git`, `.github`, `.svn`, `.hg`, `dist`, `build`, `out`,
 185    `.next`, `.nuxt`, `.svelte-kit`, `.astro`, `.expo`, `.output`, `target`,
 186    `vendor`, `__pycache__`, `.venv`, `venv`, `.idea`, `.vscode`, `.vs`,
 187    `coverage`, `.nyc_output`, `.cache`, `.parcel-cache`, `.turbo`,
 188    `.vercel`, `.pnpm-store`, `bower_components`, `jspm_packages`, `Pods`,
 189    `DerivedData`.
 190 2. Skip-by-filename (exact match on the basename). Lockfiles and OS metadata:
 191    `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`,
 192    `Cargo.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `composer.lock`,
 193    `Pipfile.lock`, `Podfile.lock`, `mix.lock`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`.
 194 3. Skip-by-pattern: minified bundles (`*.min.js|css|html|mjs|cjs`) and sourcemaps (`*.map`).
 195 4. Skip-by-extension. Binary or non-textual extensions: image (png, jpg, jpeg, gif, webp, ico, bmp, tif, tiff, svg), document (pdf, doc(x), ppt(x), xls(x)), archive (zip, tar, gz, bz2, xz, 7z, rar), audio/video (mp4, mp3, wav, ogg, mov, avi, webm, flac, aac, m4a), font (woff, woff2, ttf, otf, eot), compiled (exe, dll, so, dylib, a, o, obj, class, jar, war, ear, wasm, pyc, pyo, pyd), data (sqlite, sqlite3, db, dat, bin).
 196 5. Size cap: files larger than 1 MB are skipped (the GitHub tree response carries `size` in bytes per blob).
 197 
 198 Only tree nodes with `type === 'blob'` are considered — directories and submodule pointers are ignored before this filter even runs.
 199 ~~~
 200 
 201 ### Language Detection
 202 
 203 Each surviving file is tagged with a `language` label, derived purely from its filename. No content sniffing; this is a hackathon MVP and extension-based detection is sufficient.
 204 
 205 ~~~
 206 Language detection lives alongside the filter in `fileFilters.ts` as `detectLanguage(path)`.
 207 
 208 Special-cased filenames (no extension): `Dockerfile` and `Dockerfile.*` → `dockerfile`; `Makefile`, `GNUmakefile` → `makefile`; `Rakefile`, `Gemfile`, `Podfile` → `ruby`.
 209 
 210 Otherwise mapped by extension. Initial mapping covers the common languages a developer onboarding to a typical repo would encounter: typescript, javascript, python, ruby, go, rust, java, kotlin, swift, objective-c/cpp, c, cpp, csharp, fsharp, php, scala, clojure, elixir, erlang, elm, haskell, ocaml, dart, lua, r, perl, shell, powershell, sql, graphql, protobuf, vue, svelte, astro, html, css/scss/sass/less/stylus, markdown/mdx/restructuredtext/text, json/yaml/toml/ini/xml/csv.
 211 
 212 Unmapped extensions → `language: 'unknown'`. This is a deliberate fall-through, not an error condition; downstream agents can still read `chunkText` and reason about the file.
 213 ~~~
 214 
 215 ### `chunkAndEmbedFile` Placeholder
 216 Per-file chunk + embed + persist routine. Public signature unchanged from Step 2 (`chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`).
 217 
 218 ~~~
 219 Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.
 220 
 221 Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number, failedChunkCount: number }>`.
 222 
 223 Behavior:
 224   1. Drop existing `code_chunks` rows for `fileId` so re-ingestion replaces in place. (This is intra-file idempotency. The cascade-delete chain documented earlier handles the case where the entire `files` row is removed.) Implemented as a single `removeAll` with a bindings predicate, which compiles to one DELETE WHERE.
 225   2. Run the smart chunker (`chunkText`) over `rawContent`. Empty / whitespace-only files produce zero chunks and exit with `{ chunkCount: 0, failedChunkCount: 0 }`.
 226   3. Embed all chunks in a single batched call to `embedTexts`. Slots that fail to embed (after one rate-limit retry) come back as `null` and are skipped, not retried per-chunk.
 227   4. Build rows for `code_chunks`: `fileId`, `chunkText`, `chunkType` (classified per-chunk by `classifyChunkType`), `embedding`. Embeddings whose dimensionality differs from `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (1536 for text-embedding-3-small) are still persisted, but a `console.warn` is emitted so model swaps are visible.
 228   5. Persist via `db.batch` in groups of 100 mutations.
 229 
 230 Returns `{ chunkCount, failedChunkCount }` for diagnostic purposes; the ingestion driver aggregates these across files for its summary log line.
 231 ~~~
 232 
 233 ### Smart Chunker (`textChunker.ts`)
 234 
 235 A pragmatic chunker that splits source files and prose along logical boundaries, packs them into chunks of ≌4,000 characters, and preserves a 400-character overlap between adjacent chunks so context is not lost when a function or paragraph straddles a split.
 236 
 237 ~~~
 238 Location: `dist/methods/src/common/textChunker.ts`.
 239 
 240 Exports:
 241   - `chunkText(text: string, opts?: { maxChars?: number, overlap?: number }): string[]`
 242   - `classifyChunkType(content: string): 'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`
 243 
 244 Defaults: `maxChars = 4_000`, `overlap = 400` (10% of maxChars per the spec).
 245 
 246 Algorithm (in `chunkText`):
 247   1. Empty / whitespace-only input → `[]`.
 248   2. Input shorter than `maxChars` → `[text]` (single chunk, no work).
 249   3. Otherwise split into "blocks" along logical boundaries. The split regex matches:
 250      - Runs of blank lines (`\n\s*\n+`).
 251      - Lines starting (after optional indentation) with a declaration keyword common in code: `export`, `default`, `async`, `function`, `class`, `interface`, `type`, `enum`, `namespace`, `module`, `struct`, `impl`, `trait`, `fn`, `def`, `public`, `private`, `protected`, `static`.
 252      - Markdown headings (`#` through `######`).
 253      This is heuristic, NOT an AST. It is good enough that well-formatted code keeps function/class units together; it is not perfect on minified or oddly-formatted input.
 254   4. Greedy-pack blocks into chunks: append blocks to the current chunk until adding the next block would exceed `maxChars`. At that point, flush the current chunk, then seed a new chunk with the LAST `overlap` characters of the just-flushed chunk, followed by the new block. The seeded overlap is what preserves cross-chunk context.
 255   5. If a single block is itself larger than `maxChars` (e.g. a giant minified line, or a function with no internal blank lines), it goes through a sliding-window splitter with stride `maxChars - overlap`. This guarantees no chunk exceeds the cap, at the cost of arbitrary boundaries inside that block.
 256   6. Belt-and-suspenders: any chunk that somehow ends up over the cap gets a final pass through the sliding-window splitter.
 257 
 258 Note on the relaxed `chunkType` vocabulary: the Step 1 spec mentioned a more granular vocabulary (`function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, etc.) that would only be reachable with a true AST parser. Step 3 uses heuristic chunking, so the implemented vocabulary is the simpler `'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`. The schema column is still a free-form `string`, so widening the vocabulary later is a code change, not a schema change.
 259 
 260 `classifyChunkType` is line-anchored regex sniffing:
 261   - Counts code signals: declaration keywords at line starts.
 262   - Counts documentation signals: markdown headings, list bullets at line starts, blockquotes, inline `[text](url)` links.
 263   - Decision rule: if either side is at least 2x the other and non-zero, that wins. Otherwise `'mixed'`. Files with neither signal default to `'code'` (config files, plain text).
 264 ~~~
 265 
 266 ### Embedding Client (`openaiClient.ts`)
 267 
 268 MindStudio's SDK has no embedding action, so we call an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly. Default deployment points at OpenRouter; bare OpenAI also works.
 269 
 270 ~~~
 271 Location: `dist/methods/src/common/openaiClient.ts`.
 272 
 273 Exports:
 274   - `embedTexts(texts: string[]): Promise<Array<number[] | null>>`
 275   - `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (constant, currently `1536`)
 276 
 277 Secrets required (set in the Secrets tab):
 278   - `OPENAI_API_KEY` — credential for the embeddings endpoint.
 279   - `OPENAI_BASE_URL` — e.g. `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`. If unset, the SDK falls back to OpenAI's default base URL, which lets a developer point straight at OpenAI without code changes.
 280 
 281 Model: `openai/text-embedding-3-small`. The `openai/` prefix is required for OpenRouter routing. When `OPENAI_BASE_URL` is empty or contains `api.openai.com` we strip the prefix automatically so bare OpenAI (which expects unprefixed model IDs) also works.
 282 
 283 NPM dependency: `openai@^6.x` in `dist/methods/package.json`. The pre-v5 SDK pulls in `node-fetch`, which uses dynamic `require('stream')` and does not survive the methods bundler. v6 uses native `fetch` and bundles cleanly.
 284 
 285 Resilience:
 286   - One batched request per call. The OpenAI / OpenRouter embeddings endpoint accepts arrays of inputs and returns one vector per input in order.
 287   - On HTTP 429 or `code === 'rate_limit_exceeded'`, sleep ~2 seconds and retry the entire batch ONCE. If it still fails, every slot in the batch is returned as `null`.
 288   - On any other error, log via `console.error` and return all-`null`. The caller (`chunkAndEmbedFile`) skips null slots, persists the rest, and reports `failedChunkCount` so the operator can see partial failures in the logs.
 289   - Empty / whitespace-only input strings are filtered out client-side (the API rejects them) and their slots return `null`.
 290   - The OpenAI client is instantiated once per process and cached in `cachedClient` to avoid per-call connection overhead.
 291 ~~~
 292 
 293 ### Re-ingestion Semantics
 294 
 295 Ingesting the same `githubUrl` twice is supported and idempotent at the row level:
 296 
 297 - `repositories` upserts on `githubUrl`, so the row's `id` is stable across runs. `lastScannedAt` is overwritten with the new timestamp.
 298 - `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)`, so file IDs are stable for paths that still exist in the repo. The `language` label is recomputed in case the extension mapping has been updated.
 299 
 300 What re-ingestion does **not** do in Step 2:
 301 
 302 - It does not delete `files` rows whose paths have been removed from the repo since the last scan. Stale rows linger. A "prune deleted files (and cascade-delete their chunks/docs)" pass is deferred to a later step — the cascading-delete chain documented earlier in this spec is what that pass will use.
 303 - It does not detect renames. A renamed file appears as a new `files` row with the new path; the old path remains as a stale row.
 304 
 305 ~~~
 306 Step 3 prerequisite (flagged for early visibility, not implemented in Step 2):
 307 
 308 Once `chunkAndEmbedFile` does real work, the per-file cost dominates ingestion time. Hundreds of files × (raw-content fetch + AST parse + per-chunk embedding) will exceed any reasonable method timeout. Step 3 will need to:
 309   - Add a `status` column to `repositories` (e.g. `idle | scanning | ready | failed`).
 310   - Convert `ingestRepository` to a fire-and-forget shape: persist the repo + file rows synchronously, return immediately with `repositoryId` and `status: 'scanning'`, and run the chunking + embedding loop in a background promise that updates `status` on completion or failure.
 311 
 312 The shape is documented in the platform's "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" pattern.
 313 ~~~
 314 
 315 ## Out of Scope (Tracked for Later Steps)
 316 - Step 4: Mapper and Deep-Dive agent prompts, models, and orchestration (`generateBible(repoId)` method).
 317 - Pruning of stale `files` rows on re-ingestion (and the cascading-delete pass that comes with it).
 318 - Concurrency in the per-file processing loop (currently serial).
 319 - Web interface, navigation, flowchart rendering.
 320 - Auth, multi-user concerns, per-repository ownership.
 321 - Scenarios and seed data.
 322

content.thinking:
I'm realizing the issue now—the empty-heading replacement inserted content before the first heading, but the original YAML frontmatter and prose that came before it are still duplicated in the file. The replacement targeted the space before `# Codebase Bible`, which means the old content from lines 17-28 never got removed, creating the duplication.

Looking at the structure, I also see that the "Re-ingestion Semantics" section still has both the new content I added and the old outdated framing mixed together. Rather than continuing to patch this with partial edits, I should just rewrite the entire file from scratch using `writeSpec` to get a clean structure.

content.text: The MSFM "preamble" replace inserted my new content before the H1 but didn't clear the old prose underneath, and the deletion of the old "Re-ingestion Semantics" section never landed. Easier to just rewrite the whole file cleanly.

content.name: writeSpec

input.content:
---
name: Codebase Bible
description: A RAG-based engine that ingests GitHub repositories, analyzes them with AI agents, and produces an interactive onboarding guide for new developers.
---

# Codebase Bible

An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app fetches the repo's file tree, breaks each source file into semantically meaningful chunks (function/class/declaration boundaries plus paragraph boundaries), embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context from the rest of the repo to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible: navigable markdown documentation with embedded flowcharts, designed to onboard new developers quickly.

~~~
Build proceeds strictly step-by-step under user direction. This file currently covers Steps 1–3:
  - Step 1: data model.
  - Step 2: GitHub ingestion (URL parsing, tree fetch, filtering, file persistence).
  - Step 3: async architecture, smart chunking, OpenAI-compatible embeddings, persistence.
Methods for the agent layer (Mapper, Deep-Dive) and the web UI are deliberately out of scope until subsequent steps are unlocked by the user.

Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
- The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code (the methods that perform deletes), not via SQL constraints. This is the platform contract; it does not change the logical data model.
- Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation will compute cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store (Pinecone, Turbopuffer, etc.) with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID. The interface column shape stays the same either way.
- The MindStudio SDK does NOT have an embedding action. Embeddings are generated by calling an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package, configured to point at OpenRouter (or bare OpenAI, or any other compatible provider) via the `OPENAI_BASE_URL` secret.
- All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
~~~

## Data Model

The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.

~~~
Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):

  delete repository
    → delete all files where file.repo_id = repository.id
      → delete all code_chunks where chunk.file_id IN (those file ids)
      → delete all generated_docs where doc.file_id IN (those file ids)

A shared helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) should own this so any method that removes a repo, or re-ingests one, calls a single function. Same shape applies for cascading delete of a single file.

All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
~~~

### Repositories

Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Includes a lifecycle status because ingestion is asynchronous.

~~~
Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
- `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
- `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`). Stored separately so the UI doesn't re-parse on every render.
- `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type alias) — lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. Values:
    - `pending` — row exists, ingestion not yet started. Reserved for a future queueing layer; not actively written today.
    - `processing` — ingestion is currently running in the background.
    - `completed` — last run finished successfully.
    - `failed` — last run crashed; details in method logs (`console.error`).
- `lastScannedAt` (number, unix ms, optional) — timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run. `undefined` until the first run reaches `status === 'completed'`. Deliberately NOT bumped on transitions to `processing` or `failed` — the column answers "when did we last have a complete picture of this repo?", not "when did we last touch the row?". Use the auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.

Unique constraint: `[['githubUrl']]`. Required so re-ingestion can `upsert` on URL instead of duplicating rows.
~~~

### Files

One row per source file discovered inside a repository during ingestion. Owned by exactly one repository.

~~~
Table name: `files`. Columns:
- `repoId` (string, FK → `repositories.id`) — owning repository. Required.
- `filePath` (string) — path relative to the repo root, e.g. `src/lib/auth.ts`. Stored with forward slashes regardless of source OS.
- `language` (string) — detected language label (e.g. `typescript`, `python`, `go`, `markdown`, `unknown`). Free-form string in this step; a controlled vocabulary can be introduced later if needed.

The TypeScript interface for this table is named `RepoFile` (not `File`) to avoid colliding with the global DOM `File` type. The exported table object is still `Files`.

Unique constraint: `[['repoId', 'filePath']]` — a given path appears at most once per repo. Allows re-ingestion to upsert rather than duplicate.

Cascading delete: when a repository is removed, all files with matching `repoId` are removed in the same batch.
~~~

### Code Chunks

Semantic units extracted from a file by the chunker. A single file produces one or more chunks. Each chunk carries its text, a coarse type label, and an embedding vector for retrieval.

~~~
Table name: `code_chunks`. Columns:
- `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — owning file. Required.
- `chunkText` (string) — the exact source text of the chunk, preserved verbatim (whitespace and all) so retrieval results can be shown to the user as-is.
- `chunkType` (string) — coarse category. Implemented vocabulary in Step 3: `code`, `documentation`, `mixed`. The Step 1 spec mentioned a more granular vocabulary (`function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, `type`, `import`, `boilerplate`, `comment`, `other`) that would only be reachable with a true AST parser; we use heuristic chunking instead. Free-form string at the column level so widening the vocabulary later (e.g. when an AST pass is added) is a code change, not a schema change.
- `embedding` (number[], JSON-serialized) — embedding vector for semantic search. Stored as a JSON array of floats. Length is whatever the chosen embedding model produces (1536 for OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`); the column is not length-constrained at the schema level so the embedding model can be swapped without a migration.

No unique constraint. A file can produce many chunks, and identical chunk text can legitimately recur (boilerplate, repeated imports). De-duplication, if desired, is a pipeline concern, not a schema constraint.

Cascading delete: when a file is removed (directly, or transitively via its repository), all chunks with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.

Embedding-search note: the initial query path loads candidate chunks via SQL filters (e.g. by `repoId` joined through `files`) and computes cosine similarity in the method process. Acceptable up to roughly ten-thousand chunks per query scope; above that, plan to migrate to an external vector index. The column shape does not change in either world.
~~~

### Generated Docs

Markdown documentation produced by the AI agents for a given file. Multiple rows per file are allowed and intentional, so the history of generated docs is preserved across re-runs.

~~~
Table name: `generated_docs`. Columns:
- `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — file the doc describes. Required.
- `markdownContent` (string) — the full markdown body produced by the Deep-Dive agent (or, in some flows, the Mapper). May embed Mermaid flowchart blocks; rendering is a frontend concern. Stored as-is.

Deliberately NOT declared on this table: a `createdAt` column. The platform provides a system `created_at` (unix ms) on every row, which is what the UI reads for "generated on …" values and what queries sort by. Adding a separate `createdAt` would create a confusing duplicate. The user-facing data contract for creation timestamp is satisfied by `doc.created_at`.

No unique constraint. Multiple generations per file are valid — re-running the agents must not destroy previous output. "Latest doc for a file" is a query (`sortBy(d => d.created_at).reverse().take(1)`), not a uniqueness rule.

Cascading delete: when a file is removed, all generated_docs with matching `fileId` are removed in the same batch.
~~~

## Ingestion

Ingestion is the process of pointing the app at a GitHub repository and turning it into rows in our database that downstream agents can read. It runs asynchronously: a synchronous validation phase returns to the caller immediately with a `processing` status, and a background phase finishes the heavy lifting (file tree fetch, raw content fetch, chunking, embedding, persistence).

### `ingestRepository` Method

The single public method for ingestion. The caller provides a GitHub URL; the method validates synchronously and kicks off the heavy work in a background task.

~~~
Manifest entry:
  id: `ingest-repository`
  path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
  export: `ingestRepository`

Input:
  `{ githubUrl: string }`

Output (returned synchronously):
  `{ repositoryId: string, repoName: string, defaultBranch: string, status: 'processing' }`

The method runs unauthenticated for the MVP. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
~~~

The method runs in two phases:

**Phase A — synchronous validation and row creation.** Returned to the caller as soon as it completes. Bad URLs and missing repositories produce errors here, before any background work is started.

1. **Parse the URL.** Accept `https://github.com/owner/name`, the same with `.git` suffix or trailing path (e.g. `/tree/main`), and `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalize to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so re-ingestion of variants of the same URL collapses onto one row.
2. **Fetch repository metadata** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}` to confirm the repo exists and learn the [default branch]{The branch name (e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`) the repo serves as its tip. We always ingest the default branch in Step 3; arbitrary-branch ingestion is a later step.}.
3. **Upsert the `repositories` row** keyed on `githubUrl`. Sets `repoName` to `owner/name` and `status` to `'processing'`. `lastScannedAt` is intentionally not touched yet — it will be set only when (and if) the background phase reaches `'completed'`.
4. **Return** `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, status: 'processing' }` to the caller.

**Phase B — fire-and-forget background ingestion.** Begins after Phase A returns. Uses the platform's [un-awaited promise pattern]{The execution environment persists between requests, so a promise that is not awaited continues running after the method returns. See "Fire-and-Forget Background Tasks" in the platform docs.}.

5. **Fetch the recursive file tree** from `GET /repos/{owner}/{name}/git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1`.
6. **Filter** the tree to text-only blobs worth indexing (see "Filtering Rules" below).
7. **Upsert each surviving file row** into `files`, keyed on `(repoId, filePath)`. Re-ingestion updates rows in place rather than creating duplicates, which keeps file IDs stable across runs (important so chunks from previous runs can be cleanly replaced in step 8).
8. **Per file**, fetch raw content from `raw.githubusercontent.com/{owner}/{repo}/{branch}/{path}` (with `GITHUB_TOKEN` Bearer if configured) and pass it to `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId, rawContent)`. Per-file failures (network blips, transient 5xx, unicode trouble) are logged and skipped — a single bad file does not abort the whole run.
9. **Mark the repository `'completed'`** and write a fresh `lastScannedAt`. On any unhandled error in steps 5–9, the row is marked `'failed'` instead and the error detail is in `console.error`.

~~~
GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Uses native global `fetch` (Node 18+ runtime).

Authentication is optional. If the secret `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set in `process.env`, it is sent as a Bearer token on both the REST API requests and the `raw.githubusercontent.com` requests. Without it, requests are unauthenticated and subject to GitHub's 60-requests-per-hour-per-IP limit.

GitHub-specific status codes get user-friendly error mapping (only relevant for Phase A; Phase B logs and continues):
  - 404 → "Repository not found. Make sure the URL is correct and the repository is public (or that GITHUB_TOKEN is configured for private access)."
  - 403 / 429 → "GitHub API rate limit reached. Configure a GITHUB_TOKEN secret to raise the limit, then try again."
  - other non-2xx → "GitHub API request failed (status). Check the logs for details." with the raw response body in `console.error`.

Required headers on every request: `Accept: application/vnd.github+json`, `X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28`, `User-Agent: codebase-bible-ingestor`.
~~~

### Background Task Concurrency

Files are processed serially inside the background task. Per file: fetch raw content, chunk, embed (one batched API call), persist. Files do not run in parallel today.

~~~
Serial processing is a conscious choice for the MVP:
  - Embedding cost is dominated by the per-call round trip, not the per-chunk cost. Batching all of one file's chunks into a single embedding call (which we already do) captures most of the available speedup.
  - Adding parallelism across files introduces concurrency-limit bookkeeping and complicates rate-limit handling.

If throughput becomes a problem on large repos, the natural upgrade is a small concurrency pool (e.g. 4-wide) around the per-file loop in `runIngestion`.
~~~

### Filtering Rules

Not every file in a repo is worth indexing. The filter has four layers, applied in order; the first that matches discards the file.

~~~
Filter implementation lives in `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts`. All sets are exported as `const` at the top of the file so the rule set is easy to audit and amend without rummaging through logic.

1. Boilerplate directory segments. If any segment of the path matches one of these, the file is dropped. Initial set:
   `node_modules`, `.git`, `.github`, `.svn`, `.hg`, `dist`, `build`, `out`,
   `.next`, `.nuxt`, `.svelte-kit`, `.astro`, `.expo`, `.output`, `target`,
   `vendor`, `__pycache__`, `.venv`, `venv`, `.idea`, `.vscode`, `.vs`,
   `coverage`, `.nyc_output`, `.cache`, `.parcel-cache`, `.turbo`,
   `.vercel`, `.pnpm-store`, `bower_components`, `jspm_packages`, `Pods`,
   `DerivedData`.
2. Skip-by-filename (exact match on the basename). Lockfiles and OS metadata:
   `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`,
   `Cargo.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `composer.lock`,
   `Pipfile.lock`, `Podfile.lock`, `mix.lock`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`.
3. Skip-by-pattern: minified bundles (`*.min.js|css|html|mjs|cjs`) and sourcemaps (`*.map`).
4. Skip-by-extension. Binary or non-textual extensions: image (png, jpg, jpeg, gif, webp, ico, bmp, tif, tiff, svg), document (pdf, doc(x), ppt(x), xls(x)), archive (zip, tar, gz, bz2, xz, 7z, rar), audio/video (mp4, mp3, wav, ogg, mov, avi, webm, flac, aac, m4a), font (woff, woff2, ttf, otf, eot), compiled (exe, dll, so, dylib, a, o, obj, class, jar, war, ear, wasm, pyc, pyo, pyd), data (sqlite, sqlite3, db, dat, bin).
5. Size cap: files larger than 1 MB are skipped (the GitHub tree response carries `size` in bytes per blob).

Only tree nodes with `type === 'blob'` are considered — directories and submodule pointers are ignored before this filter even runs.
~~~

### Language Detection

Each surviving file is tagged with a `language` label, derived purely from its filename. No content sniffing; this is a hackathon MVP and extension-based detection is sufficient.

~~~
Language detection lives alongside the filter in `fileFilters.ts` as `detectLanguage(path)`.

Special-cased filenames (no extension): `Dockerfile` and `Dockerfile.*` → `dockerfile`; `Makefile`, `GNUmakefile` → `makefile`; `Rakefile`, `Gemfile`, `Podfile` → `ruby`.

Otherwise mapped by extension. Initial mapping covers the common languages a developer onboarding to a typical repo would encounter: typescript, javascript, python, ruby, go, rust, java, kotlin, swift, objective-c/cpp, c, cpp, csharp, fsharp, php, scala, clojure, elixir, erlang, elm, haskell, ocaml, dart, lua, r, perl, shell, powershell, sql, graphql, protobuf, vue, svelte, astro, html, css/scss/sass/less/stylus, markdown/mdx/restructuredtext/text, json/yaml/toml/ini/xml/csv.

Unmapped extensions → `language: 'unknown'`. This is a deliberate fall-through, not an error condition; downstream agents can still read `chunkText` and reason about the file.
~~~

### `chunkAndEmbedFile`

Per-file chunk + embed + persist routine. Public signature unchanged from the Step 2 placeholder so the call site in `ingestRepository` does not move.

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.

Signature: `chunkAndEmbedFile(fileId: string, rawContent: string): Promise<{ chunkCount: number, failedChunkCount: number }>`.

Behavior:
  1. Drop existing `code_chunks` rows for `fileId` so re-ingestion replaces in place. (This is intra-file idempotency. The cascade-delete chain documented earlier handles the case where the entire `files` row is removed.) Implemented as a single `removeAll` with a bindings predicate, which compiles to one DELETE WHERE.
  2. Run the smart chunker (`chunkText`) over `rawContent`. Empty / whitespace-only files produce zero chunks and exit with `{ chunkCount: 0, failedChunkCount: 0 }`.
  3. Embed all chunks in a single batched call to `embedTexts`. Slots that fail to embed (after one rate-limit retry) come back as `null` and are skipped, not retried per-chunk.
  4. Build rows for `code_chunks`: `fileId`, `chunkText`, `chunkType` (classified per-chunk by `classifyChunkType`), `embedding`. Embeddings whose dimensionality differs from `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (1536 for text-embedding-3-small) are still persisted, but a `console.warn` is emitted so model swaps are visible.
  5. Persist via `db.batch` in groups of 100 mutations.

Returns `{ chunkCount, failedChunkCount }` for diagnostic purposes; the ingestion driver aggregates these across files for its summary log line.
~~~

### Smart Chunker

A pragmatic chunker that splits source files and prose along logical boundaries, packs them into chunks of ≌4,000 characters, and preserves a 400-character overlap between adjacent chunks so context is not lost when a function or paragraph straddles a split.

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/textChunker.ts`.

Exports:
  - `chunkText(text: string, opts?: { maxChars?: number, overlap?: number }): string[]`
  - `classifyChunkType(content: string): 'code' | 'documentation' | 'mixed'`

Defaults: `maxChars = 4_000`, `overlap = 400` (10% of maxChars per the spec).

Algorithm (in `chunkText`):
  1. Empty / whitespace-only input → `[]`.
  2. Input shorter than `maxChars` → `[text]` (single chunk, no work).
  3. Otherwise split into "blocks" along logical boundaries. The split regex matches:
     - Runs of blank lines (`\n\s*\n+`).
     - Lines starting (after optional indentation) with a declaration keyword common in code: `export`, `default`, `async`, `function`, `class`, `interface`, `type`, `enum`, `namespace`, `module`, `struct`, `impl`, `trait`, `fn`, `def`, `public`, `private`, `protected`, `static`.
     - Markdown headings (`#` through `######`).
     This is heuristic, NOT an AST. It is good enough that well-formatted code keeps function/class units together; it is not perfect on minified or oddly-formatted input.
  4. Greedy-pack blocks into chunks: append blocks to the current chunk until adding the next block would exceed `maxChars`. At that point, flush the current chunk, then seed a new chunk with the LAST `overlap` characters of the just-flushed chunk, followed by the new block. The seeded overlap is what preserves cross-chunk context.
  5. If a single block is itself larger than `maxChars` (e.g. a giant minified line, or a function with no internal blank lines), it goes through a sliding-window splitter with stride `maxChars - overlap`. This guarantees no chunk exceeds the cap, at the cost of arbitrary boundaries inside that block.
  6. Belt-and-suspenders: any chunk that somehow ends up over the cap gets a final pass through the sliding-window splitter.

`classifyChunkType` is line-anchored regex sniffing:
  - Counts code signals: declaration keywords at line starts.
  - Counts documentation signals: markdown headings, list bullets at line starts, blockquotes, inline `[text](url)` links.
  - Decision rule: if either side is at least 2x the other and non-zero, that wins. Otherwise `'mixed'`. Files with neither signal default to `'code'` (config files, plain text).
~~~

### Embedding Client

MindStudio's SDK has no embedding action, so we call an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package. Default deployment points at OpenRouter; bare OpenAI also works.

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/openaiClient.ts`.

Exports:
  - `embedTexts(texts: string[]): Promise<Array<number[] | null>>`
  - `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` (constant, currently `1536`)

Secrets required (set in the Secrets tab):
  - `OPENAI_API_KEY` — credential for the embeddings endpoint.
  - `OPENAI_BASE_URL` — e.g. `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`. If unset, the SDK falls back to OpenAI's default base URL, which lets a developer point straight at OpenAI without code changes.

Model: `openai/text-embedding-3-small`. The `openai/` prefix is required for OpenRouter routing. When `OPENAI_BASE_URL` is empty or contains `api.openai.com` we strip the prefix automatically so bare OpenAI (which expects unprefixed model IDs) also works.
