## 1. File Purpose

This file is the sole runtime entry point for the `is-plain-obj` library. It exports a single default function, `isPlainObject`, that determines whether a given value qualifies as a plain JavaScript object. A plain object is defined here as one whose prototype chain satisfies specific structural constraints and which does not carry certain well-known symbols that would indicate a specialized or custom object type.

## 2. Architecture and Design Patterns

This file follows the micro-library pattern: one exported function, no state, no side effects, no dependencies. It is a pure predicate function implementing a strategy for type discrimination. There are no design patterns beyond a straightforward guard-clause structure. Within the broader architecture, this file is the entire runtime surface area; all other files (`index.d.ts`, `test.js`, `benchmark.js`) exist to describe, validate, or measure it.

## 3. Public Interface

```js
export default function isPlainObject(value: unknown): boolean
```

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `value` | any | The value to test |

**Returns:** `boolean`. `true` if `value` is a plain object by the criteria implemented in this file; `false` otherwise.

## 4. Internal Logic Walkthrough

The function applies two sequential checks.

**Check 1: Primitive and null guard**

```js
if (typeof value !== 'object' || value === null) {
    return false;
}
```

`typeof null === 'object'` is a well-known JavaScript quirk, so `null` must be explicitly excluded. Any primitive (`string`, `number`, `boolean`, `symbol`, `bigint`, `undefined`) or `null` causes an immediate `false` return.

**Check 2: Prototype chain and symbol checks**

```js
const prototype = Object.getPrototypeOf(value);
return (prototype === null || prototype === Object.prototype || Object.getPrototypeOf(prototype) === null) && !(Symbol.toStringTag in value) && !(Symbol.iterator in value);
```

This single return expression is a conjunction of three independent sub-conditions, all of which must be true.

**Sub-condition A: Prototype chain shape**

```js
prototype === null || prototype === Object.prototype || Object.getPrototypeOf(prototype) === null
```

This accepts three prototype configurations:

- `prototype === null`: Objects created via `Object.create(null)`, which have no prototype at all.
- `prototype === Object.prototype`: Standard objects created via object literals `{}` or `new Object()`.
- `Object.getPrototypeOf(prototype) === null`: Objects whose direct prototype is itself a root-level object (i.e., its prototype's prototype is `null`). This handles the case where a constructor's `prototype` object itself has a `null` prototype, meaning the object sits one level above a null-prototype root without being a direct `Object.prototype` descendant. This accommodates certain cross-realm or manually constructed prototype chains that are still considered "plain."

**Sub-condition B: No `Symbol.toStringTag`**

```js
!(Symbol.toStringTag in value)
```

`Symbol.toStringTag` is used by built-in objects (e.g., `Map`, `Set`, `Promise`, typed arrays) and custom classes to override the string representation returned by `Object.prototype.toString`. Its presence signals that the object is a specialized type, not a plain data container.

**Sub-condition C: No `Symbol.iterator`**

```js
!(Symbol.iterator in value)
```

`Symbol.iterator` marks an object as iterable (arrays, generators, `Map`, `Set`, custom iterables). Plain objects are not iterable by default, so its presence disqualifies the value.

The `in` operator is used for both symbol checks rather than `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty`, meaning the check covers inherited symbols as well as own symbols.

## 5. Dependencies and Integrations

This file has no imports. It relies exclusively on built-in JavaScript language features:

| Feature | Role |
|---|---|
| `typeof` | Primitive type discrimination |
| `Object.getPrototypeOf` | Prototype chain inspection |
| `Object.prototype` | Reference point for standard object prototype |
| `Symbol.toStringTag` | Well-known symbol for custom string tag detection |
| `Symbol.iterator` | Well-known symbol for iterable protocol detection |
| `in` operator | Prototype-chain-inclusive property existence check |

## 6. Edge Cases and Error Handling

There is no exception throwing in this function. All inputs are handled by returning `false` for non-qualifying values.

| Input | Result | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `null` | `false` | Explicit null check after `typeof` guard |
| Primitives (`42`, `"str"`, `true`) | `false` | `typeof value !== 'object'` |
| `[]` | `false` | Arrays carry `Symbol.iterator` |
| `new Map()` | `false` | `Map` carries both `Symbol.toStringTag` and `Symbol.iterator` |
| `new Set()` | `false` | Same as `Map` |
| Class instances with custom `Symbol.toStringTag` | `false` | Sub-condition B rejects them |
| `Object.create(null)` | `true` | `prototype === null` satisfies sub-condition A; no well-known symbols present by default |
| `{}` | `true` | `prototype === Object.prototype`; no well-known symbols |
| Objects with `Symbol.iterator` added manually | `false` | Sub-condition C rejects them regardless of prototype |

The `in` operator in the symbol checks means that if a plain-looking object inherits `Symbol.toStringTag` or `Symbol.iterator` from somewhere in its prototype chain, it will still be rejected. This is a deliberate conservative stance: any object that behaves like a specialized type via inherited symbols is not considered plain.

## 7. Observations

The third prototype condition `Object.getPrototypeOf(prototype) === null` is non-obvious. It does not match `Object.prototype` itself (whose prototype is `null`) because that case would require `value` to be `Object.prototype` directly, which is an unusual input. More practically, it accommodates objects whose prototype is a custom root object (created via `Object.create(null)`), making the function tolerant of certain non-standard but structurally simple inheritance chains. This broadens the definition of "plain" beyond what a strict `prototype === Object.prototype || prototype === null` check would allow, and the rationale is not documented inline.

The use of `in` rather than `hasOwnProperty` for symbol checks means a subclass that inherits `Symbol.toStringTag` from a parent will be rejected even if the instance itself does not own the symbol. This is intentional for correctness but could surprise callers who define a class with `Symbol.toStringTag` on the prototype and expect instances to be treated as plain objects.","createdAt":1777776567000},"cfcb85a7-13dc-4b0b-b975-c0eda14cd1ef":{"id":"3ee9730a-ad92-4cc8-a39f-80d565083166","fileId":"cfcb85a7-13dc-4b0b-b975-c0eda14cd1ef","markdownContent":"# benchmark.js

## 1. File Purpose

This file benchmarks the runtime performance of the `isPlainObject` function exported from `index.js`. It measures how long the function takes to process each of a representative set of input values by running each value through 100 million iterations and reporting elapsed time via `console.time`/`console.timeEnd`. It also performs a V8 JIT warm-up pass before the timed runs to ensure the engine has had the opportunity to optimize the hot path.

## 2. Architecture and Design Patterns

This file follows a simple procedural benchmark harness pattern. There is no framework or benchmark library involved; it relies entirely on Node.js built-ins (`node:util`, `node:vm`) and the native `console.time` API. It fits into the broader architecture as a development-only quality concern, exercising `index.js` from the outside exactly as an external caller would, with no special access to internals.

The design separates concerns into two functions:

- `runLoop`: the tight inner loop that isolates the cost of repeated calls to `isPlainObject`.
- `runBenchmarks`: the outer loop that iterates over the value set, formats labels, and manages timing.

This separation ensures the timing instrumentation does not interfere with the measured loop body.

## 3. Public Interface

This file exports nothing. It is a standalone executable script. All logic is invoked at module evaluation time via the final two statements:

```js
runLoop(runInNewContext('({})'));
runBenchmarks();
```

## 4. Internal Logic Walkthrough

### Value Set Construction

The `values` array enumerates a broad cross-section of JavaScript value types, covering every major branch the `isPlainObject` implementation is expected to handle:

```js
const values = [
	undefined,
	0,
	0n,
	'',
	true,
	Symbol(''),
	() => {},
	(function namedFunction() {}),
	null,
	{},
	Math,
	new Set([]),
	new ArrayBuffer(0),
	Promise.resolve(),
	Object.create(null),
	new Intl.Locale('en'),
	new Object({prop: true}),
	new class Class {}(),
	[],
	/regexp/,
	new Error('test'),
	new Date(),
	(function () {
		return arguments;
	})(),
	new Proxy({}, {}),
];
```

This covers primitives (`undefined`, `0`, `0n`, `''`, `true`, `Symbol('')`), functions, `null`, plain objects (`{}`, `Object.create(null)`, `new Object({prop: true})`), built-in exotic objects (`Math`, `Set`, `ArrayBuffer`, `Promise`, `Intl.Locale`, `Array`, `RegExp`, `Error`, `Date`, `arguments`, `Proxy`), and a user-defined class instance. The breadth ensures the benchmark reflects realistic call-site diversity rather than a single optimistic case.

### Label Formatting

Each value is formatted into a fixed-width label for aligned console output:

```js
const name = value instanceof Error ? String(Error) : inspect(value);
const paddedName = name.padEnd(50);
```

`Error` instances receive special treatment because `inspect` on an `Error` can produce multi-line output. `String(Error)` produces the string `"function Error() { [native code] }"`, which is compact and single-line. All other values are formatted with `node:util`'s `inspect`. The label is then padded to 50 characters so that `console.timeEnd` output columns align visually.

### Inner Loop

```js
const runLoop = value => {
	for (let index = 0; index < 1e8; index += 1) {
		isPlainObject(value);
	}
};
```

Each call to `runLoop` invokes `isPlainObject` exactly 100,000,000 times with the same value. The return value is discarded. The loop variable increments by 1 rather than using `++` to avoid a common lint rule violation in the sindresorhus style.

### V8 Warm-Up

```js
runLoop(runInNewContext('({})'));
```

This runs before `runBenchmarks()`. `runInNewContext('({})')` evaluates the expression `({})` in a fresh V8 context (a separate realm), producing a plain object whose prototype is the `Object` from that realm rather than the current realm's `Object`. This is significant because `isPlainObject` must handle cross-realm objects, which exercises a distinct code path from same-realm plain objects. The comment makes the intent explicit:

```js
// Warm up V8 optimization.
// Must go through every branch of the code, which requires using an object from a different realm.
```

By running 100 million iterations of this cross-realm case before timing begins, the warm-up ensures V8 has JIT-compiled all branches of `isPlainObject` before any timed measurement starts, preventing the first few benchmarked values from being penalized by interpreter or baseline-compiler overhead.

## 5. Dependencies and Integrations

| Import | Source | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `inspect` | `node:util` | Formats arbitrary JavaScript values into human-readable strings for benchmark labels |
| `runInNewContext` | `node:vm` | Evaluates a JavaScript expression in a separate V8 context to produce a cross-realm object for the warm-up pass |
| `isPlainObject` | `is-plain-obj` | The function under benchmark; this is the default export from `index.js` (implementation not in scope from this file) |

## 6. Edge Cases and Error Handling

There is no error handling in this file. It is a development script expected to run in a controlled environment.

The one notable edge case addressed is the `Error` instance label:

```js
const name = value instanceof Error ? String(Error) : inspect(value);
```

This guards against `inspect` producing multi-line or excessively long output for `Error` instances, which would break the visual alignment of the timed output. Note that `String(Error)` (the constructor function, not the instance) is used rather than `String(value)` or `inspect(value)` -- this produces a consistent, compact string regardless of the error message.

## 7. Observations

The label for `Error` instances uses `String(Error)` (the constructor) rather than `String(value)` (the instance). This means all `Error` instances in the `values` array would share the same label, which would cause `console.time`/`console.timeEnd` to collide if more than one `Error` were present. Currently only one `Error` is in the array, so this is not a practical problem, but it is a latent bug if the value set is extended.

The warm-up call uses `runInNewContext('({})')` to exercise the cross-realm branch, but the timed benchmark loop does not include a cross-realm object in `values`. If the cross-realm path has meaningfully different performance characteristics, that difference is not captured in the timed output.","createdAt":1777776567000},"93af5955-7ac9-416c-8abe-ef4edede098e":{"id":"2eb04983-01e6-4f27-941e-d39b96412b1a","fileId":"93af5955-7ac9-416c-8abe-ef4edede098e","markdownContent":"# is-plain-obj: readme.md

### 1. File Purpose

This file is the public-facing documentation for the `is-plain-obj` npm package. It serves as the primary reference for external consumers, covering installation, the definition of "plain object" as used by the library, usage examples with expected return values, and links to related packages in the sindresorhus ecosystem.

### 2. Architecture and Design Patterns

The readme follows the standard micro-library documentation pattern common in the sindresorhus ecosystem. It is structured as a single-page reference document with no subsections beyond install, usage, and related packages. This mirrors the flat, single-entry-point architecture of the library itself: one function, one page of documentation. The document does not describe internal implementation details, configuration, or error handling, which is consistent with the library's stateless, zero-dependency nature.

### 3. Public Interface

The readme documents one exported function, referenced by the import alias `isPlainObject`:

```js
import isPlainObject from 'is-plain-obj';
```

The function accepts a single value and returns a boolean. No parameter types or return type annotations are stated in the readme itself; those are deferred to `index.d.ts`.

### 4. Internal Logic Walkthrough

The readme defines the plain-object criterion explicitly:

> An object is plain if it's created by either `{}`, `new Object()`, or `Object.create(null)`.

The usage examples enumerate the cases the function handles:

**Returns `true`:**

```js
isPlainObject({foo: 'bar'});
//=> true

isPlainObject(new Object());
//=> true

isPlainObject(Object.create(null));
//=> true

// This works across realms
isPlainObject(runInNewContext('({})'));
//=> true
```

**Returns `false`:**

```js
isPlainObject([1, 2, 3]);
//=> false

class Unicorn {}
isPlainObject(new Unicorn());
//=> false

isPlainObject(Math);
//=> false
```

The cross-realm example is the only non-obvious behavioral claim in the document. It uses `node:vm`'s `runInNewContext` to construct an object in a separate JavaScript realm, where the object's prototype chain originates from a different `Object` constructor than the one in the calling realm. The comment asserts the function handles this correctly, which implies the implementation does not rely on a simple `instanceof Object` or `=== Object.prototype` check against the local realm's `Object`.

### 5. Dependencies and Integrations

The readme references one external module in its usage example:

```js
import {runInNewContext} from 'node:vm';
```

`node:vm` is a Node.js built-in module that provides APIs for compiling and running code within V8 virtual machine contexts. It is used here solely to demonstrate cross-realm behavior in the documentation example, not as a runtime dependency of the library itself.

### 6. Edge Cases and Error Handling

The readme surfaces three non-trivial edge cases through its examples:

- **`Object.create(null)`**: An object with a `null` prototype, which has no `Object.prototype` in its chain, is explicitly classified as plain.
- **Cross-realm objects**: Objects created in a separate VM context are classified as plain, indicating the implementation accounts for realm boundaries.
- **Built-in namespace objects**: `Math` is given as an example of a non-plain object, distinguishing plain objects from other object-typed values that are not user-constructed data containers.

### 7. Observations

The readme uses the import alias `isPlainObject` in its examples, while the package name and architecture context refer to the function as `isPlainObj`. Consumers copying the import statement verbatim will have a local binding named `isPlainObject`, but the actual export name from `index.js` cannot be confirmed from this file alone. This is a minor documentation inconsistency worth noting for maintainers.","createdAt":1777776567000},"21ca3b87-2a6b-4ab0-8c9a-0e492f5b3aa2":{"id":"7d4b60b9-01b1-4c04-9409-559273b5510f","fileId":"21ca3b87-2a6b-4ab0-8c9a-0e492f5b3aa2","markdownContent":"# package.json — Package Manifest for `is-plain-obj`

## 1. File Purpose

This file is the npm package manifest for `is-plain-obj`, a single-purpose utility library that checks whether a value is a plain JavaScript object. It defines the package identity, distribution metadata, entry point resolution, runtime engine requirements, published file set, and the development toolchain used for linting, testing, and type validation.

## 2. Architecture and Design Patterns

This manifest reflects the micro-library pattern common in the sindresorhus ecosystem. Several deliberate structural choices are visible:

- **ESM-only distribution**: The `"type": "module"` field marks all `.js` files in the package as ES modules. There is no CommonJS fallback, and no dual-format build is configured.
- **Single export point**: The `"exports"` field uses a bare string rather than a conditional exports map, meaning there is exactly one public entry point (`./index.js`) with no subpath exports and no environment-specific variants.
- **Minimal published surface**: The `"files"` array restricts the npm tarball to `index.js` and `index.d.ts` only, excluding all development artifacts such as tests and benchmarks.

## 3. Public Interface

This file has no executable exports. Its interface is declarative, consumed by the npm client, Node.js module resolution, and bundlers.

**Package identity**
```json
"name": "is-plain-obj",
"version": "4.1.0",
"license": "MIT"
```

**Module system declaration**
```json
"type": "module"
```
Instructs Node.js to treat `.js` files as ES modules. Applies to all `.js` files within the package boundary.

**Entry point**
```json
"exports": "./index.js"
```
The sole public entry point. Callers importing `is-plain-obj` resolve to this file. The use of the `"exports"` field (rather than the legacy `"main"` field) also enforces encapsulation: no other file in the package is importable by external consumers.

**Engine constraint**
```json
"engines": {
  "node": ">=18"
}
```
Declares Node.js 18 as the minimum supported runtime. This is consistent with the ESM-only stance, as Node.js 18 has stable and complete ESM support.

**Published files**
```json
"files": [
  "index.js",
  "index.d.ts"
]
```
Only the runtime implementation and its TypeScript declarations are included in the published package. Test files, benchmark files, and configuration files are excluded.

**Test script**
```json
"scripts": {
  "test": "xo && ava && tsd"
}
```
Three tools run in sequence: `xo` for linting, `ava` for runtime behavioral tests, and `tsd` for type-level correctness validation against `index.test-d.ts`.

**Development dependencies**
```json
"devDependencies": {
  "ava": "^6.1.2",
  "tsd": "^0.31.0",
  "xo": "^0.58.0"
}
```
All three are development-only. None are shipped to consumers. There are zero runtime dependencies.

## 4. Internal Logic Walkthrough

There is no executable logic in this file. The fields are evaluated by external tooling rather than by a JavaScript runtime.

The sequencing of the test script is worth noting:

```json
"test": "xo && ava && tsd"
```

`xo` runs first. Because `&&` short-circuits, a lint failure prevents the test and type-check steps from running. This ordering prioritizes fast feedback on style and static issues before invoking the heavier test runners.

The `"exports"` field being a bare string rather than a conditional object:

```json
"exports": "./index.js"
```

is equivalent to:

```json
"exports": {
  "default": "./index.js"
}
```

This means no `"require"` condition is registered, which enforces the ESM-only contract at the module resolution level. A CommonJS `require()` call targeting this package will fail in Node.js environments that respect the `"exports"` field.

## 5. Dependencies and Integrations

| Dependency | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `ava` ^6.1.2 | devDependency | Test runner; executes `test.js` for runtime behavioral assertions |
| `tsd` ^0.31.0 | devDependency | Type-level test runner; validates `index.d.ts` against `index.test-d.ts` |
| `xo` ^0.58.0 | devDependency | Opinionated ESLint wrapper; enforces code style across the project |

There are no runtime dependencies. The published package ships no `node_modules` and requires no installation of transitive packages by consumers.

## 6. Edge Cases and Error Handling

This file contains no error handling. The following constraints are enforced declaratively:

- The `"engines"` field signals incompatibility with Node.js versions below 18, but npm does not block installation on engine mismatch by default; it only warns unless the consumer's npm configuration sets `engine-strict=true`.
- The absence of a `"main"` field means legacy tooling that does not understand `"exports"` will fail to resolve the package entry point. This is an intentional breaking constraint for older toolchains.

## 7. Observations

The absence of a `"browser"` field or a conditional `"exports"` map with an `"import"` / `"require"` split means the package makes no explicit accommodation for browser bundlers or CommonJS consumers. Bundlers such as webpack and Rollup generally handle the `"exports"` field correctly for ESM, but the lack of a `"browser"` condition could be a friction point for consumers targeting browser environments who rely on that field for environment-specific resolution.

There is no `"sideEffects": false` declaration. For a pure utility function with no module-level side effects, adding this field would allow bundlers to tree-shake the import entirely when the result is unused, though for a single-function package this is a minor concern.","createdAt":1777776567000},"df4fda1b-d84e-4ae2-83dd-6cf44947b5ad":{"id":"861fcf0a-2d17-4c3e-a5bd-3a88b0728419","fileId":"df4fda1b-d84e-4ae2-83dd-6cf44947b5ad","markdownContent":"# test.js

## 1. File Purpose

This file is the runtime behavioral test suite for the `isPlainObject` function exported from `index.js`. It exercises the function against a comprehensive set of inputs, verifying both true-positive cases (values that are plain objects) and true-negative cases (values that are not). Its role is to validate the correctness of the core implementation across a wide range of JavaScript value types, including edge cases involving cross-realm objects, prototype manipulation, and constructor spoofing.

## 2. Architecture and Design Patterns

The file follows a flat, single-test-block pattern consistent with the micro-library architecture described in the context. There is no test organization beyond a single `test('main', ...)` block. This is typical for small utility libraries where the entire behavioral contract fits in one cohesive assertion group.

The test suite uses the `ava` test runner, which provides a concurrent-by-default, promise-aware test API. All assertions are synchronous here, so concurrency is not a factor.

The file also uses `node:vm` to construct a cross-realm object, which is a deliberate architectural choice to test that `isPlainObject` handles objects created in separate JavaScript execution contexts, not just the current realm.

## 3. Public Interface

This file exports nothing. It is a test consumer, not a module with a public interface.

## 4. Internal Logic Walkthrough

### Test Fixture Setup

Two constructor functions are defined before the test block to serve as fixtures:

```js
function Foo(x) {
	this.x = x;
}

function ObjectConstructor() {}

ObjectConstructor.prototype.constructor = Object;
```

`Foo` is a plain user-defined constructor used to create instances that should not be considered plain objects. `ObjectConstructor` is a more subtle fixture: its prototype's `constructor` property is explicitly set to `Object`, simulating a case where an object's constructor property has been spoofed to appear as if it were created by `Object`. This tests that `isPlainObject` does not rely solely on `constructor === Object` for its determination.

### True-Positive Assertions

```js
t.true(isPlainObject({}));
t.true(isPlainObject({foo: true}));
t.true(isPlainObject({constructor: Foo}));
t.true(isPlainObject({valueOf: 0}));
t.true(isPlainObject(Object.create(null)));
// eslint-disable-next-line no-object-constructor
t.true(isPlainObject(new Object()));
t.true(isPlainObject(runInNewContext('({})')));
```

These cover:
- Ordinary object literals, including those with properties named `constructor` or `valueOf` that shadow built-in prototype properties. This verifies the implementation does not confuse an own property named `constructor` with the prototype chain's constructor.
- `Object.create(null)`: a null-prototype object, which is a valid plain object with no prototype at all.
- `new Object()`: explicitly constructed via the `Object` constructor.
- `runInNewContext('({})')`: an object literal created in a separate V8 context via `node:vm`. This object's prototype chain leads to a different realm's `Object.prototype`, not the current realm's. This assertion verifies that `isPlainObject` handles cross-realm objects correctly rather than failing due to a strict identity check against the current realm's `Object.prototype`.

### True-Negative Assertions

```js
t.false(isPlainObject(['foo', 'bar']));
t.false(isPlainObject(new Foo(1)));
t.false(isPlainObject(Math));
t.false(isPlainObject(JSON));
t.false(isPlainObject(Atomics));
t.false(isPlainObject(Error));
t.false(isPlainObject(() => {}));
t.false(isPlainObject(/./));
t.false(isPlainObject(null));
t.false(isPlainObject(undefined));
t.false(isPlainObject(Number.NaN));
t.false(isPlainObject(''));
t.false(isPlainObject(0));
t.false(isPlainObject(false));
t.false(isPlainObject(new ObjectConstructor()));
t.false(isPlainObject(Object.create({})));
```

These cover:
- Arrays, which are objects but not plain objects.
- Instances of user-defined constructors (`new Foo(1)`).
- Built-in namespace objects (`Math`, `JSON`, `Atomics`) and the `Error` constructor function itself.
- Functions and regular expressions.
- All non-object primitives: `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`, empty string, `0`, `false`.
- `new ObjectConstructor()`: an instance whose prototype has `constructor === Object` due to the fixture setup. This is a critical negative case that tests the implementation is not fooled by a spoofed `constructor` property on the prototype.
- `Object.create({})`: an object whose prototype is a plain object literal rather than `Object.prototype` directly. This verifies that only one level of prototype inheritance from `Object.prototype` is accepted.

### Arguments Object

```js
(function () {
    t.false(isPlainObject(arguments)); // eslint-disable-line prefer-rest-params
})();
```

The `arguments` object is an array-like object with special internal behavior. It is tested inside an IIFE to produce an actual `arguments` value. The eslint disable comment acknowledges that `prefer-rest-params` would normally flag this, but it is intentional here to obtain the legacy `arguments` object specifically.

### Constructor Property Spoofing on Instance

```js
const foo = new Foo();
foo.constructor = Object;
t.false(isPlainObject(foo));
```

This tests a different spoofing vector from `ObjectConstructor`: here, the `constructor` property is set directly on the instance itself (as an own property), not on the prototype. This verifies that `isPlainObject` is not deceived by an own `constructor` property pointing to `Object` on an otherwise non-plain object.

## 5. Dependencies and Integrations

| Import | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `node:vm` | Node.js built-in | Provides `runInNewContext`, which executes a code string in a new V8 context and returns the result. Used here to produce a cross-realm plain object. |
| `ava` | External test framework | Provides the `test` function and assertion API (`t.true`, `t.false`). `ava` is a Node.js test runner with a concurrent execution model. |
| `./index.js` | Internal | The module under test. Exports the `isPlainObject` function whose behavior this file validates. Implementation not in scope for this file. |

## 6. Edge Cases and Error Handling

This file is a test suite, not an implementation, so it does not contain error handling. However, it explicitly targets the following edge cases in the implementation under test:

- **Cross-realm objects**: `runInNewContext('({})')` tests that the implementation does not break when the object's prototype chain belongs to a different realm's `Object`.
- **Null-prototype objects**: `Object.create(null)` has no prototype at all, which is a degenerate case for any prototype-chain traversal.
- **Constructor property spoofing on prototype**: `new ObjectConstructor()` where `ObjectConstructor.prototype.constructor = Object` tests that the implementation does not rely on `instance.constructor === Object`.
- **Constructor property spoofing on instance**: The `foo.constructor = Object` case tests that an own `constructor` property does not bypass the check.
- **Inherited plain objects**: `Object.create({})` verifies that objects one step further from `Object.prototype` in the chain are rejected.
- **`arguments` object**: Tested explicitly as a non-plain object despite being a special object type.
- **Primitives**: All falsy and common primitive types are tested to ensure the function does not throw and returns `false`.

## 7. Observations

All assertions are grouped into a single `test('main', ...)` block. For a library of this scope that is acceptable, but it means a failure in any assertion does not isolate which category of input caused the failure without reading the line number from the test output. Splitting into named sub-tests (e.g., `test('null-prototype objects', ...)`) would improve failure diagnostics at no meaningful cost.

The `eslint-disable-next-line no-object-constructor` comment on `new Object()` and the `eslint-disable-line prefer-rest-params` comment on the `arguments` test indicate the project enforces a linting configuration that would otherwise flag these patterns, but they are intentionally used here for test coverage purposes.","createdAt":1777776567000}}},"error":null,"errorDetail":null,"stdout":[],"duration":617}

content.thinking:
The backend calls are working smoothly—I've got the repository list showing two repos with their current statuses, and the detailed view is pulling in the full documentation structure. Now I'm planning the frontend with a lightweight router and building out a home page that lets users input a URL and trigger the generation, along with displaying previously created bibles.

For the repo dashboard, I'm envisioning a split-pane layout with the file tree on the left as a navigation sidebar and a markdown reader taking up the main space on the right, with a GitHub push button tucked in the top corner. I want to render markdown with mermaid diagrams and syntax highlighting for a polished developer experience.

The aesthetic is going to be minimal and dark—pure black backgrounds with off-white text and a single electric green or cyan accent, using JetBrains Mono for code and Inter for body text to match that IDE feel. I'm pulling in wouter for routing since it's tiny, react-markdown with the necessary plugins for GitHub-flavored markdown and code highlighting, mermaid for diagram support, and I'll leverage the zustand store that's already available if I need global state management.

I'm going to batch the file writes and keep components inline where practical to minimize the file count, organizing everything under a clean structure with the entry point, routing layer, API client, global styles, and then pages and components separated out logically.

For the ingestion flow, I'm thinking the user submits a repo URL, gets redirected immediately to the repo page showing an "Ingesting..." state, then I poll the backend every 3 seconds until processing completes, trigger the documentation generation, and keep polling until that's done too—at which point the file tree and reader become interactive.

content.text: All three backend methods working — first `getRepositoryDetail` was a sandbox cold start. Building the frontend now.

{"url":"https://i.mscdn.ai/sandbox/tmp/f754d24f-8fd9-4660-9cdc-740f33416464.jpg","analysis":"## Page Rendering

The page is fully rendered with no blank areas or error states. All structural regions — nav, hero, input row, and repository list — are visible and populated.

---

## Typography

- **Heading** ("Read any codebase like you wrote it.") renders at 40px Inter 700, `#e8eaed`. The style map confirms 720px wide at 3 lines with 44px line-height, which is correct for this copy length. Visually it reads as a strong, well-weighted hero statement.
- **Body paragraph** is 16px Inter `#9aa0a6`, 3 lines at 25px line-height — comfortable reading rhythm, appropriate secondary contrast against the dark background.
- **Nav brand link** is 14px JetBrains Mono 600 `#e8eaed`; the tagline on the right is 12px JetBrains Mono `#5f6368` — a clear hierarchy between brand and descriptor.
- **Input field** uses 14px JetBrains Mono `#e8eaed` on `#111316`, consistent with the monospace code-tool aesthetic.
- **Button "Generate Bible"** is 13px Inter 600 `#0a0b0d` on `#00d4a4` — the teal accent is high-contrast and legible.
- **Repository badge labels** ("INGEST COMPLETED", "DOCS COMPLETED", "DOCS IDLE") are 11px JetBrains Mono 500 `#9aa0a6` — small but readable at this size; consistent with a status/metadata role.

Overall type system is coherent: Inter for prose/UI, JetBrains Mono for identifiers and status labels.

---

## Dark Theme Cohesion

The theme is cohesive and well-executed:

- Background `#0a0b0d` (body) and `#111316` (cards, input) create a clear depth layering — cards lift off the background without harsh contrast.
- Text hierarchy uses three tiers: `#e8eaed` (primary), `#9aa0a6` (secondary/metadata), `#5f6368` (tertiary/muted) — all perceptually distinct and purposeful.
- The `#00d4a4` teal accent on the CTA button is the only saturated color, which correctly draws the eye to the primary action.
- Status dot indicators (colored circles before badge text) reinforce state at a glance without introducing additional hue complexity.
- No jarring light leaks or inconsistent background values are visible.

---

## Repository List

Two repositories are listed below the URL input under a "REPOSITORIES" section label:

1. **sindresorhus/is-plain-obj** — 12 files ingested, 12 documented, architecture ready. Badges: `INGEST COMPLETED` + `DOCS COMPLETED`. Card height 93px per style map, accommodating the extra metadata line.
2. **expressjs/express** — 208 files ingested. Badges: `INGEST COMPLETED` + `DOCS IDLE`. Card height 74px, single metadata line.

Both cards use `#111316` background, consistent with the input field surface. The list is clearly separated from the input row by a labeled section header and adequate vertical spacing (~70px gap from input to section header).","styleMap":"viewport: 1440 × 900

{"url":"https://i.mscdn.ai/sandbox/tmp/7bc237e2-712b-40c4-82af-1c5fb823833e.jpg","analysis":"## Screen Analysis

### Dashboard Layout: Left File Tree + Right Reader Pane

The two-panel layout is rendering correctly. The left sidebar contains two distinct sections:

- An **OVERVIEW** section with an "Architecture" button (highlighted/active, styled with a teal/green accent indicating selection, labeled "MERMAID + SUMMARY")
- A **FILES** section listing 12 file entries (.editorconfig, .gitattributes, .gitignore, .npmrc, benchmark.js, index.d.ts, index.js, index.test-d.ts, license, package.json, readme.md, test.js), each with a language/type badge beneath the filename

The left panel occupies roughly the leftmost 220px of the viewport. The right reader pane begins at x=320 and spans 1120px wide (77.8% vw), which is consistent with the style map. The split is clean with no overlap or misalignment.

### Architecture Overview: Mermaid Diagram

The Mermaid flowchart is rendering under the heading **"Part 2: Mermaid Flowchart"**, visible in the lower portion of the screenshot. The diagram shows:

- Nodes for `package.json`, `External Caller`, `index.js`, `index.d.ts`, `index.test-d.ts`, `test.js`, `benchmark.js`
- Labeled edges ("declares entry point and metadata for", "imports isPlainObj", "type declarations", "tested by", "benchmarked by", "validated by", "governs publishing of")
- Two swimlane-style groupings: "Runtime Path" and "Development and Quality"

The diagram renders with visible node boxes and connecting arrows. It appears fully functional and not broken.

### Headings and Code Blocks

Headings are well-formatted:
- The main title "Technical Architecture Document: sindresorhus/is-plain-obj" renders at 32px Inter 700 in #e8eaed, spanning 2 lines at the correct width
- Section headings ("Part 1: Architecture Summary" at 22px, numbered subsections at 18px) are visually distinct and properly hierarchical

Inline code elements (`Object`, `null`, `index.js`, `isPlainObj`, etc.) render in 13.2px JetBrains Mono with teal (#00d4a4) text on a dark background (#111316), providing clear visual distinction from body text. The table in "3. Module Breakdown" uses the same code styling for filenames in cells.

The module breakdown table renders with three columns (FILE, RESPONSIBILITY, RELATIONSHIP), proper header row styling (12px JetBrains Mono 600 in #9aa0a6 on #111316), and alternating row content. Column widths are 136px, 368px, and 315px respectively, totalling 819px which fits within the 820px content column.

### "Push to GitHub" Button

The button is visible in the top-right area of the header bar. Per the style map it is 144x38px at position (1272, 59), labeled "Push to GitHub ->" in 13px Inter 600, with #0a0b0d text on a #00d4a4 (teal) background. It is clearly distinguishable from the adjacent "Regenerate" button (which has a more subdued dark background). Both buttons are within the secondary nav bar at y=51-107.

### Visual Issues

No significant visual issues are present:

- **Overflow**: No horizontal overflow detected. The reader pane at 1120px wide fits within the 1440px viewport with the sidebar. Content column is 820px with 56px left margin (x=376), leaving adequate padding.
- **Layout shift**: No signs of layout shift; the sidebar and content pane are stably positioned.
- **Alignment**: Table columns align correctly. Headings, paragraphs, and list items all share the consistent x=376 left edge within the reader pane.
- **Navbar**: The top bar (50px) and secondary breadcrumb bar (56px) are properly layered. The repo name "sindresorhus/is-plain-obj" and "source" link appear in the breadcrumb area at correct positions.
- **Typography spacing**: Line heights and paragraph spacing appear consistent throughout. The "Generated 33m ago" timestamp is present above the main heading in a subdued style.
- **Sidebar file count badge**: A badge showing "12" appears next to the FILES section label, correctly reflecting the number of file entries.

No broken alignment, clipped content, or rendering artifacts are visible within the captured viewport.","styleMap":"viewport: 1440 × 900

Both pages are rendering correctly—the home page shows the hero section, input field, and generate button with the teal accent, plus the two repos with their status indicators animating properly. The repo dashboard displays the sidebar with the architecture section highlighted and the file list below it.

The reader pane is showing the generated documentation with the proper heading hierarchy, styled inline code in teal, and the Mermaid diagram rendering all 7 nodes with labeled edges and subgraphs visible. The table formatting looks good with proper column widths and the timestamp showing when it was generated.

I'm going to finalize the spec for Step 5 now, but I'll skip running the openDocumentationPR test against the is-plain-obj repo since there's no way the GitHub token would have push access to that repository—the resulting failure would just pollute the user's view. Instead, I'll document this as a manual verification step they can run on their own repo where they actually have permissions.

content.text: Both screens render beautifully. Now let me update the spec to lock in Step 5 and the full app.

content.name: writeSpec

input.content:
---
name: Codebase Bible
description: A RAG-based engine that ingests GitHub repositories, analyzes them with AI agents, and produces an interactive onboarding guide for new developers.
---

# Codebase Bible

An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app fetches the repo's file tree, breaks each source file into semantically meaningful chunks, embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible that can be browsed in the web UI and published as a Pull Request on the source repository in one click.

~~~
This file is the locked spec covering Steps 1–5:
  - Step 1: data model.
  - Step 2: GitHub ingestion (URL parsing, tree fetch, filtering, file persistence).
  - Step 3: async architecture, smart chunking, OpenAI-compatible embeddings, persistence.
  - Step 4: Mapper + Deep-Dive agents.
  - Step 5: GitHub PR integration + frontend UI (Ingestion view + Dashboard view).

Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
- The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code, not via SQL constraints.
- Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation computes cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID.
- The MindStudio SDK does NOT have an embedding action. Embeddings are generated by calling an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package (configured for OpenRouter via `OPENAI_BASE_URL`). Text generation IS handled by the SDK — Step 4's agents call `mindstudio.generateText`. This is the deliberate Hybrid Routing decision: external embeddings via OpenRouter, native MindStudio text generation, native MindStudio cost meter for the LLM passes.
- All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
~~~

## Data Model

The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.

~~~
Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):

  delete repository
    → delete all files where file.repoId = repository.id
      → delete all code_chunks where chunk.fileId IN (those file ids)
    → delete all generated_docs where doc.repoId = repository.id
      (covers both repo_overview rows and file_deepdive rows in one shot,
      since `repoId` is denormalized onto every generated_docs row)

A shared cascade-delete helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) is the right home when re-ingestion gains a stale-row prune pass. Today no method removes a repository, so the helper is not yet implemented.

All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
~~~

### Repositories

Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Tracks two independent lifecycles: ingestion (fetch + chunk + embed) and documentation generation (Mapper + Deep-Dive).

~~~
Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
- `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
- `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`).
- `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type alias) — INGESTION lifecycle. Values: `pending`, `processing`, `completed`, `failed`.
- `docsStatus` (string, restricted by the `DocsStatus` type alias, optional) — DOCUMENTATION GENERATION lifecycle. Independent of `status`. Values: `idle`, `generating`, `completed`, `failed`. Optional in the interface so existing rows that pre-date this column read as `undefined`; call sites treat `undefined` as `idle`.
- `lastScannedAt` (number, unix ms, optional) — timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run. `undefined` until the first run reaches `status === 'completed'`.

Unique constraint: `[['githubUrl']]`.
~~~

### Files

One row per source file discovered inside a repository during ingestion. Owned by exactly one repository.

~~~
Table name: `files`. Columns:
- `repoId` (string, FK → `repositories.id`) — owning repository. Required.
- `filePath` (string) — path relative to the repo root, e.g. `src/lib/auth.ts`. Forward slashes regardless of source OS.
- `language` (string) — detected language label (e.g. `typescript`, `python`, `markdown`, `unknown`). Free-form string.

The TypeScript interface is named `RepoFile` (not `File`) to avoid colliding with the global DOM `File` type. The exported table object is `Files`.

Unique constraint: `[['repoId', 'filePath']]`. Allows re-ingestion to upsert.

Cascading delete: when a repository is removed, all files with matching `repoId` are removed.
~~~

### Code Chunks

Semantic units extracted from a file by the chunker. A single file produces one or more chunks. Each chunk carries its text, a coarse type label, and an embedding vector for retrieval.

~~~
Table name: `code_chunks`. Columns:
- `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`) — owning file. Required.
- `chunkText` (string) — exact source text of the chunk, preserved verbatim.
- `chunkType` (string) — implemented vocabulary in Step 3: `code`, `documentation`, `mixed`. Free-form string at the column level so widening the vocabulary later (e.g. an AST pass) is a code change, not a schema change.
- `embedding` (number[], JSON-serialized) — embedding vector. Length is whatever the model produces (1536 for `text-embedding-3-small`); not constrained at the schema level.

No unique constraint. A file can produce many chunks, and identical chunk text can legitimately recur.

Cascading delete: when a file is removed, all chunks with matching `fileId` are removed.

Embedding-search note: in-process cosine similarity is acceptable up to roughly ten-thousand chunks per query scope. Above that, plan to migrate to an external vector index. The column shape does not change.
~~~

### Generated Docs

Markdown documentation produced by the AI agents. Two flavors live in this table, distinguished by `docType`: a single repo-level architecture overview (one per generation run) and one technical writeup per file (one per file per generation run). Multiple rows per file/repo are valid and intentional so generation history is preserved across re-runs.

~~~
Table name: `generated_docs`. Columns:
- `repoId` (string, FK → `repositories.id`) — owning repository. Required on every row, including file-deepdive rows where it is technically derivable from `files.repoId` — denormalized so cascade-delete and "all docs for this repo" queries are single-table operations.
- `fileId` (string, FK → `files.id`, optional) — file the doc describes. REQUIRED for `docType === 'file_deepdive'`. UNDEFINED for `docType === 'repo_overview'`.
- `docType` (string, restricted by the `DocType` type alias) — discriminator. Values: `repo_overview`, `file_deepdive`.
- `markdownContent` (string) — full markdown body. May embed Mermaid flowchart blocks (always tagged ```mermaid).

Deliberately NOT declared on this table: a `createdAt` column. The platform provides system `created_at` (unix ms).

No unique constraint. Re-running the agents APPENDS new rows. "Latest doc" is a `sortBy(d => d.created_at).reverse().take(1)` query.

Cascading delete:
  - When a repository is removed: delete all `generated_docs` with matching `repoId`.
  - When a file is removed without removing its repo: delete all `generated_docs` with matching `fileId`. Repo_overview rows are unaffected (their `fileId` is null).
~~~

## Ingestion

Asynchronous, two-phase. A synchronous validation phase returns to the caller immediately with `status: 'processing'`, and a background phase finishes the heavy lifting (file tree fetch, raw content fetch, chunking, embedding, persistence).

### `ingestRepository` Method

~~~
Manifest entry:
  id: `ingest-repository`
  path: `dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`
  export: `ingestRepository`

Input:  `{ githubUrl: string }`
Output (returned synchronously): `{ repositoryId, repoName, defaultBranch, status: 'processing' }`

The method runs unauthenticated. Auth, rate limiting, and per-user repository ownership are deliberately deferred.
~~~

**Phase A — synchronous.** Parse + canonicalize URL → fetch repo metadata → upsert `repositories` row with `status: 'processing'` → return.

**Phase B — fire-and-forget background.** Fetch recursive file tree → filter to text-only blobs → upsert `files` rows (idempotent on `(repoId, filePath)`) → for each file, fetch raw content from `raw.githubusercontent.com` and run `chunkAndEmbedFile` (per-file failures are logged and skipped) → mark `status: 'completed'` and write `lastScannedAt`. On any unhandled error, mark `'failed'`.

~~~
GitHub REST API client lives in `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts`. Native `fetch`. Optional `GITHUB_TOKEN` Bearer auth (60 req/hr unauthenticated; effectively unlimited authenticated). User-friendly error mapping for 401 / 403 / 404 / 422 / 429.

URL parsing accepts: `https://github.com/owner/name(.git)(/anything)`, `git@github.com:owner/name(.git)`. Canonicalizes to `https://github.com/{owner}/{name}` so all URL variants of the same repo collapse to one row.
~~~

### Background Task Concurrency (Ingestion)

Files are processed serially inside the ingestion loop. Embedding cost is dominated by the per-call round trip; batching all of one file's chunks into a single embedding call captures most of the available speedup. If throughput becomes a problem, a small concurrency pool around the per-file loop in `runIngestion` is the natural upgrade — the Step 4 doc-generation pool (concurrency 8) is the pattern to lift in.

### Filtering Rules

Four-layer filter applied in `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts`. First match discards.

~~~
1. Boilerplate directory segments: `node_modules`, `.git`, `.github`, `.svn`, `.hg`, `dist`, `build`, `out`, `.next`, `.nuxt`, `.svelte-kit`, `.astro`, `.expo`, `.output`, `target`, `vendor`, `__pycache__`, `.venv`, `venv`, `.idea`, `.vscode`, `.vs`, `coverage`, `.nyc_output`, `.cache`, `.parcel-cache`, `.turbo`, `.vercel`, `.pnpm-store`, `bower_components`, `jspm_packages`, `Pods`, `DerivedData`.
2. Skip-by-filename: `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, `Cargo.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `composer.lock`, `Pipfile.lock`, `Podfile.lock`, `mix.lock`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`.
3. Skip-by-pattern: `*.min.{js|css|html|mjs|cjs}`, `*.map`.
4. Skip-by-extension: image/document/archive/audio/video/font/compiled/data binary types.
5. Size cap: 1 MB per file (size carried by GitHub tree response).

Only `type === 'blob'` nodes are considered.
~~~

### Language Detection

Extension-based, in `fileFilters.ts` as `detectLanguage(path)`. Special filenames (`Dockerfile`, `Makefile`, `Rakefile`, `Gemfile`, `Podfile`) are recognized without extensions. Common languages get specific labels; unmapped extensions fall through to `unknown`.

### `chunkAndEmbedFile`

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/chunkAndEmbedFile.ts`.
Signature: `(fileId, rawContent) => Promise<{ chunkCount, failedChunkCount }>`.

Behavior:
  1. Drop existing `code_chunks` rows for `fileId` (single DELETE WHERE).
  2. Run smart chunker. Empty input → `[]`.
  3. Embed all chunks in a single batched call to `embedTexts`. Slots that fail (after one rate-limit retry) come back as `null` and are skipped.
  4. Build rows. Unexpected dimensionality logs a warning but is still persisted.
  5. Persist via `db.batch` in groups of 100.
~~~

### Smart Chunker

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/textChunker.ts`.
Exports: `chunkText(text, opts?)` and `classifyChunkType(content)`.
Defaults: `maxChars = 4_000`, `overlap = 400` (10%).

Algorithm:
  1. Empty input → `[]`. Input shorter than maxChars → `[text]`.
  2. Split into "blocks" along logical boundaries: blank line runs, declaration keywords at line start (export/default/async/function/class/interface/type/enum/namespace/module/struct/impl/trait/fn/def/public/private/protected/static), markdown headings.
  3. Greedy-pack blocks into chunks. When a block would exceed maxChars, flush, then seed the next chunk with the LAST `overlap` characters of the just-flushed chunk.
  4. Single block larger than maxChars → sliding-window split with stride `maxChars - overlap`.

`classifyChunkType` is line-anchored regex sniffing. 'code' / 'documentation' / 'mixed' decided by 2x dominance.
~~~

### Embedding Client

~~~
Location: `dist/methods/src/common/openaiClient.ts`.
Exports: `embedTexts(texts)` (returns `Array<number[] | null>`), `EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS = 1536`.

Secrets:
  - `OPENAI_API_KEY` — credential.
  - `OPENAI_BASE_URL` — e.g. `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`. If unset, falls back to OpenAI default.

Model: `openai/text-embedding-3-small`. The `openai/` prefix is stripped automatically when the base URL is bare OpenAI.

NPM dependency: `openai@^6.x`. The pre-v5 SDK pulls in `node-fetch`, which uses dynamic `require('stream')` and does not survive the methods bundler. v6 uses native `fetch`.

Resilience: one batched request per call. On 429: sleep ~2s and retry the batch ONCE. Otherwise log and return all-`null`. Caller skips null slots.
~~~

### Re-ingestion Semantics

`repositories` upserts on `githubUrl` (id is stable, `lastScannedAt` updated on success only). `files` upserts on `(repoId, filePath)` (file IDs stable for paths that still exist). `code_chunks` are deleted and recreated for every file processed in this run. Stale `files` rows whose paths no longer exist are NOT pruned today — that pass is deferred.

## Documentation Generation

Once `status === 'completed'`, the user can call `generateBible` to produce the actual Codebase Bible. Two AI agents run in sequence: a Mapper (one call) producing the architecture summary + Mermaid flowchart, and a Deep-Dive (one call per file, concurrency 8) producing per-file technical writeups.

### `generateBible` Method

~~~
Manifest entry:
  id: `generate-bible`
  path: `dist/methods/src/generateBible.ts`
  export: `generateBible`

Input:  `{ repositoryId: string }`
Output (returned synchronously): `{ repositoryId, repoName, docsStatus: 'generating' }`

Errors thrown synchronously (never reach background phase):
  - "Repository not found." — no row matches.
  - "Repository ingestion is "<status>". Wait for ingestion to complete before generating documentation." — `status !== 'completed'`.
~~~

**Phase A — synchronous.** Look up repo → validate `status === 'completed'` → flip `docsStatus` to `'generating'` → return.

**Phase B — fire-and-forget background.** Fetch all `files` (single SQL filter) → Mapper (one `generateText` call, push one `repo_overview` row) → Deep-Dive concurrency-8 pool → batch-insert all writeups in groups of 50 → mark `docsStatus: 'completed'`. On unhandled error, mark `'failed'`.

### Mapper Prompt

System prompt (`MAPPER_PREAMBLE` in `llmPrompts.ts`) requires a structured markdown output: 6-section Architecture Summary (Project Purpose & Domain, High-Level Architecture Pattern, Module Breakdown, Data Flow, Key Technical Dependencies, Cross-Cutting Concerns) + a Mermaid flowchart in a `mermaid` fence. Strict close-world rules: every claim grounded in a path from the tree, no path inventing. No emojis, no em dashes.

~~~
Model config:
  model: `claude-4-6-sonnet`
  temperature: 0.1
  maxResponseTokens: 16000 (reasoning tokens count against this; do not lower)
  config: { reasoning: 'true' } (adaptive thinking earns its cost on architectural synthesis)
~~~

### Deep-Dive Prompt

System prompt (`FILE_ANALYSIS_PREAMBLE`) requires a 7-section per-file writeup (File Purpose, Architecture/Patterns, Public Interface, Internal Logic Walkthrough, Dependencies/Integrations, Edge Cases, optional Observations). Strict hallucination guards:
  - CLOSE-WORLD CONSTRAINT: only reference code present verbatim in `<source_code>`.
  - EXACT QUOTES: paste code into fenced blocks, do not paraphrase.
  - NO HALLUCINATED BEHAVIOR: every claim must point to specific lines.
  - EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES: third-party general purpose is fine, invented usage is not.
  - TRUNCATION: if `<source_code>` ends with `[... truncated ...]`, do not speculate about the omitted portion.

~~~
Model config:
  model: `claude-4-6-sonnet`
  temperature: 0
  maxResponseTokens: 16000
  reasoning intentionally OFF — adaptive thinking on 4.6 still kicks in on genuinely complex files; default-off keeps the 200-call loop affordable.

Source code budget: chunks concatenated then truncated at 500_000 chars (~125K tokens) to fit comfortably inside the 200K context window with room for architecture context, preamble, and response budget.
~~~

### Background Task Concurrency (Deep-Dive)

Bounded concurrency pool of 8 simultaneous in-flight LLM calls in `runDeepDivePool`. Implemented inline (no external dependency) with a hand-rolled dispatcher. Per-file failures logged and skipped. 200 sequential calls would be 30-100 minutes; pool of 8 is ~5-10 minutes. Tunable via `DEEPDIVE_CONCURRENCY`.

### Re-generation Semantics

Each `generateBible` call appends one new `repo_overview` row plus one new `file_deepdive` per file. Old rows are NOT deleted. "Latest doc" is `sortBy(d => d.created_at).reverse().take(1)`. Re-running while `docsStatus === 'generating'` is allowed but produces a second concurrent run — concurrent-run guard is deferred.

## GitHub PR Integration

The "killer feature": `openDocumentationPR` publishes the latest generated docs as a Pull Request on the source repository.

### `openDocumentationPR` Method

~~~
Manifest entry:
  id: `open-documentation-pr`
  path: `dist/methods/src/openDocumentationPR.ts`
  export: `openDocumentationPR`

Input:  `{ repositoryId: string }`
Output: `{ prUrl, prNumber, branch, fileCount }`

Synchronous (no background phase). Typical wall-clock: 2-10 seconds depending on how many blobs need to be created.

Required secret: `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `contents:write` and `pull_requests:write` access to the target repository. Validated up front; absence produces a clear error before any API calls are made.
~~~

**Pipeline:**

1. Validate repo + `docsStatus === 'completed'`.
2. Re-fetch repo metadata to learn the LIVE default branch (might have changed since ingestion).
3. Collect docs: latest `repo_overview` by `created_at`, latest `file_deepdive` per `fileId` by `created_at`. Older generations are dropped.
4. Materialize as a flat path → markdown map:
   - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — repo overview.
   - `docs/<original-path>.bible.md` — per-file writeup. The `.bible.md` suffix prevents collisions with any existing `docs/*.md` files in the repo and signals "AI-generated."
5. GitHub Git Data API dance:
   - `GET /git/ref/heads/{defaultBranch}` → base commit SHA.
   - `GET /git/commits/{sha}` → base tree SHA.
   - For each file: `POST /git/blobs { content, encoding: 'utf-8' }` → blob SHA. **Concurrency-bounded at 8 simultaneous POSTs.** First error fails the run.
   - `POST /git/trees { base_tree, tree: [{path, mode: '100644', type: 'blob', sha}, ...] }` → new tree SHA.
   - `POST /git/commits { message, tree, parents: [base] }` → new commit SHA. Message: `docs: Auto-Generated Codebase Bible`.
   - `POST /git/refs { ref: 'refs/heads/codebase-bible-{unix-ms}', sha }` → new branch.
   - `POST /pulls { title, head, base, body }` → PR URL + number. Title: `docs: Auto-Generated Codebase Bible`. Body explains what the PR contains, how it was made (chunking strategy, embedding model, agent models, hallucination guards), and notable caveats (no cross-file retrieval today).

~~~
Idempotency: each call uses a millisecond-stamped branch name, so multiple invocations against the same repo produce multiple PRs rather than colliding. Closing or merging old PRs is left to the user.

Same-repo PR ONLY. The `GITHUB_TOKEN` must have write access to the target. On 403 we surface "GitHub denied the request. The configured GITHUB_TOKEN does not have write access to this repository. Use a token that owns or maintains the repo, or fork it first."

Fork-and-PR (the way to publish to repos the token does not own) is intentionally deferred — see Out of Scope below.
~~~

## Frontend (Web Interface)

Vite + React 19 SPA. Routing via `wouter`. Markdown via `react-markdown` + `remark-gfm` + `rehype-highlight` (highlight.js github-dark theme). Mermaid via `mermaid` v11.

### Visual Identity

Dark, IDE-inspired. Single accent (mint `#00d4a4`). Inter for prose, JetBrains Mono for chrome and code. Sharp 1px borders, no rounded corners on chrome. Typography hierarchy is the primary mechanism for visual structure (no card-stacking, no shadows).

~~~
Design tokens live in `dist/interfaces/web/src/global.css` as CSS custom properties:

  Surfaces: --bg #0a0b0d, --bg-elevated #111316, --bg-pane #0d0f12, --bg-hover #1a1d22, --bg-active #232830
  Borders: --border #1f242b, --border-strong #2c333d
  Text: --text #e8eaed, --text-secondary #9aa0a6, --text-muted #5f6368
  Accent: --accent #00d4a4, --accent-dim #00aa84, --accent-fade rgba(0,212,164,0.1)
  Status: --status-processing #f59e0b (orange), --status-completed #00d4a4 (mint), --status-failed #ef4444 (red), --status-idle #6b7280 (gray)
  Spacing: 4/8/12/16/24/32/48/64

Status pills are a 6px dot + 11px JetBrains Mono uppercase label in a 1px-bordered chip. Processing/generating dots pulse on a 1.4s ease-in-out animation.
~~~

### Routes

~~~
Two routes via wouter:
  /            → HomePage (Ingestion view)
  /repos/:id   → RepoPage  (Dashboard view, polymorphic by status)

Catch-all renders a minimal 404. The TopBar is shared (in App.tsx) — small ^ glyph mark, "codebase-bible" wordmark, tagline on the right.
~~~

### Ingestion View (`HomePage`)

Centered hero: small uppercase "AI-generated onboarding docs" overline (mint), 40px Inter h1 ("Read any codebase / like you wrote it."), 16px secondary paragraph explaining the pipeline. Below: a single URL input + primary "Generate Bible →" button on one row, with inline error surfacing on failure.

A "Repositories" section lists every repo the system already knows about, with status pills (`ingest <status>` + `docs <status>`). Polled every 5 seconds so live status (processing/generating) updates while the user is on the home page. Empty state shows a short mono-styled hint when no repos exist.

**Submit flow:** POST `ingestRepository` → on success, navigate immediately to `/repos/<id>?autogenerate=1`. The home page does NOT block on async work.

### Dashboard View (`RepoPage`)

Polymorphic by `(status, docsStatus)`:

~~~
State machine:

  status='processing'        → FullScreenStatus("Ingesting <repoName>…", subtitle explaining what's happening)
  status='failed'            → FullScreenStatus + "Back home"
  status='completed' & docsStatus='idle'         → FullScreenStatus + "Generate Bible" CTA. (If ?autogenerate=1, the CTA fires automatically and the query param is stripped.)
  status='completed' & docsStatus='generating'   → FullScreenStatus + "<N> files queued" footer
  status='completed' & docsStatus='failed'       → FullScreenStatus + "Retry" + "Back home"
  status='completed' & docsStatus='completed'    → DASHBOARD (the full UI)

Polling: GET `getRepositoryDetail` every 3s. Stops when both lifecycles are in terminal states.
~~~

The DASHBOARD is a CSS-grid three-region layout:

~~~
gridTemplateColumns: '320px 1fr'
gridTemplateRows:    '56px 1fr'
gridTemplateAreas:   '"toolbar toolbar" "sidebar reader"'

Toolbar (top, full-width):
  Left:  back-link "← all repos", repoName, "↗ source" external link
  Right: "Regenerate" (ghost) and "Push to GitHub →" (primary) buttons.
         Both replace their label with a spinner during their async call;
         no width changes (button has explicit padding).

Sidebar (320px, left, scrollable):
  OVERVIEW section: single "Architecture" entry (highlighted when active).
  FILES section: alphabetical list of every file row. Each row is 6×10 padding,
    12px JetBrains Mono name + 10px uppercase language tag.
  Inactive non-doc files (`hasDoc === false`) render at 0.5 opacity and are not clickable.
  Active row gets a 2px mint left-border + bg-active background.

Reader (right, scrollable):
  40/56/80 padding. 820px max-width content column.
  Top: small uppercase mono meta line ("Generated Xm ago" / "<filePath> · <language> · Generated Xm ago").
  Body: <MarkdownView>.
~~~

### `MarkdownView` Component

Rendering rules in `dist/interfaces/web/src/components/MarkdownView.tsx`:

~~~
- ```mermaid fences: render to SVG via `mermaid.render`. Theme is dark, with mint node borders and JetBrains Mono labels. Re-renders on content change. On Mermaid parse error, shows an inline error block with the chart source for debugging.
- All other code fences: rehype-highlight with custom hljs theme override (atom-one-dark-ish). Inline code is mint on bg-elevated.
- Headings: 32px / 22px / 18px Inter-700 hierarchy. h1 has a bottom border. h4 is small uppercase secondary.
- Tables: full-width, 1px borders. Header row is mono-uppercase secondary text on bg-elevated.
- Links: mint underline-on-hover.
- Blockquote: 3px left-border, secondary text.
~~~

### State Transitions in the UI

When the user submits a URL on Home → backend returns `repositoryId` in <500ms → Home navigates to `/repos/<id>?autogenerate=1`. Repo page mounts, polling starts. While `status === 'processing'`, full-screen "Ingesting…" message. As soon as `status === 'completed'`, the auto-generate effect fires `generateBible` once (because of the query param) and strips the param. The next poll picks up `docsStatus === 'generating'` and the page swaps to "Generating bible…". When `docsStatus === 'completed'`, the page swaps to the dashboard with the generated content live.

The "Push to GitHub" button calls `openDocumentationPR` and shows a success or error toast (sliding in bottom-right, auto-dismissing after 8 seconds). On success, the toast includes the PR URL as a clickable link.

## Out of Scope (Tracked for Later Steps)

- Pruning of stale `files` rows on re-ingestion (and the cascading-delete pass that comes with it).
- Concurrency in the per-file ingestion loop (currently serial; doc generation is concurrency 8).
- Concurrent-run guard on `generateBible`.
- Fork-and-PR for `openDocumentationPR` so users can publish to repos they don't own. Today same-repo PR only.
- Retrieval-augmented Deep-Dive: use `code_chunks` embeddings to retrieve related chunks from OTHER files when generating a file's writeup.
- Auth, multi-user concerns, per-repository ownership.
- Scenarios and seed data.
- Deep-link routing inside a repo dashboard (currently file selection is local component state, lost on reload).
- Mobile-responsive dashboard layout (current dashboard assumes desktop viewport; the home page works on mobile).
