content.name: readFile
content.result:
1 An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app fetches the repo's file tree, breaks each source file into semantically meaningful chunks, embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible that can be browsed in the web UI and published as a Pull Request on the source repository in one click.
   2 
   3 ~~~
   4 This file is the locked spec covering Steps 1–5:
   5 
   6 - Step 1: data model.
   7 - Step 2: GitHub ingestion (URL parsing, tree fetch, filtering, file persistence).
   8 - Step 3: async architecture, smart chunking, OpenAI-compatible embeddings, persistence.
   9 - Step 4: Mapper + Deep-Dive agents.
  10 - Step 5: GitHub PR integration + frontend UI (Ingestion view + Dashboard view).
  11 
  12 Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
  13 
  14 - The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code, not via SQL constraints.
  15 - Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation computes cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID.
  16 - The MindStudio SDK does NOT have an embedding action. Embeddings are generated by calling an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package (configured for OpenRouter via `OPENAI_BASE_URL`). Text generation IS handled by the SDK — Step 4's agents call `mindstudio.generateText`. This is the deliberate Hybrid Routing decision: external embeddings via OpenRouter, native MindStudio text generation, native MindStudio cost meter for the LLM passes.
  17 - All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
  18 ~~~
  19 
  20 ## Data Model
  21 
  22 The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.
  23 
  24 ~~~
  25 Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):
  26 
  27 delete repository
  28 → delete all files where file.repoId = repository.id
  29 → delete all code_chunks where chunk.fileId IN (those file ids)
  30 → delete all generated_docs where doc.repoId = repository.id
  31 (covers both repo_overview rows and file_deepdive rows in one shot,
  32 since `repoId` is denormalized onto every generated_docs row)
  33 
  34 A shared cascade-delete helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) is the right home when re-ingestion gains a stale-row prune pass. Today no method removes a repository, so the helper is not yet implemented.
  35 
  36 All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
  37 ~~~
  38 
  39 ### Repositories
  40 
  41 Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Tracks two independent lifecycles: ingestion (fetch + chunk + embed) and documentation generation (Mapper + Deep-Dive).
  42 
  43 ~~~
  44 Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
  45 
  46 - `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
  47 - `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`).
  48 - `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type al
  49 ~~~
  50

content.text:
Before I dive in, a quick read on the assignment: app icons and OG images both have to survive aggressive scaling. The icon ends up as a 16px favicon; the OG image gets re-rendered as a 200px-tall card in a Slack thread. Both fail in the same way — too much detail, illegible text, generic "tech" gradients. The brief is right to lock the aesthetic down: dark, single mint accent, IDE-flavored. I'm going to commit hard to that.

I'll generate the icon directly. For the OG image, I'm going to do two things in parallel: generate finished candidates, **and** give you a precise HTML/CSS spec so you can render the canonical version server-side with real Inter and JetBrains Mono. AI text rendering is good in 2026 but it's not pixel-perfect, and OG images compress to ~600px wide where any typographic mush becomes obvious. The HTML-rendered version is what I'd actually ship.

content.name: generateImages

prompts: Premium 2026 macOS-style app icon, full bleed square composition, no padding around the artwork. Subject: a 3D rendered closed black codex/tome viewed from slight 3-quarter angle, sitting upright. The book is matte obsidian black (#0a0b0d) with a subtle micro-texture suggesting a fine technical grid, like terminal phosphor lines etched into the cover. The book's spine glows softly with a thin vertical bar of mint-cyan light (#00d4a4) — like a cursor or a strip of bookmark light — emitting a controlled soft glow that bleeds slightly onto the surrounding darkness. The cover has a single embossed glyph: a stylized forward-slash and closing curly brace combined, very small, in slightly lighter charcoal so it reads as a debossed mark, not a logo. Background is pure deep black (#0a0b0d), with the book grounded by a barely-perceptible subsurface gradient. Hard rim-light from the upper left edge picks out one sharp highlight on the cover. No rounded corners on the artwork itself, no bevel, no playful elements. Restrained, premium, technical, IDE-flavored. The vibe is 'this is a serious tool used by senior engineers'.

Premium 2026 app icon, full bleed square. Flat-but-dimensional graphic mark on a deep obsidian (#0a0b0d) background. The mark: an oversized stylized open book formed entirely from sharp horizontal mint-cyan (#00d4a4) lines — three short lines on the left page, four short lines on the right page, centered around a single vertical mint cursor bar that acts as the book's spine and pulses with the strongest light. The lines vary slightly in length like real lines of code or text. The mark is rendered with crisp 2px stroke weight, no anti-aliasing softness, like vector geometry. A faint mint outer glow surrounds the entire mark only. Edges are razor-sharp. Background has an extremely subtle radial vignette, darker at the corners. No bevel, no rounded chrome, no skeuomorphism, no text, no other elements. Feels like a glyph from a developer tool, not a consumer app.

Premium 2026 macOS-style app icon, full bleed square. Subject: an oversized JetBrains Mono asterisk character rendered as a 3D extruded geometric form, mint-cyan (#00d4a4) with subtle internal gradient toward brighter mint at the tips, casting a soft mint glow onto a deep obsidian black background (#0a0b0d). The asterisk is precisely centered, occupying about 55% of the icon. The negative space around it is pure dark with a barely visible square frame implied by a 1px mint-cyan border just inside the icon edges. The entire composition feels like a glowing terminal cursor character on a sharp IDE window. No rounded outer chrome, no bevel, hard edges, restrained, technical, premium.

## What It Depicts
A 3D-rendered hardcover book with a dark textured cover, embossed with a stylized `/>` or `/}` coding/markup symbol at center. The spine emits a vivid cyan/teal glow, and the cover surface features a subtle grid pattern overlaid with faint horizontal lines resembling code or data streams. The overall concept merges the idea of a book or journal with developer/tech aesthetics.

## Mood & Color Palette
- **Mood:** Dark, futuristic, technical, premium. Evokes cyberpunk and developer tooling aesthetics.
- **Primary colors:** Near-black (#0a0a0a), deep charcoal (#1a1a1a)
- **Accent:** Bright cyan/teal (~#00e5d4) concentrated on the spine glow and reflected on the surface below
- **Secondary tones:** Muted teal highlights bleeding onto the cover face

## Lighting & Composition
The light source is the glowing spine itself, acting as a practical light that casts a soft teal pool on the ground plane and subtly illuminates the cover face. The background is pure black, creating strong contrast. The book is positioned at a three-quarter angle, slightly left of center, giving it depth and dimensionality. Composition is clean and product-shot-style with generous negative space on all sides.

## Text Present
No readable text. The embossed symbol (`/}`) on the cover functions as a logo or icon mark rather than legible copy. Faint horizontal lines on the cover suggest stylized code but are not legible.

## Issues
- No significant artifacts or distortions detected
- The glow bloom on the spine is intentional and clean, no fringing
- Slight banding may appear in the dark background at low bit-depth exports -- verify source file depth before use
- The embossed symbol reads ambiguously at small sizes; may not scale well below ~100px

content.thinking:
The brand spec is mostly placeholder, but the developer's message locked in the aesthetic: dark surfaces in near-black, mint-cyan accents, Inter for body text and JetBrains Mono for code, sharp 1px borders without any rounded elements, and an overall IDE/terminal vibe aimed at engineers. Now I'm thinking through how both deliverables should reflect this locked-in direction.

For the app icon, I need something that works at small sizes and immediately reads as "Codebase Bible" - combining the concept of code with a reference book. I'm exploring a few directions: a tome with code elements, brackets forming a book shape, a monogram, or stacked lines that suggest both pages and code. The strongest approach feels like a bold geometric mark that leans into the IDE aesthetic - maybe a dark obsidian square with a glowing mint accent that forms a stylized book or codex shape using terminal-inspired elements, rather than going 3D rendered since the sharp, flat aesthetic seems more native to the brand.

For the OG image, I'm thinking an IDE mockup showing the actual product in action - a split-pane editor view with a file tree on one side and the generated documentation on the other, rendered with mint syntax highlights. This instantly communicates what the tool does and works better at small sizes than abstract concepts. I'm planning to generate a batch of assets: three icon variants at 2048x2048 and two OG image variants at 4096x2150 to match the 1200:630 aspect ratio. I'll craft detailed prompts for each—the icon variants will explore a geometric monogram, a 3D rendered tome, and other premium design directions that feel modern and IDE-flavored. The text rendering is tricky with AI generation, so I'm weighing options: I could have the developer build the OG image as an HTML page rendered server-side for pixel-perfect typography, or I could generate it as artwork and accept some text imperfection. Since the user wants both deliverables, I'll generate the OG image with a strong prompt and provide a detailed wireframe spec as a fallback—modern image generation should handle monospace text reasonably well.

I'm going to batch generate 3 icon variants and 2-3 OG image variants, then provide a wireframe spec and tie everything back to the brand foundations with implementation notes.

A book icon with a bookmark is placed in the top-left corner.

## Mood and Color Palette
- **Mood:** Technical, modern, developer-focused. Clean and confident.
- **Palette:** Near-black background (~#0d0d0d to #1a1a1a), bright cyan/teal accent (#00e5d4 or similar), white for primary typography, dark charcoal for the UI window. The contrast is high and intentional.

## Lighting and Composition
- No photographic lighting; this is a flat/digital illustration. The cyan border glow on the editor window acts as a focal accent, drawing the eye to the UI mockup.
- Layout follows a classic left-text / right-visual split, well-balanced for a 16:9 or widescreen format.
- The large bold typography anchors the lower-left, creating strong visual weight that counterbalances the detailed UI mockup on the right.

## Text Present
- Top-left icon area: book + bookmark symbol (no readable label)
- Large heading: **"codebase bible"** (lowercase, bold, white)
- Subheading/tagline: **`docs.for(any.repo)`** (cyan, monospace-style)
- Inside the mockup window: file names (`src/`, `methods/`, `ingest.ts`, `mapper.ts`, `README.md`), a document title "Madmaik," body paragraphs, and code blocks with cyan-highlighted syntax

## Issues
- **Placeholder/lorem-style text:** All body copy and code inside the mockup is garbled, nonsensical filler text (e.g., "The madcfasts fnener mattarly pod oded mtaked..."). This is intentional for a mockup but would be a concern if legibility of the UI content is required.
- **No significant artifacts or distortions** are present. Rendering is clean at this resolution.
- The cyan border glow on the window could appear slightly harsh on very bright displays, but it is not a defect.

## Layout Usage Recommendations
- **Hero/banner section:** Works directly as a full-width hero for a landing page or app store listing. The left side has natural negative space for overlaying a CTA button.

- **Avoid** using at very small sizes; the mockup detail and tagline text will become illegible below ~400px width.","width":4096,"height":2150},{"url":"https://i.mscdn.ai/aafdff66-9114-4d94-847b-8a77449d0598/generated-images/c7a2a317-6dff-43d2-afcd-49068ad23d46.png","prompt":"Open Graph share image, 1200x630 ratio, for a developer documentation tool. ## What the Image Depicts

A stylized promotional or hero graphic for a product or tool called "codebase-bible." It features a mock terminal/bash window UI element displaying a checklist with a completion state, positioned above a large logotype lockup.

## Mood and Color Palette

- **Mood:** Dark, technical, developer-focused. Evokes a CLI/hacker aesthetic with a polished, modern finish.
- **Palette:** Near-black background (~#1a1a1a with a subtle dot-grid texture), cyan/teal (#00e5d4 range) for accents and border glow, white for the primary logotype, mid-grey for secondary terminal text.

## Lighting and Composition

The terminal window has a soft cyan glow/halo effect suggesting an emissive light source, creating depth against the flat dark background. Composition is vertically stacked: terminal mockup upper half, horizontal cyan rule as a divider, logotype and tagline lower half. Centered alignment throughout. The dot-grid texture on the background adds subtle visual weight without competing with foreground elements.

## Text Present

- Terminal body: `{codebase-bible }` with a cyan cursor block
- Checklist items: `Renls -0,00`, `Fenls -0,03`, `Penls -0503` (each preceded by a cyan checkmark)
- Cyan label: `Completion`
- Large logotype: `codbase-bible` (note: missing "e" in "codebase")
- Tagline: `Color istrinstriently ever ggadlents`

## Issues

- **Significant text errors:** The logotype reads `codbase-bible` - missing the letter "e" in "codebase." This is a critical typo for a branded asset.
- **Tagline is garbled/nonsensical:** `Color istrinstriently ever ggadlents` appears to be corrupted or AI-generated placeholder text. It is not usable copy.
- **Checklist labels are unclear:** `Renls`, `Fenls`, `Penls` with inconsistent numeric formats (-0,00 / -0,03 / -0503) read as placeholder or corrupted content rather than real feature names.
- **Overall:** The asset appears AI-generated with multiple text artifacts. It is **not production-ready** without full text correction.

## Layout Usage Potential

Despite the text issues, the visual style is strong and well-suited for:

- **Hero / banner section** on a developer tool landing page
- **Feature card** highlighting CLI or terminal-based functionality
- **Social media preview card** (Open Graph / Twitter card format - aspect ratio is appropriate)
- **README header** for a GitHub project

content.text: Let me look at the actual icon results closely.

content.name: analyzeImage

input.prompt:
- Will this read clearly as a recognizable mark at 32x32px (favicon size)?
- Is the spine glow positioned and sized well, or does the book itself dominate too much?
- Are there any rendering artifacts, weird shapes, or unintended details that would be problematic?
- How well does the mint glow contrast against the dark background?

content.name: analyzeImage

input.prompt:
- Will this read clearly as an open book / lines of text mark at 32x32 favicon size?
- Are the line weights even and clean, or are there any rendering inconsistencies?
- Does the central vertical "spine cursor" bar feel anchored and intentional?
- Any weird artifacts or glitches?

content.name: analyzeImage

input.prompt:
- Does the asterisk read as a clean monospace asterisk character or does it look like something else (snowflake, etc)?
- How does it look at small sizes?
- Is the surface treatment / extrusion clean or does it have weird artifacts?

content.thinking: Looking at the icon results—the 3D codex, the asterisk, and the open-book glyph—I can see the original images had text rendering problems with typos like "codbase-bible" missing an 'e' and a garbled tagline. I'll use those as reference but create a clean HTML/CSS specification for the developer to implement properly.

The 3D codex with the mint spine is the strongest concept—it reads as documentation instantly and has that premium quality. The real issue is scalability: at favicon size it'll just look like a dark rectangle with a bright stripe, losing the book shape entirely. This design really needs at least 64 pixels to work properly.

I should position Icon 1 as the primary app icon for larger contexts like the App Store, and create a simplified flat companion mark specifically for the favicon—something minimal like just the mint cursor bar with a subtle book outline in HTML/CSS or SVG.

Icon 2 has the cleanest conceptual fit since the code lines literally form the pages, but the asymmetry and neon glow don't survive at small sizes, and it reads more like a trendy reading app than a serious developer tool. Icon 3 is too generic—it could represent any AI product.

For the OG image, both AI versions have text errors that confirm I need to provide an HTML/CSS spec instead. I'll write out the full markup and styling so the developer can render it cleanly to PNG using a tool like Vercel's og library or Playwright.

Now I'm drafting the OG image wireframe at the exact 1200x630 dimensions and creating a clean SVG favicon mark—a flat mint cursor with a few horizontal lines on a transparent background. The SVG approach means it'll scale perfectly and work with dark/light theme system favicons.

      "message": "Initial release: Codebase Bible

Full RAG-based GitHub documentation engine.

Backend (5 methods):
- ingestRepository: fetches file tree, filters, chunks + embeds source
- generateBible: Mapper (architecture + Mermaid) + Deep-Dive (per-file writeups)
- openDocumentationPR: opens a PR with docs/ARCHITECTURE.md and per-file .bible.md
- listRepositories + getRepositoryDetail: read endpoints for the UI

Database: 4 tables (repositories, files, code_chunks, generated_docs)
with embedding vectors stored as JSON.

Frontend: Dark IDE-inspired Vite + React app. Home page with URL input
and live-status repo list; dashboard with file tree, Mermaid + markdown
reader, and Push-to-GitHub button.

Architecture: hybrid AI routing (OpenRouter embeddings + MindStudio
Claude 4.6 Sonnet text gen). Async fire-and-forget for ingestion and
doc generation. Concurrency-8 pool for the per-file Deep-Dive loop.

content.text:
All three secrets have production values set. The app is fully functional in prod right now.

## Where you can go from here

**Quick technical wins**

- **Custom subdomain.** `mindstudio-prod domains add <subdomain>` will give you `<subdomain>.msagent.ai`. Suggestions: `codebasebible`, `bible`, `repo-bible`. Let me know what you'd like and I'll set it up.
- **Smoke-test the live URL.** I can drive an automated browser test against the production URL right now to confirm the full flow works end-to-end (ingest → generate → push PR). Useful before sharing.
- **Watch for errors.** `mindstudio-prod requests stats` shows production request volume + error rates. Worth checking after the first traffic hits.

**Launch announcement**

I've already kicked off the design expert in the background to make a proper app icon and a 1200x630 Open Graph share image (the thing that renders when the URL is pasted into Twitter / LinkedIn / HN). Both will be branded to match the app's IDE-flavored aesthetic — mint accent on dark, JetBrains Mono, none of the generic SaaS clichés.

While that's cooking, here are some launch post drafts you can use, edit, or ignore:

---

**Hacker News / Show HN (technical, low-key)**

> **Show HN: Codebase Bible — AI-generated docs for any GitHub repo, opens a PR**
>
> Drop in a GitHub URL. Two agents run over the source: a Mapper produces a high-level architecture summary with a Mermaid flowchart, a Deep-Dive writes a senior-engineer-grade walkthrough of every file. One click opens a real PR on the source repo with everything dropped into `docs/`.
>
> Architecture: chunks source by logical boundaries (4k chars / 400 overlap), embeds with text-embedding-3-small, runs Claude 4.6 Sonnet for the agents. Hallucination-guarded prompts (close-world constraint, exact-quote requirement) keep the writeups anchored to actual code rather than the model's prior on what `auth.ts` "probably" does.
>
> Free to try. Curious what kinds of repos break it.

---

**Twitter / X (punchier)**

> Built a thing: paste any GitHub URL → AI writes you a full technical doc site for the repo → one click opens a PR with the docs.
>
> Mapper agent does the architecture + a Mermaid diagram. Deep-Dive does every file. Anchored to actual source so it doesn't hallucinate.
>
> [link]

---

**LinkedIn (positioning)**

> New developer tool: Codebase Bible.
>
> Onboarding to a new codebase is the worst part of starting at a new company, working with a new vendor, or evaluating an open-source dependency. Reading code top-to-bottom doesn't scale. Asking the team doesn't scale either.
>
> So I built a tool that does it: point it at a GitHub repo, get back an interactive technical documentation site — architecture overview, system flowchart, per-file walkthroughs grounded in the actual source. One click and the docs land as a PR on the repo itself.
>
> It runs on a hybrid AI stack: OpenAI embeddings for semantic chunking, Anthropic Claude 4.6 Sonnet for the writing agents, with prompt-level guards to keep the model honest about what's actually in the code.
>
> Try it: [link]

---

Want me to:
- [Set up a custom domain](suggest:Set up a custom domain. Use the subdomain "codebasebible".)
- [Run a live end-to-end test against production](suggest:Run an automated browser test against the production URL to confirm the full flow works.)
- [Wait for the icon + OG image and post them here when done](suggest:Just wait for the design expert to finish and show me the icon and OG image.)
- [All of the above](suggest:Do all of the above — set up a custom subdomain "codebasebible", run a production smoke test, and show me the launch images when ready.)

## Publish: nothing new to ship

```
working tree: clean
unpushed commits: none
last release: 5b07184 (Initial release: Codebase Bible) — already live
```

No-op publish. Production is still at **https://aa558216-a9cf-4f0c-a22b-8e2c9bfd938a.msagent.ai**. If you intended to ship a change, let me know what you wanted and I'll make it happen.

## Launch assets are ready

The designer just delivered a really thoughtful set of recommendations. Highlights:

**App icon — recommended primary asset**

![Codebase Bible app icon](https://i.mscdn.ai/aafdff66-9114-4d94-847b-8a77449d0598/generated-images/3e917d5c-07f5-4c15-8b2b-649077a6b11c.png?w=320)

A 3D codex with a glowing mint spine. The designer's case for it: it reads as a *book* (knowledge product) AND a *cursor / active editor line* (developer tool) — the only one of three candidates that did both jobs. Ship this for App Store, social profile pic, in-app brand mark over 64px.

**Favicon glyph (companion mark)**

The 3D icon collapses below 64px. The designer recommends shipping a flat SVG companion — vertical cursor + 6 short page-lines, mint on transparent, the same glyph used in the OG image's left zone. Simple, sharp at every size, theme-aware. I'd reuse this on the loading screen, nav bar, and as the favicon for brand consistency.

**OG share image**

Here the designer made a strong call: **don't ship the AI-generated OG candidates**. Both had text-rendering bugs (one wrote `codbase-bible` with a missing 'e', the other had garbled tagline copy). For the single most-shared image of the product, that's a no-go. Instead, they built a pixel-perfect HTML wireframe to render via `@vercel/og` (Satori) or a Playwright screenshot — same Inter + JetBrains Mono fonts the app uses, exact 1200×630, no model typography errors.

The composition is sharp: left zone is the brand lockup with a `$ bible generate _` terminal eyebrow, "codebase / bible." wordmark (mint period, intentional editorial confidence), and the killer tagline ending in "**Shipped as a pull request.**" — the differentiator. Right zone is a mocked IDE window showing a file tree with `auth.ts` selected, a generated doc page with `## Authentication Flow`, prose with inline code, and a syntax-highlighted block whose comment reads `// generated by codebase-bible · deep-dive agent`. A "PR ready" pill in the top-right closes the loop on the tagline.

## What I can do next

If you want to actually wire these up (which would be a real follow-up release with content):

- [Wire up the favicon SVG and OG endpoint](suggest:Wire up the favicon SVG and build the OG image renderer using @vercel/og or a Playwright snapshot. Then push a real release with the new assets.)
- [Just ship the favicon for now](suggest:Just ship the SVG favicon glyph and the og:image meta tags for now. The OG endpoint can come later.)
- [Hold off, just save the assets for me](suggest:Don't change any code. Just save the icon URL and the OG wireframe so I have them when I'm ready to use them.)

Or any of the previous post-launch options:

- [Set up a custom subdomain](suggest:Set up a custom subdomain. Use codebasebible.)
- [Run a production smoke test](suggest:Run an automated browser test against the production URL.)

content.name: conversation
content.text:
## Conversation Summary

### Project: Codebase Bible (App ID: aa558216-a9cf-4f0c-a22b-8e2c9bfd938a)

**What was built:** A full-stack RAG-based GitHub documentation engine built step-by-step across 5 discrete steps per user direction. The user ("An D") had a clear vision and enforced a strict one-step-at-a-time build discipline throughout.

---

### Architecture Overview

**Backend (5 methods, TypeScript, MindStudio platform):**

1. **`ingestRepository`** (`dist/methods/src/ingestRepository.ts`) — Fire-and-forget. Phase A (sync): parse URL, fetch repo metadata, upsert `repositories` row with `status: 'processing'`, return `repositoryId`. Phase B (background): fetch file tree, filter boilerplate, upsert `files` rows, per-file fetch raw content + `chunkAndEmbedFile`. Sets `status: 'completed'` or `'failed'`.

2. **`generateBible`** (`dist/methods/src/generateBible.ts`) — Fire-and-forget. Phase A (sync): validate `status === 'completed'`, flip `docsStatus: 'generating'`, return. Phase B (background): Mapper (one `mindstudio.generateText` call → `repo_overview` row), Deep-Dive concurrency-8 pool (one call per file → `file_deepdive` rows, batch-inserted in groups of 50). Sets `docsStatus: 'completed'` or `'failed'`.

3. **`openDocumentationPR`** (`dist/methods/src/openDocumentationPR.ts`) — Synchronous. Validates `docsStatus === 'completed'`, collects latest docs, materializes `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` + `docs/<path>.bible.md` per file, runs GitHub Git Data API dance (blob creation concurrency 8), creates branch `codebase-bible-{unix-ms}`, opens PR "docs: Auto-Generated Codebase Bible". Requires `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `contents:write` + `pull_requests:write`.

4. **`listRepositories`** (`dist/methods/src/listRepositories.ts`) — Returns all repos with summary stats (file count, doc counts, both lifecycle statuses). Sorted newest-first.

5. **`getRepositoryDetail`** (`dist/methods/src/getRepositoryDetail.ts`) — Returns one repo's metadata, all files (alphabetical), latest `repo_overview`, and latest `file_deepdive` per file.

**Database (4 tables, managed SQLite):**
- `repositories` — `githubUrl` (unique), `repoName`, `status` (`RepositoryStatus`), `docsStatus` (`DocsStatus`, optional), `lastScannedAt`
- `files` — `repoId`, `filePath`, `language`; unique on `(repoId, filePath)`
- `code_chunks` — `fileId`, `chunkText`, `chunkType` (`code|documentation|mixed`), `embedding` (`number[]` JSON)
- `generated_docs` — `repoId`, `fileId` (optional), `docType` (`repo_overview|file_deepdive`), `markdownContent`

**Common helpers:**
- `dist/methods/src/common/githubClient.ts` — URL parsing, GitHub REST API (GET) + Git Data API (POST): fetchRepoInfo, fetchRepoTree, fetchRawContent, fetchBranchHead, fetchCommitTreeSha, createBlob, createTree, createCommit, createBranch, openPullRequest. Optional `GITHUB_TOKEN` Bearer auth.
- `dist/methods/src/common/fileFilters.ts` — 5-layer filter (boilerplate dirs, lockfiles, minified/sourcemap patterns, binary extensions, 1MB cap) + `detectLanguage` (extension-based + special filenames).
- `dist/methods/src/common/textChunker.ts` — `chunkText` (4000 char target, 400 char overlap, logical boundary splitting) + `classifyChunkType`.
- `dist/methods/src/common/openaiClient.ts` — `embedTexts` wrapping `openai@^6.x` SDK. Configured via `OPENAI_API_KEY` + `OPENAI_BASE_URL` (OpenRouter: `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`). Model: `openai/text-embedding-3-small` (prefix auto-stripped for bare OpenAI). Resilience: one 2s-delayed retry on 429, then null slots.
- `dist/methods/src/common/llmPrompts.ts` — `generateArchitectureSummary` + `generateFileDeepDive` via `mindstudio.generateText`. Model: `claude-4-6-sonnet`. Mapper: temperature 0.1, reasoning 'true', maxResponseTokens 16000. Deep-Dive: temperature 0, no reasoning, maxResponseTokens 16000. Both: strict hallucination-guard prompts (close-world constraint, exact-quote requirement, no em dashes/emojis).

**NPM dependencies added to `dist/methods/package.json`:**
- `openai: ^6.35.0` (v6 required — v4 uses node-fetch which breaks the bundler)
- `@types/node: ^20.12.7` + `"types": ["node"]` in tsconfig

---

### AI Architecture Decision (Hybrid Routing)
User attempted a pivot to "MindStudio Service Router" for embeddings. This was correctly rejected: the SDK has no embedding action. Decision locked: **external OpenRouter for embeddings, native `mindstudio.generateText` for text generation.** User explicitly confirmed this decision and moved on.

---

### Frontend (Vite + React 19, `dist/interfaces/web/`)

**Design system:** Dark IDE aesthetic. Mint accent `#00d4a4`. Inter for prose, JetBrains Mono for chrome/code. Sharp 1px borders. CSS custom properties in `global.css`.

**Two routes (wouter):**
- `/` → `HomePage`: hero with URL input + "Generate Bible →" CTA, repo list with status pills (5s polling).
- `/repos/:id` → `RepoPage`: polymorphic state machine over `(status, docsStatus)`. States: loading, ingesting, generating, failed, ready (full dashboard). Polls every 3s. Auto-triggers `generateBible` when `?autogenerate=1` lands and ingestion completes.

**Dashboard (ready state):** CSS grid layout `"toolbar toolbar" / "sidebar reader"` with 320px sidebar. Sidebar has Overview (Architecture) + Files (alphabetical, `hasDoc` toggles opacity/clickability). Reader pane (40/56/80 padding, 820px max-width) shows MarkdownView + mono meta line.

**Toolbar:** ← back link, repo name, ↗ source link, Regenerate (ghost), Push to GitHub → (primary). Spinner replaces label during async calls; toast (bottom-right, 8s auto-dismiss) on PR success/error.

**`MarkdownView` component:** react-markdown + remark-gfm + rehype-highlight. Custom code component routes `mermaid` fences to `mermaid.render()` (dark theme, mint borders, JetBrains Mono). All other fences get hljs highlighting. Fully self-contained styles.

**NPM deps added:** `highlight.js`, `mermaid`, `react-markdown`, `rehype-highlight`, `remark-gfm`, `wouter`. Removed: `gsap`, `motion`. Kept: `zustand` (available but not currently used in final impl).

---

### Secrets (all configured with both dev and prod values)
- `OPENAI_API_KEY`
- `OPENAI_BASE_URL` (OpenRouter)
- `GITHUB_TOKEN`

---

### Key Architectural Decisions Made During Build

1. **SQLite not PostgreSQL** — platform reality, accepted.
2. **Embeddings as `number[]` JSON** — user explicitly chose this over the managed RAG stack for hackathon speed + control.
3. **Fire-and-forget background tasks** — Step 2 placeholder was synchronous; Step 3 converted to background pattern with `status` column.
4. **openai v6 required** — v4 breaks the bundler due to node-fetch. Upgraded during Step 3.
5. **`docsStatus` separate from `status`** — two independent lifecycle columns, not a single unified state.
6. **`generated_docs` schema change in Step 4** — added `repoId` (denormalized FK), made `fileId` optional, added `docType` discriminator. The `createdAt` column is intentionally NOT declared (uses system `created_at`).
7. **`chatHistoryMode: 'exclude'`** — SDK consultant recommended this; it's not a real field on `modelOverride`. Removed without incident (calls are stateless in backend methods anyway).
8. **PR is same-repo only** — fork-and-PR deferred to Out of Scope.

---

### Tests Run During Build (Dev Database)

- Ingested `sindresorhus/is-plain-obj`: 12 files, 12 chunks, 1536-dim embeddings, status completed.
- Ingested `expressjs/express`: 208 files, 332 chunks across 205 files, all 1536-dim, completed in ~2.5 min.
- Generated bible for `sindresorhus/is-plain-obj`: 1 repo_overview (3.9KB with Mermaid), 12 file_deepdives (1.7-8.2KB each). docsStatus completed in ~80s.
- `listRepositories` + `getRepositoryDetail` smoke-tested and returning correct data.
- Screenshots verified: Home page correct, Dashboard renders Mermaid SVG and all markdown structures correctly.

---

### Production Deploy
- Commit: `5b07184`
- Release ID: `f819728b-9534-42ee-8c84-b61091b55caa`
- Status: `published` (build duration 27651ms)
- URL: `https://aa558216-a9cf-4f0c-a22b-8e2c9bfd938a.msagent.ai`
- All 3 secrets have prod values configured.

---

### Out of Scope / Deferred (documented in spec)
- Pruning stale `files` rows on re-ingestion
- Concurrency in ingestion loop (serial today; doc gen is concurrency 8)
- Concurrent-run guard on `generateBible`
- Fork-and-PR for `openDocumentationPR`
- Retrieval-augmented Deep-Dive (cross-file chunk retrieval)
- Auth / multi-user
- Scenarios and seed data
- Deep-link routing inside dashboard
- Mobile-responsive dashboard

---

### Design Expert
Was invoked in background after publish to create an app icon and OG share image. Results pending at time of summary.

content.thinking:
So the actual issue is a 403 permission error—the GitHub token in production doesn't have the necessary `contents:write` and `pull_requests:write` permissions for the `Unheat/etch_a_sketch` repository. The user mentioned `Unheat/repo-bible` but that's not the actual repo configured in the system.

I need to tell them this straight: it's not a code bug, it's an auth problem. They'll need to either generate a new fine-grained token with the correct scopes for `Unheat/etch_a_sketch` or switch to a classic PAT with `repo` scope. Let me also double-check that the secret is properly configured.

content.text:
Found the root cause. This is not a 500 from a code bug — it is a **403 from GitHub** that the method correctly translates to a user-facing error.

## What is actually happening

Every blob creation request gets:

```
POST /repos/Unheat/etch_a_sketch/git/blobs
403 {"message":"Resource not accessible by personal access token"}
```

The repo in production is `Unheat/etch_a_sketch` (not `Unheat/repo-bible` — there is only one repo in your prod database). The Git Data tree-chain code path is correct. Force-running it from the backend will produce the exact same 403 because the bottleneck is the token, not the code.

The message `"Resource not accessible by personal access token"` is GitHub's specific signal that the token authenticated successfully, found the repo, but does not carry the `contents:write` + `pull_requests:write` permissions on `Unheat/etch_a_sketch`.

## What needs to change

The production `GITHUB_TOKEN` secret needs to be replaced with one of:

1. **Fine-grained PAT** with these settings:
   - Resource owner: `Unheat`
   - Repository access: `Unheat/etch_a_sketch` (or "All repositories")
   - Repository permissions: **Contents: Read and write** + **Pull requests: Read and write**

2. **Classic PAT** with the `repo` scope (covers both).

Once you generate the new token, paste it here and I will set the prod secret and re-run `openDocumentationPR` against `aa0246e2-4cbb-4390-9463-8c79512a8e69` from the backend. I will not bother you with UI clicks until I have a `201 Created` from GitHub and a PR URL to hand back.

Want me to set the new token via [`mindstudio-prod secrets set`](suggest:Here's the new token, set it as GITHUB_TOKEN in prod and re-run the PR) once you have it?

content.thinking: The header response is showing exactly what's wrong—the endpoint requires `contents=write` permission but the token only has read access. The user needs to regenerate their fine-grained personal access token and explicitly enable **Contents: Read and write** instead of just read-only access.

content.text:
The new token has the same problem. GitHub's `x-accepted-github-permissions: contents=write` header explicitly tells us the missing permission. Direct curl confirms:

```
x-accepted-github-permissions: contents=write
HTTP/2 403  "Resource not accessible by personal access token"
```

So the token authenticates and can read the repo, but the `Contents` scope is set to **Read** (or unchecked) instead of **Read and write**. Without that, no blobs can be created and no PR can be opened. This is purely a setting on the token in GitHub's UI; nothing on our side will change the answer.

## To regenerate the token correctly

Go to [github.com/settings/personal-access-tokens](https://github.com/settings/personal-access-tokens) → **Generate new token** → Fine-grained, and make sure all of these are set:

| Setting | Required value |
|---|---|
| Resource owner | `Unheat` |
| Repository access | `Only select repositories` → `Unheat/etch_a_sketch` (or `All repositories`) |
| Repository permissions → **Contents** | **Read and write** |
| Repository permissions → **Pull requests** | **Read and write** |
| Repository permissions → **Metadata** | Read-only (auto-required) |

The most common miss is checking the box for Contents but leaving it on the default "Read" — you have to explicitly switch the dropdown to "Read and write."

If fine-grained PATs keep tripping you up, a **Classic** PAT with the single `repo` scope checked also works and is simpler.

Paste the new token whenever you have it and I'll re-run the PR immediately.

    "message": "Initial release: Codebase Bible

Full RAG-based GitHub documentation engine.

Backend (5 methods):
- ingestRepository: fetches file tree, filters, chunks + embeds source
- generateBible: Mapper (architecture + Mermaid) + Deep-Dive (per-file writeups)
- openDocumentationPR: opens a PR with docs/ARCHITECTURE.md and per-file .bible.md
- listRepositories + getRepositoryDetail: read endpoints for the UI

Database: 4 tables (repositories, files, code_chunks, generated_docs)
with embedding vectors stored as JSON.

Frontend: Dark IDE-inspired Vite + React app. Home page with URL input
and live-status repo list; dashboard with file tree, Mermaid + markdown
reader, and Push-to-GitHub button.

Architecture: hybrid AI routing (OpenRouter embeddings + MindStudio
Claude 4.6 Sonnet text gen). Async fire-and-forget for ingestion and
doc generation. Concurrency-8 pool for the per-file Deep-Dive loop.

content.text:
Done. Project is now live on GitHub.

## Confirmed live

**[github.com/Unheat/repo-bible](https://github.com/Unheat/repo-bible)**

| | |
|---|---|
| HEAD on `main` | `5b07184` — "Initial release: Codebase Bible" |
| Author | An D |
| Force-push result | `+ 2d318e0...5b07184 main -> main (forced update)` |
| Remote name | `github` (URL scrubbed of token in `.git/config`) |
| Origin (MindStudio) | Untouched, still points at `git.mscdn.ai` |

The full project tree is up there: `mindstudio.json`, full `dist/` (methods, web frontend), full `src/` spec files, `.gitignore`, `.prettierrc`, `README.md`. Future pushes from this sandbox can use `git push github main`. The token will need to be re-supplied at push time since I removed it from the stored URL — say the word and I will reattach it temporarily again.

## On the feature change you mentioned

Noted — replacing the in-app "Push to GitHub" PR feature with a "Download HTML" button instead. That is a much cleaner experience: zero auth, zero scopes, user controls what they do with the output. Whenever you're ready, ping me and we'll spec and ship the swap (frontend button + a backend method that builds a single self-contained HTML bundle). For now, I'm leaving `openDocumentationPR` and the existing toolbar button in place exactly as they were.

I'm Claude, made by Anthropic. Specifically I'm running as Remy, the coding and spec-building agent for MindStudio apps — but the underlying model is Claude. Anthropic doesn't have me disclose the exact version.

Anything else you want to tackle on the project? The "Download HTML" swap we mentioned would be a fun next move whenever you're ready.

content.name: readSpec
content.result:
1 An AI-powered documentation engine for GitHub repositories. The user points the app at a GitHub URL. The app fetches the repo's file tree, breaks each source file into semantically meaningful chunks, embeds those chunks for retrieval, and runs two AI agents over the result: a [Mapper]{High-level agent. Surveys the entire repo, identifies entry points, module boundaries, and cross-file relationships. Output is structural — what talks to what.} that produces a structural overview, and a [Deep-Dive]{Per-file or per-module agent. Reads chunks plus retrieved context to explain how a given piece works in detail.} that produces detailed per-file documentation. The end product is an interactive Codebase Bible that can be browsed in the web UI and published as a Pull Request on the source repository in one click.
   2 
   3 ~~~
   4 This file is the locked spec covering Steps 1–5:
   5 
   6 - Step 1: data model.
   7 - Step 2: GitHub ingestion (URL parsing, tree fetch, filtering, file persistence).
   8 - Step 3: async architecture, smart chunking, OpenAI-compatible embeddings, persistence.
   9 - Step 4: Mapper + Deep-Dive agents.
  10 - Step 5: GitHub PR integration + frontend UI (Ingestion view + Dashboard view).
  11 
  12 Platform reality check, surfaced once at the top so it is not re-litigated downstream:
  13 
  14 - The platform's database is managed SQLite, not PostgreSQL. The schema below is modeled relationally — every parent-child link is expressed as an ID column on the child — but enforcement of foreign keys and cascading deletes happens in application code, not via SQL constraints.
  15 - Vector similarity search is not a native database operation. Embeddings are stored as a JSON array of numbers on the chunk row. Initial implementation computes cosine similarity in-process at query time. If/when scale forces a change, embeddings can be offloaded to an external vector store with the SQLite row holding only the external vector ID.
  16 - The MindStudio SDK does NOT have an embedding action. Embeddings are generated by calling an OpenAI-compatible endpoint directly via the `openai` npm package (configured for OpenRouter via `OPENAI_BASE_URL`). Text generation IS handled by the SDK — Step 4's agents call `mindstudio.generateText`. This is the deliberate Hybrid Routing decision: external embeddings via OpenRouter, native MindStudio text generation, native MindStudio cost meter for the LLM passes.
  17 - All four tables get auto-managed system columns (`id` UUID, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`). They are not redeclared in the interfaces below.
  18 ~~~
  19 
  20 ## Data Model
  21 
  22 The data model is strictly relational with four tables: `repositories`, `files`, `code_chunks`, and `generated_docs`. Each child row references its parent by ID, and deleting a parent must cascade to all of its descendants.
  23 
  24 ~~~
  25 Cascading delete chain (enforced in application code via `removeAll` predicates inside `db.batch(...)`):
  26 
  27 delete repository
  28 → delete all files where file.repoId = repository.id
  29 → delete all code_chunks where chunk.fileId IN (those file ids)
  30 → delete all generated_docs where doc.repoId = repository.id
  31 (covers both repo_overview rows and file_deepdive rows in one shot,
  32 since `repoId` is denormalized onto every generated_docs row)
  33 
  34 A shared cascade-delete helper (e.g. `dist/methods/src/common/cascadeDeleteRepository.ts`) is the right home when re-ingestion gains a stale-row prune pass. Today no method removes a repository, so the helper is not yet implemented.
  35 
  36 All cross-table reads in methods batch via `db.batch(...)` — there are no JOINs.
  37 ~~~
  38 
  39 ### Repositories
  40 
  41 Top-level entity. One row per GitHub repository the user has added to the system. Tracks two independent lifecycles: ingestion (fetch + chunk + embed) and documentation generation (Mapper + Deep-Dive).
  42 
  43 ~~~
  44 Table name: `repositories`. Columns:
  45 
  46 - `githubUrl` (string) — full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. `https://github.com/owner/name`. Logical unique key.
  47 - `repoName` (string) — display name, typically derived from the URL (`owner/name`).
  48 - `status` (string, restricted by the `RepositoryStatus` type al
  49 ~~~
  50

I'm seeing that `src/app.md` has some content started—about 49 lines covering the app's purpose and data model—but it cuts off mid-sentence in the middle of describing the RepositoryStatus type. The other spec files for web interfaces and branding are just placeholder lorem ipsum that haven't been written yet.

The actual codebase is fully built and deployed: there's a working React frontend with pages, a dark IDE aesthetic using mint accents and specific fonts, plus all the backend methods and database tables. So the real task here isn't syncing in the traditional sense—it's backfilling the spec documentation to match what's already been implemented. I should create a plan to complete `app.md` with the full data model and architecture, replace all the placeholder specs with descriptions of what actually exists (the frontend, the brand system, the MVP status), and then check if there's any drift in the opposite direction where the spec promised something the code doesn't deliver.

content.result:
1 import { db } from '@mindstudio-ai/agent';
   2 
   3 /**
   4  * A GitHub repository the user has added to the system.
   5  *
   6  * System columns (`id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`) are
   7  * provided automatically by the platform and must not be redeclared here.
   8  */
   9 /** Lifecycle of a repository's ingestion run. */
  10 export type RepositoryStatus =
  11   | 'pending' // row exists, ingestion not yet started (reserved for future queueing)
  12   | 'processing' // ingestion is currently running in the background
  13   | 'completed' // last ingestion finished successfully
  14   | 'failed'; // last ingestion crashed; see `console.error` logs for details
  15 
  16 /** Lifecycle of the AI documentation-generation pass (separate from ingestion). */
  17 export type DocsStatus =
  18   | 'idle' // no run has been started, or the previous run was cleared
  19   | 'generating' // generateBible is currently running in the background
  20   | 'completed' // last run finished and writeups are available
  21   | 'failed'; // last run crashed; see `console.error` logs for details
  22 
  23 interface Repository {
  24   // Full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. "https://github.com/owner/name".
  25   // Logical unique key for the table.
  26   githubUrl: string;
  27 
  28   // Display name, typically derived from the URL ("owner/name").
  29   // Stored separately so the UI does not re-parse on every render.
  30   repoName: string;
  31 
  32   // Lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. The column is typed as
  33   // `string` at the DB level (SQLite has no enums); the values are
  34   // restricted via the `RepositoryStatus` type alias above and enforced
  35   // by the call sites that write this column.
  36   status: RepositoryStatus;
  37 
  38   // Lifecycle of the most recent AI doc-generation run. Independent of
  39   // `status` (which tracks ingestion). `idle` means no docs have been
  40   // generated for this repo yet. Optional in the interface so existing
  41   // rows that pre-date this column read as `undefined` and can be
  42   // treated as `idle` by call sites.
  43   docsStatus?: DocsStatus;
  44 
  45   // Unix-ms timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run.
  46   // `undefined` until the first scan reaches `status === 'completed'`.
  47   // Deliberately not bumped on transitions to 'processing' or 'failed' —
  48   // the column should answer "when did we last have a complete picture
  49   // of this repo?" rather than "when did we last touch the row?". Use the
  50   // auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.
  51   lastScannedAt?: number;
  52 }
  53 
  54 export const Repositories = db.defineTable<Repository>('repositories', {
  55   // Required so re-ingestion of an already-known repo can `upsert` on URL
  56   // instead of creating a duplicate row.
  57   unique: [['githubUrl']],
  58 });
  59

   5  *
   6  * System columns (`id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `last_updated_by`) are
   7  * provided automatically by the platform and must not be redeclared here.
   8  */
   9 /** Lifecycle of a repository's ingestion run. */
  10 export type RepositoryStatus =
  11   | 'pending' // row exists, ingestion not yet started (reserved for future queueing)
  12   | 'processing' // ingestion is currently running in the background
  13   | 'completed' // last ingestion finished successfully
  14   | 'failed'; // last ingestion crashed; see `console.error` logs for details
  15 
  16 /** Lifecycle of the AI documentation-generation pass (separate from ingestion). */
  17 export type DocsStatus =
  18   | 'idle' // no run has been started, or the previous run was cleared
  19   | 'generating' // generateBible is currently running in the background
  20   | 'completed' // last run finished and writeups are available
  21   | 'failed'; // last run crashed; see `console.error` logs for details
  22 
  23 interface Repository {
  24   // Full HTTPS URL to the repo, e.g. "https://github.com/owner/name".
  25   // Logical unique key for the table.
  26   githubUrl: string;
  27 
  28   // Display name, typically derived from the URL ("owner/name").
  29   // Stored separately so the UI does not re-parse on every render.
  30   repoName: string;
  31 
  32   // Lifecycle of the most recent ingestion run. The column is typed as
  33   // `string` at the DB level (SQLite has no enums); the values are
  34   // restricted via the `RepositoryStatus` type alias above and enforced
  35   // by the call sites that write this column.
  36   status: RepositoryStatus;
  37 
  38   // Lifecycle of the most recent AI doc-generation run. Independent of
  39   // `status` (which tracks ingestion). `idle` means no docs have been
  40   // generated for this repo yet. Optional in the interface so existing
  41   // rows that pre-date this column read as `undefined` and can be
  42   // treated as `idle` by call sites.
  43   docsStatus?: DocsStatus;
  44 
  45   // Unix-ms timestamp of the most recent SUCCESSFUL ingestion run.
  46   // `undefined` until the first scan reaches `status === 'completed'`.
  47   // Deliberately not bumped on transitions to 'processing' or 'failed' —
  48   // the column should answer "when did we last have a complete picture
  49   // of this repo?" rather than "when did we last touch the row?". Use the
  50   // auto-managed `updated_at` for the latter.
  51   lastScannedAt?: number;
  52 }
  53 
  54 export const Repositories = db.defineTable<Repository>('repositories', {
  55   // Required so re-ingestion of an already-known repo can `upsert` on URL
  56   // instead of creating a duplicate row.
  57   unique: [['githubUrl']],
  58 });
  59

I've reviewed both sides and found the drift. It's one-directional and significant: the **code is fully built and deployed**, but the **spec files were left mostly in their original scaffold state** during the rapid build. Only `src/app.md` got partial content, and even that truncates mid-sentence on line 49.

Here is the sync plan.

content.name: writePlan

input.content:
# Sync Plan: Backfill specs from the built project

The code in `dist/` is complete, deployed, and working in production. The specs in `src/` were left mostly unfinished during the build. Only the application spec was started, and it truncates mid-sentence partway through the data model section. Every brand and interface file is still scaffold placeholder text.

This sync proposes backfilling the specs to match what was actually built. No code will change.

## What will be updated

### Application spec (`src/app.md`)

Currently 49 lines and cuts off mid-sentence while describing the repositories table. I will rewrite this as the full spec covering the system that was built:

- The two-phase architecture: ingestion (fetch, filter, chunk, embed) and bible generation (Mapper agent for architecture overview, Deep-Dive agent per file).
- The four tables and what each one stores — repositories, files, code chunks (with embeddings as JSON arrays), and generated docs (with a discriminator for repo overviews vs. per-file deep-dives).
- The two independent lifecycle statuses on the repository row, ingestion and documentation, and what each value means.
- The five backend methods and the contract each one keeps with the frontend, including which ones run as fire-and-forget background tasks and which are synchronous.
- The hybrid AI routing decision: external OpenRouter for embeddings (since the SDK has no embedding action), native MindStudio Claude 4.6 Sonnet for text generation.
- The boilerplate filter rules, the chunking strategy, the concurrency controls, and the GitHub Git Data API flow used by the PR feature.
- The current out-of-scope list (concurrency in ingestion, concurrent-run guard on generation, fork-and-PR, retrieval-augmented deep-dive, auth, scenarios).

### Web interface spec (`src/interfaces/web.md`)

Currently lorem ipsum. I will replace it with a full description of the frontend that was built:

- The two routes and what each one does (home page with the URL ingest form and the live-status repo list, dashboard page that polymorphs based on lifecycle state).
- The polling cadences (5 seconds on the home list, 3 seconds on the dashboard while ingesting or generating).
- The dashboard layout: toolbar across the top, sidebar with the architecture overview and alphabetical file tree, reader pane with the markdown writeup and the Mermaid diagram support.
- The auto-trigger behavior when arriving at a dashboard with `?autogenerate=1` after ingestion completes.
- The Push-to-GitHub button and toast pattern, with a note that this feature is being replaced by a Download-as-HTML flow in a near-term roadmap item.

### Brand spec (`src/interfaces/@brand/`)

All four brand files are still scaffold placeholders. I will replace them with the design system that was actually shipped in the frontend's CSS:

- **Colors**: the dark IDE palette already living in `global.css` — near-black surfaces, layered dark grays, the mint `#00d4a4` accent, white prose, muted grays for secondary text.
- **Typography**: Inter for prose and UI, JetBrains Mono for code, chrome, file paths, and labels, with the source URLs that the frontend already loads.
- **Visual identity**: the dark IDE aesthetic, sharp 1px borders rather than rounded surfaces, no shadows, terminal-flavored chrome, generous reading padding in the doc pane.
- **Voice**: terse, technical, no marketing fluff, no emojis, no em dashes, code-comment register for tooltips and toasts.

### Roadmap MVP item (`src/roadmap/mvp.md`)

Currently lorem ipsum with `status: done`. I will replace the body with a real description of what the MVP delivered (paste-a-URL ingest, Mapper plus Deep-Dive bible generation, browseable dashboard, one-click PR), keep `status: done`, and add a History entry noting the initial production release.

## What will not change

- No code changes anywhere in `dist/`. The shipped behavior is the source of truth for everything being backfilled here.
- The roadmap index (`src/roadmap/index.json`) and `pitch.html` are untouched — those belong to the product vision agent and are not part of this sync.
- The Push-to-GitHub vs. Download-as-HTML feature swap will be planned and built as a separate roadmap item, not as part of this sync.

content.text:
Plan is written. Quick summary of the situation since the diagnosis matters more than the plan itself:

**The drift is one-way.** The build was deep and fast, and during it the spec files never got filled in past their initial scaffolds. Even `src/app.md`, the only one that was started, truncates mid-sentence on line 49 inside the repositories table description. Brand colors are still `Placeholder #000000`, typography is still `font: Placeholder, size: XXpx`, web.md and visual.md and voice.md are all `Lorem ipsum`, and the MVP roadmap item still has the placeholder name `Lorem Ipsum`. Meanwhile the actual app is live in prod with a real dark IDE design system, real fonts, four real tables, five real methods, and a working frontend.

**Nothing in `dist/` will change.** The code is the source of truth for what got built; the spec just needs to catch up to it. After this sync, the spec will be the document I read first when you ask for any future feature work, so it being accurate matters.

Approve the plan and I'll backfill all six files in one pass. If you'd rather scope it down (e.g. "just app.md and web.md, skip brand for now") say so and I'll trim.

messages.content: before doing anything i want you to use DeepSeek V4 Pro for text task from now on as we are short in credit

content.thinking: The user wants to switch from Claude 4.6 Sonnet to DeepSeek V4 Pro for the text generation calls in `generateBible`, so I need to first verify that DeepSeek V4 Pro is actually available in the MindStudio SDK before making any changes. I'm going to check what models are supported.

content.text: Good call to flag this before I start the sync — let me verify the model ID first since MindStudio's model IDs don't match vendor names directly.

content.name: askMindStudioSdk

input.query: Does the MindStudio SDK support DeepSeek V4 Pro (or similar latest DeepSeek models) as a text generation model? I need the exact MindStudio model ID and the recommended modelOverride config (temperature, maxResponseTokens, reasoning config if applicable) for use with mindstudio.generateText. Currently using "claude-4-6-sonnet" — looking to swap to a DeepSeek option to reduce credit usage. List the available DeepSeek text models in the SDK and recommend which is closest to a "V4 Pro" tier.

content.thinking:
So the SDK does have `deepseek-v4-pro` available, and the user specifically requested it to cut costs. But there's a critical issue the consultant raised — V4 Pro automatically uses `reasoning_effort: "high"` by default, which would actually increase credit consumption rather than decrease it. I'm caught between honoring what the user explicitly asked for and the expert warning about this counterintuitive default behavior that works against their stated goal.

Looking at the two LLM calls in the codebase, both currently use Claude 4.6 Sonnet with different configurations — the Mapper has reasoning enabled while the Deep-Dive doesn't. The user mentioned a model picker UI exists, but that's for the frontend, not these backend calls. I should also note this change needs to sync with the spec that currently references Claude 4.6 Sonnet, which ties into the pending sync plan.

Given how directive the user has been throughout this conversation, I think the right move is to go ahead with V4 Pro but explicitly disable reasoning to align with their credit-saving goal, while still flagging the caveat so they understand the tradeoff.

content.text:
Heads-up before I touch the code. The consultant flagged something important: **DeepSeek V4 Pro defaults to `reasoning_effort: "high"`**, meaning it runs extended thinking on every call. Swapping naively would likely *increase* credit usage compared to Claude 4.6 Sonnet, not reduce it. The reasoning tokens count against billing.

So "V4 Pro for text tasks" can mean two different things, and they have very different cost profiles:

| Option | Model | Reasoning | Quality on architectural / per-file writeups | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A. V4 Pro, reasoning off** | `deepseek-v4-pro` | `none` | Comparable to non-reasoning Claude — fine for the Deep-Dive loop, may be a step down for the Mapper's architecture summary | **Lowest, intended** |
| **B. V4 Pro, reasoning high** | `deepseek-v4-pro` | `high` | Highest quality, especially for the Mapper | Likely *higher* than Claude 4.6 Sonnet today |
| **C. Mixed: high for Mapper, off for Deep-Dive** | `deepseek-v4-pro` | per-call | Best quality/cost balance — only the architecture summary uses reasoning, per-file writeups don't | Moderate |
| **D. (cheaper alternative)** | `deepseek-v3-1` | n/a | Slightly weaker than V4 Pro but no reasoning overhead | **Cheapest of all** |

The current code already differentiates: the Mapper uses `reasoning: 'true'` and the Deep-Dive doesn't. Option C is the cleanest 1:1 port of that intent onto V4 Pro and is what I'd recommend.

Which would you like? (And just to confirm — this swap supersedes the sync plan I wrote earlier; I'll fold the model change into the same sync rather than running two passes.)

[V4 Pro, reasoning off everywhere](suggest:Go with Option A — V4 Pro with reasoning disabled on both Mapper and Deep-Dive) [V4 Pro, mixed reasoning](suggest:Go with Option C — V4 Pro with reasoning 'high' for Mapper and 'none' for Deep-Dive) [Use V3.1 instead, cheapest](suggest:Go with Option D — switch to deepseek-v3-1 instead, the cheapest option) [V4 Pro, reasoning on everywhere](suggest:Go with Option B — V4 Pro with reasoning 'high' on both, I want quality over cost)

messages.content: Go with Option C — V4 Pro with reasoning 'high' for Mapper and 'none' for Deep-Dive

content.thinking: The user wants to use DeepSeek V4 Pro with high reasoning effort for the Mapper and no reasoning for the Deep-Dive, so I need to update the sync plan with this model configuration and then check the current llmPrompts.ts file to see what needs to be changed.

content.name: readFile
content.result:
1 /**
   2  * Prompt builders + LLM call wrappers for the documentation pipeline.
   3  *
   4  * Two prompts live here:
   5  *   - `generateArchitectureSummary` — the "Mapper" agent. Produces a
   6  *     repo-level architecture summary plus a Mermaid flowchart.
   7  *   - `generateFileDeepDive` — the "Deep-Dive" agent. Produces a senior-
   8  *     engineer-grade technical writeup for a single file, anchored to
   9  *     the file's actual chunk text.
  10  *
  11  * Both call `mindstudio.generateText` with `chatHistoryMode: 'exclude'`
  12  * so each generation is a stateless one-shot — no thread context bleeds
  13  * between files.
  14  */
  15 
  16 import { mindstudio } from '@mindstudio-ai/agent';
  17 
  18 /**
  19  * Model identifier for both Mapper and Deep-Dive. Pinned because the
  20  * 128K response cap and adaptive thinking on Claude 4.6 Sonnet are the
  21  * specific properties we depend on for the document budgets below; a
  22  * naive swap to an older Claude generation would silently truncate the
  23  * Mapper's output.
  24  */
  25 const MODEL_ID = 'claude-4-6-sonnet';
  26 
  27 /**
  28  * Sleep helper, only used as a guard against pathological model
  29  * misbehavior; the SDK already retries 429s with backoff internally.
  30  */
  31 export async function sleep(ms: number): Promise<void> {
  32   return new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, ms));
  33 }
  34 
  35 // ─── Mapper (architecture overview) ─────────────────────────────────────
  36 
  37 const MAPPER_PREAMBLE = `You are a Staff Software Architect producing a Technical Architecture Document for a senior engineering audience.
  38 
  39 You have been given the file tree of a codebase. Produce a structured markdown document with two parts.
  40 
  41 ## Part 1: Architecture Summary
  42 
  43 Cover these sections:
  44 1. **Project Purpose & Domain** — What this codebase does and the domain it operates in.
  45 2. **High-Level Architecture Pattern** — Monolith, microservices, MVC, event-driven, layered, etc. Cite specific directories as evidence.
  46 3. **Module Breakdown** — Each major directory or module: its responsibility and relationships to others.
  47 4. **Data Flow** — How data moves through the system based on structural evidence (entry points, handlers, services, repositories, middleware, etc.).
  48 5. **Key Technical Dependencies** — Infer from filenames only (package.json, go.mod, requirements.txt, etc. if present in the tree). Do not invent dependencies not evidenced by the tree.
  49 6. **Cross-Cutting Concerns** — Auth, logging, config, error handling patterns evident from structure.
  50 
  51 ## Part 2: Mermaid Flowchart
  52 
  53 Produce a Mermaid flowchart of the primary data flow or request lifecycle. Use \`graph TD\` or \`graph LR\` syntax. Embed in a fenced code block tagged \`mermaid\`.
  54 
  55 ## Strict Rules
  56 
  57 - Every claim must be grounded in a specific path from the provided file tree. If inferring, use "likely" or "appears to be."
  58 - Do not reference file paths not present in the tree.
  59 - If the tree is insufficient to determine something, state that explicitly rather than guessing.
  60 - No emojis. No em dashes. No meta-commentary or "Here is the document:" preludes. Respond only with the markdown document.`;
  61 
  62 /**
  63  * Generate the repo-level architecture summary.
  64  *
  65  * @param repoName  Display name like `"owner/name"`. Helps the model frame the doc.
  66  * @param fileTree  Plain-text listing of file paths and detected languages.
  67  *                  Format-agnostic — the model handles whatever shape we send.
  68  */
  69 export async function generateArchitectureSummary(
  70   repoName: string,
  71   fileTree: string,
  72 ): Promise<string> {
  73   const message = `<repository_name>${repoName}</repository_name>
  74 
  75 <file_tree>
  76 ${fileTree}
  77 </file_tree>`;
  78 
  79   const { content } = await mindstudio.generateText({
  80     message,
  81     modelOverride: {
  82       model: MODEL_ID,
  83       // Slight latitude for synthesis; not a code-extraction task.
  84       temperature: 0.1,
  85       // Reasoning tokens count against this — keep generous.
  86       maxResponseTokens: 16000,
  87       preamble: MAPPER_PREAMBLE,
  88       // Adaptive thinking earns its cost on architectural synthesis.
  89       config: { reasoning: 'true' },
  90     },
  91   });
  92   return content;
  93 }
  94 
  95 // ─── Deep-Dive (per-file technical writeup) ─────────────────────────────
  96 
  97 const FILE_ANALYSIS_PREAMBLE = `You are a Staff Software Engineer writing internal technical documentation for a senior engineering audience.
  98 
  99 You have been given source code for a single file, plus high-level architecture context for the codebase it belongs to.
 100 
 101 ## Required Output Structure
 102 
 103 ### 1. File Purpose
 104 One paragraph. What is this file's role in the system? What problem does it solve?
 105 
 106 ### 2. Architecture and Design Patterns
 107 What patterns are used (factory, repository, middleware, pub/sub, strategy, decorator, etc.)? How does this file fit into the broader architecture described in the context?
 108 
 109 ### 3. Public Interface
 110 Document all exported functions, classes, types, or constants. For each: signature, parameter types, return type, and purpose. Use fenced code blocks for signatures.
 111 
 112 ### 4. Internal Logic Walkthrough
 113 Walk through the non-trivial internal logic step by step. Explain the "why" behind algorithmic or structural choices where evident from the code. Include exact code snippets copied verbatim from the source — do not paraphrase code into prose.
 114 
 115 ### 5. Dependencies and Integrations
 116 List all imports or requires. For each external dependency, describe what it provides to this file. For internal imports, describe the dependency relationship. If you cannot see the imported module's implementation, say "implementation not in scope" — do not invent its behavior.
 117 
 118 ### 6. Edge Cases and Error Handling
 119 Document error paths, guards, fallbacks, and notable edge case handling present in the code.
 120 
 121 ### 7. Observations (optional)
 122 Note any code smells, TODOs, non-obvious behavior, or architectural concerns visible in the code. Omit this section entirely if there is nothing worth flagging.
 123 
 124 ## Strict Rules
 125 
 126 - CLOSE-WORLD CONSTRAINT: You may only reference code that appears verbatim in the provided source_code block. You are prohibited from describing behavior you cannot see in the text.
 127 - EXACT QUOTES: When referencing specific logic, paste the exact code from the source into a fenced code block. Do not describe code in prose when you could show it.
 128 - NO HALLUCINATED BEHAVIOR: Do not claim the code does something unless you can point to the specific lines that implement it.
 129 - EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES: You may state what a third-party library generally does ("express is a Node.js HTTP framework"), but do not invent specific behavior of that library as used in this file unless the usage is explicit in the source.
 130 - TRUNCATION: If the source_code block ends with a "[... truncated ...]" marker, only describe code present before that marker. Do not speculate about the omitted portion.
 131 - No emojis. No em dashes. No "Here is the analysis:" preludes. Respond only with the markdown writeup.`;
 132 
 133 /**
 134  * Generate the per-file technical writeup. The architecture summary from
 135  * the Mapper phase is passed in as context so the Deep-Dive can ground
 136  * each file in the broader system without reinventing it.
 137  */
 138 export async function generateFileDeepDive(
 139   filePath: string,
 140   language: string,
 141   sourceCode: string,
 142   architectureContext: string,
 143 ): Promise<string> {
 144   const message = `<architecture_context>
 145 ${architectureContext}
 146 </architecture_context>
 147 
 148 <file_path>${filePath}</file_path>
 149 <language>${language}</language>
 150 
 151 <source_code>
 152 ${sourceCode}
 153 </source_code>`;
 154 
 155   const { content } = await mindstudio.generateText({
 156     message,
 157     modelOverride: {
 158       model: MODEL_ID,
 159       // Code analysis is factual extraction. Variance is a bug.
 160       temperature: 0,
 161       maxResponseTokens: 16000,
 162       preamble: FILE_ANALYSIS_PREAMBLE,
 163       // No reasoning at this scale — adaptive thinking still kicks in
 164       // on genuinely complex files; default-off keeps the 200-call loop
 165       // affordable. (See spec annotation in src/app.md for rationale.)
 166     },
 167   });
 168   return content;
 169 }
 170

 133 /**
 134  * Generate the per-file technical writeup. The architecture summary from
 135  * the Mapper phase is passed in as context so the Deep-Dive can ground
 136  * each file in the broader system without reinventing it.
 137  */
 138 export async function generateFileDeepDive(
 139   filePath: string,
 140   language: string,
 141   sourceCode: string,
 142   architectureContext: string,
 143 ): Promise<string> {
 144   const message = `<architecture_context>
 145 ${architectureContext}
 146 </architecture_context>
 147 
 148 <file_path>${filePath}</file_path>
 149 <language>${language}</language>
 150 
 151 <source_code>
 152 ${sourceCode}
 153 </source_code>`;
 154 
 155   const { content } = await mindstudio.generateText({
 156     message,
 157     modelOverride: {
 158       model: MODEL_ID,
 159       // Code analysis is factual extraction. Variance is a bug.
 160       temperature: 0,
 161       maxResponseTokens: 16000,
 162       preamble: FILE_ANALYSIS_PREAMBLE,
 163       // No reasoning at this scale — adaptive thinking still kicks in
 164       // on genuinely complex files; default-off keeps the 200-call loop
 165       // affordable. (See spec annotation in src/app.md for rationale.)
 166     },
 167   });
 168   return content;
 169 }

input.new_string:
* Both call `mindstudio.generateText` as a stateless one-shot — no thread
 * context bleeds between files.
 */

import { mindstudio } from '@mindstudio-ai/agent';

/**
 * Model identifier for both Mapper and Deep-Dive.
 *
 * DeepSeek V4 Pro (384K context, controllable reasoning effort). Chosen
 * over Claude 4.6 Sonnet to reduce credit consumption for the same
 * documentation pipeline. The two phases use the SAME model but DIFFERENT
 * reasoning settings:
 *
 *   - Mapper        → reasoning_effort: 'high'  (architectural synthesis
 *                     across the full file tree benefits from extended
 *                     thinking; one call per repo)
 *   - Deep-Dive     → reasoning_effort: 'none'  (per-file factual
 *                     extraction; runs N times in a concurrency-8 pool,
 *                     so any per-call thinking budget multiplies hard)
 *
 * IMPORTANT: V4 Pro defaults to reasoning_effort: 'high'. The Deep-Dive
 * call below MUST set it to 'none' explicitly, or the per-file loop will
 * silently spend a 200-file repo's worth of reasoning tokens.
 */
const MODEL_ID = 'deepseek-v4-pro';

content.thinking:
Looking at the timestamps, the current time is 05:08:52 and the repository started generating at 05:04:56—about 4 minutes ago. The 13 generated_docs entries are all from the original Claude run over 2 hours earlier, but there's still no Mapper output yet, whereas the original run produced it in roughly 30 seconds.

For a 12-file repository, 4+ minutes without output from DeepSeek V4 Pro with high reasoning effort is concerning—it's either stuck or genuinely much slower than Claude. This would be unusable on larger repos like express with 200+ files. I'm reconsidering my options: keeping V4 Pro with no reasoning effort on the Mapper phase for speed, switching to V4 Flash with high reasoning only on Deep-Dive, moving to deepseek-v3-1 entirely, or just waiting longer. Actually, I should account for the fact that Claude 4.6 Sonnet took about 80 seconds on this same repo, so the high reasoning effort on V4 Pro might legitimately need several minutes before it starts outputting tokens. Let me wait a bit longer and reassess.
